title: Short title, extent and commencement
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: This Act may be called the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. It extends to the whole of India except the State of Jammu and Kashmir and came into force on the 1st day of April, 1974.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the name of the law, specifies where it applies, and states when it came into effect.
examples: A criminal case registered anywhere in India is generally governed by the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of this section?
faqs.answer: It identifies the law, its territorial application, and the date on which it became operational.

title: Definitions
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: In this Code, unless the context otherwise requires, various terms such as bailable offence, cognizable offence, complaint, inquiry, investigation, offence, police report, summons-case, warrant-case and victim shall have the meanings assigned to them in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the meaning of important legal terms used throughout the Code so that everyone interprets them consistently.
examples: An offence for which police can arrest without a warrant is called a cognizable offence.
A complaint made to a Magistrate is interpreted according to the definition provided in this section.

faqs.question: Why are definitions important?
faqs.answer: They ensure that courts, police, lawyers and citizens understand legal terms in the same way.

title: Construction of references
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: References in this Code to Magistrates, Courts and other authorities shall be construed in the manner specified in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how references to courts, magistrates and judicial officers should be interpreted when applying the Code.
examples: Where the Code refers to a Magistrate, the appropriate class of Magistrate is determined according to this section.

faqs.question: Does this section create any offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only helps interpret references used throughout the Code.

title: Trial of offences under the Indian Penal Code and other laws
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: All offences under the Indian Penal Code shall be investigated, inquired into, tried and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions of this Code. Offences under any other law shall also be dealt with according to this Code, subject to any special procedure prescribed by that law.
simpleExplanation: This section states that criminal offences are generally investigated and tried according to CrPC procedures unless a special law provides a different procedure.
examples: A theft case under the Indian Penal Code is investigated and tried according to CrPC procedures.
An offence under a special law may follow a different procedure if that law specifically provides one.

faqs.question: Does CrPC apply to offences under special laws?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the special law provides a different procedure.

title: Saving
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: Nothing contained in this Code shall, in the absence of a specific provision to the contrary, affect any special or local law or any special jurisdiction or power conferred, or any special form of procedure prescribed, by any other law for the time being in force.
simpleExplanation: This section protects special laws and procedures. If another law provides a special power or procedure, that law will continue to apply unless CrPC specifically overrides it.
examples: A special anti-corruption law may prescribe its own procedure, which will continue to apply.
Special courts created under another law can continue exercising their powers.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of the saving clause?
faqs.answer: It ensures that existing special laws and procedures are not automatically overridden by the CrPC.

title: Classes of Criminal Courts
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Besides the High Courts and the Courts constituted under any law other than this Code, there shall be, in every State, the following classes of Criminal Courts, namely: Courts of Session; Judicial Magistrates of the first class and, in any metropolitan area, Metropolitan Magistrates; Judicial Magistrates of the second class; and Executive Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section lists the different types of criminal courts that operate in every State under the Code.
examples: A murder case is generally tried before a Court of Session.
Minor criminal cases may be heard by a Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: Does this section create any offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only defines the classes of criminal courts.

title: Territorial divisions
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Every State shall be a sessions division or shall consist of sessions divisions; every sessions division shall, for the purposes of this Code, be a district or consist of districts; and every metropolitan area shall be divided into metropolitan divisions.
simpleExplanation: This section divides States into sessions divisions, districts and metropolitan areas for the administration of criminal justice.
examples: A State may have several districts, each falling within a sessions division.
Metropolitan cities are divided into metropolitan divisions for court administration.

faqs.question: Why are territorial divisions important?
faqs.answer: They determine which courts have authority over criminal cases in a particular area.

title: Metropolitan areas
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may declare any area having a population of one million or more to be a metropolitan area for the purposes of this Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the State Government to designate large urban areas as metropolitan areas for special judicial administration.
examples: A city with a population exceeding one million may be notified as a metropolitan area.

faqs.question: Who declares a metropolitan area?
faqs.answer: The State Government through an official notification.

title: Court of Session
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government shall establish a Court of Session for every sessions division and appoint a Sessions Judge to preside over it. Additional Sessions Judges and Assistant Sessions Judges may also be appointed.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the establishment of Courts of Session and the appointment of Sessions Judges.
examples: Serious offences such as murder are generally tried before a Court of Session.
Additional Sessions Judges may be appointed to handle the workload.

faqs.question: Who presides over a Court of Session?
faqs.answer: A Sessions Judge appointed by the State Government.

title: Subordination of Assistant Sessions Judges
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: All Assistant Sessions Judges shall be subordinate to the Sessions Judge in whose Court they exercise jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes that Assistant Sessions Judges work under the authority and supervision of the Sessions Judge.
examples: An Assistant Sessions Judge functions under the administrative control of the Sessions Judge of that division.

faqs.question: To whom is an Assistant Sessions Judge subordinate?
faqs.answer: The Assistant Sessions Judge is subordinate to the Sessions Judge.

title: Courts of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: In every district, the State Government shall establish as many Courts of Judicial Magistrates of the first class and of the second class as it considers necessary after consultation with the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the State Government to establish Judicial Magistrate Courts in each district based on the needs of the justice system.
examples: A growing district may require additional Judicial Magistrate Courts to handle increasing criminal cases.

faqs.question: Who decides how many Judicial Magistrate Courts are needed?
faqs.answer: The State Government, after consulting the High Court.

title: Chief Judicial Magistrate and Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: In every district, the High Court shall appoint a Judicial Magistrate of the first class to be the Chief Judicial Magistrate and may appoint Additional Chief Judicial Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the appointment of a Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) to supervise Judicial Magistrates within a district.
examples: The Chief Judicial Magistrate oversees the functioning of Judicial Magistrate Courts in the district.

faqs.question: Who appoints the Chief Judicial Magistrate?
faqs.answer: The High Court appoints the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

title: Special Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The High Court may appoint Special Judicial Magistrates for particular cases or classes of cases for a term not exceeding one year at a time.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the High Court to appoint Special Judicial Magistrates to handle specific types of cases or special situations.
examples: A Special Judicial Magistrate may be appointed to deal with a particular category of offences.

faqs.question: Can a Special Judicial Magistrate be appointed permanently?
faqs.answer: No. The appointment is generally for a specified term not exceeding one year at a time.

title: Local jurisdiction of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Subject to the control of the High Court, the Chief Judicial Magistrate may define the local limits of the areas within which the Magistrates appointed under section 11 or section 13 may exercise their powers.
simpleExplanation: This section determines the geographical area within which a Judicial Magistrate can exercise legal authority.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate may be assigned jurisdiction over a specific district or subdivision.

faqs.question: Who determines a Magistrate's local jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: The Chief Judicial Magistrate, subject to the control of the High Court.

title: Subordination of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Every Judicial Magistrate shall be subordinate to the Chief Judicial Magistrate, subject to the general control of the Sessions Judge.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the hierarchy among Judicial Magistrates and places them under the supervision of the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate follows administrative directions issued by the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: To whom are Judicial Magistrates subordinate?
faqs.answer: They are subordinate to the Chief Judicial Magistrate and subject to the general control of the Sessions Judge.

title: Courts of Metropolitan Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: In every metropolitan area, there shall be established as many Courts of Metropolitan Magistrates, and at such places, as the State Government may, after consultation with the High Court, specify by notification.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the establishment of Metropolitan Magistrate Courts in metropolitan areas to handle criminal cases arising within those cities.
examples: A large metropolitan city may have multiple Metropolitan Magistrate Courts to handle criminal matters efficiently.

faqs.question: Who establishes Metropolitan Magistrate Courts?
faqs.answer: The State Government establishes them after consulting the High Court.

title: Chief Metropolitan Magistrate and Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrate
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The High Court shall appoint one of the Metropolitan Magistrates to be the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate and may appoint Additional Chief Metropolitan Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the appointment of a Chief Metropolitan Magistrate to supervise Metropolitan Magistrates in a metropolitan area.
examples: The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate oversees the functioning of Metropolitan Magistrate Courts within a city.

faqs.question: Who appoints the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate?
faqs.answer: The High Court appoints the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate.

title: Special Metropolitan Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The High Court may appoint Special Metropolitan Magistrates for particular cases or classes of cases or for a specified area within a metropolitan area.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the High Court to appoint Special Metropolitan Magistrates to deal with specific categories of cases or designated areas.
examples: A Special Metropolitan Magistrate may be appointed to handle a particular class of offences in a city.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of a Special Metropolitan Magistrate?
faqs.answer: To handle specific cases or classes of cases requiring dedicated judicial attention.

title: Subordination of Metropolitan Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Every Metropolitan Magistrate shall be subordinate to the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate, subject to the general control of the Sessions Judge.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the administrative hierarchy among Metropolitan Magistrates and places them under the supervision of the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate.
examples: A Metropolitan Magistrate follows administrative directions issued by the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate.

faqs.question: To whom are Metropolitan Magistrates subordinate?
faqs.answer: They are subordinate to the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate and subject to the general control of the Sessions Judge.

title: Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: In every district and in every metropolitan area, the State Government may appoint as many persons as it thinks fit to be Executive Magistrates and shall appoint one of them to be the District Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the State Government to appoint Executive Magistrates and designate one of them as the District Magistrate.
examples: The District Magistrate exercises administrative and preventive powers under the Code.

faqs.question: Who appoints Executive Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The State Government appoints Executive Magistrates.

title: Special Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may appoint, for particular areas or for the performance of particular functions, any Executive Magistrates to be called Special Executive Magistrates and confer upon them such powers as are exercisable by Executive Magistrates under this Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the State Government to appoint Special Executive Magistrates for specific areas or special duties and grant them appropriate powers.
examples: A Special Executive Magistrate may be appointed during major public events to help maintain law and order.

faqs.question: Who appoints a Special Executive Magistrate?
faqs.answer: The State Government appoints Special Executive Magistrates.

title: Local Jurisdiction of Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Subject to the control of the State Government, the District Magistrate may define the local limits within which the Executive Magistrates appointed under section 20 may exercise their powers.
simpleExplanation: This section determines the geographical area within which an Executive Magistrate may exercise authority.
examples: An Executive Magistrate may be assigned authority over a specific district subdivision.

faqs.question: Who determines the local jurisdiction of Executive Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The District Magistrate, subject to the control of the State Government.

title: Subordination of Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: All Executive Magistrates, other than the Additional District Magistrate, shall be subordinate to the District Magistrate. The Additional District Magistrate shall also be subordinate to the District Magistrate to such extent as the State Government may direct.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the hierarchy among Executive Magistrates and places them under the supervision of the District Magistrate.
examples: Executive Magistrates working in a district are generally supervised by the District Magistrate.

faqs.question: To whom are Executive Magistrates subordinate?
faqs.answer: They are generally subordinate to the District Magistrate.

title: Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: For every High Court and every district, the Central Government or the State Government shall appoint a Public Prosecutor and may appoint one or more Additional Public Prosecutors for conducting prosecutions, appeals and other proceedings on behalf of the Government.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the appointment of Public Prosecutors who represent the Government in criminal cases before courts.
examples: A Public Prosecutor presents evidence and arguments on behalf of the State in a murder trial.

faqs.question: What is the role of a Public Prosecutor?
faqs.answer: A Public Prosecutor conducts criminal proceedings on behalf of the Government.

title: Assistant Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government shall appoint in every district one or more Assistant Public Prosecutors for conducting prosecutions in the Courts of Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the appointment of Assistant Public Prosecutors to conduct criminal prosecutions before Magistrate Courts.
examples: An Assistant Public Prosecutor may represent the State in a theft case before a Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: Where do Assistant Public Prosecutors usually conduct cases?
faqs.answer: They generally conduct prosecutions before Magistrate Courts.

title: Directorate of Prosecution
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may establish a Directorate of Prosecution consisting of a Director of Prosecution and as many Deputy Directors of Prosecution as it thinks fit. The Directorate shall function under the administrative control of the Home Department in the State.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the State Government to establish a Directorate of Prosecution to supervise and coordinate the work of prosecutors across the State.
examples: The Directorate of Prosecution may issue guidelines to ensure consistent conduct of criminal prosecutions throughout the State.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of the Directorate of Prosecution?
faqs.answer: It supervises, coordinates and improves the functioning of prosecutors in criminal cases.

title: Courts by which offences are triable
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Subject to the other provisions of this Code, offences under the Indian Penal Code and other laws shall be tried by the High Court, Court of Session, or any other court by which such offences are shown in the First Schedule to be triable.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies which courts have the authority to try different criminal offences depending on the nature and seriousness of the offence.
examples: A murder case is generally tried by a Court of Session.
A minor offence may be tried by a Magistrate Court.

faqs.question: How is the trial court determined?
faqs.answer: The First Schedule of the Code specifies which court can try a particular offence.

title: Jurisdiction in the case of juveniles
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Any offence committed by a person who is under the age specified by law relating to children may be dealt with under the provisions of the law relating to children in force at the time.
simpleExplanation: This section provides that offences committed by children are handled according to juvenile justice laws rather than ordinary criminal procedures.
examples: A child accused of theft may be dealt with under the Juvenile Justice Act rather than through a regular criminal court.

faqs.question: Are children tried like adults?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Special juvenile justice laws apply to children in conflict with the law.

title: Sentences which High Courts and Sessions Judges may pass
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: A High Court may pass any sentence authorized by law. A Sessions Judge or Additional Sessions Judge may pass any sentence authorized by law, subject to confirmation requirements in certain cases.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the sentencing powers of High Courts and Sessions Courts.
examples: A Sessions Judge may impose imprisonment for serious offences as permitted by law.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge award any sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to legal provisions and confirmation requirements where applicable.

title: Sentences which Magistrates may pass
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The Court of a Chief Judicial Magistrate, Judicial Magistrate of the First Class and Judicial Magistrate of the Second Class may pass sentences within the limits prescribed by this Code.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the maximum punishments that different classes of Magistrates are authorized to impose.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate of the First Class can impose punishment only within the limits prescribed by law.

faqs.question: Can every Magistrate impose any sentence?
faqs.answer: No. Each class of Magistrate has specific sentencing limits defined by law.

title: Sentence of imprisonment in default of fine
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The Court of a Magistrate may award imprisonment in default of payment of fine, subject to the limits prescribed by law and the sentencing powers of that Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to order imprisonment if a convicted person fails to pay a fine imposed by the court.
punishment: Imprisonment in default of payment of fine, within the limits prescribed by law.
examples: A person fined by a Magistrate may be required to undergo imprisonment if the fine is not paid.

faqs.question: Does imprisonment replace the fine?
faqs.answer: No. The imprisonment is a consequence of non-payment of the fine and does not automatically cancel the fine.

title: Sentence in cases of conviction of several offences at one trial
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: When a person is convicted at one trial of two or more offences, the Court may sentence him for such offences to the several punishments prescribed therefor, subject to the limitations provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how courts may impose sentences when a person is convicted of multiple offences in the same trial.
punishment: Multiple sentences may be imposed, either consecutively or concurrently, subject to legal limits.
examples: A person convicted of theft and forgery in the same trial may receive separate sentences for each offence.

faqs.question: Can multiple sentences run together?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may direct sentences to run concurrently or consecutively depending on the circumstances and law.

title: Mode of conferring powers
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: In conferring powers under this Code, the High Court or State Government may confer such powers by name, by virtue of office, or upon classes of officials generally.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how powers under the Code can be granted to judges, magistrates, or other authorities.
examples: The State Government may grant powers to all Executive Magistrates in a district through a general notification.

faqs.question: Can powers be granted to a group of officials?
faqs.answer: Yes. Powers may be conferred upon classes of officials rather than specific individuals.

title: Powers of officers appointed
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Whenever a person holding an office under the Government is appointed to another office under this Code, he shall be deemed to possess the powers attached to that office unless otherwise directed.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that when a person is appointed to a position under the Code, they automatically receive the powers associated with that position.
examples: A newly appointed Magistrate acquires the powers legally attached to that office.

faqs.question: Does a new appointment automatically include legal powers?
faqs.answer: Yes. The powers associated with the office are generally granted automatically unless restricted.

title: Withdrawal of powers
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The High Court or State Government may withdraw any powers conferred under this Code from a person or authority, subject to the provisions of the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the High Court or State Government to revoke powers previously granted under the Code.
examples: The High Court may withdraw special powers granted to a Magistrate if circumstances require.

faqs.question: Who can withdraw powers granted under the Code?
faqs.answer: The High Court or the State Government, depending on the nature of the power and authority involved.

title: Powers of Judges and Magistrates exercisable by their successors-in-office
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Subject to the other provisions of this Code, the powers and duties of a Judge or Magistrate may be exercised or performed by his successor-in-office.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures continuity in judicial proceedings by allowing a successor Judge or Magistrate to exercise the powers and perform the duties of the previous office-holder.
examples: If a Magistrate is transferred before completing a case, the successor Magistrate may continue the proceedings.

faqs.question: Can a new Magistrate continue a case started by another Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Yes. The successor-in-office can generally exercise the same powers and continue the proceedings.

title: Powers of superior officers of police
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Police officers superior in rank to an officer in charge of a police station may exercise the same powers throughout the local area to which they are appointed as may be exercised by such officer within the limits of his station.
simpleExplanation: This section allows senior police officers to exercise the same powers as a Station House Officer (SHO) within their jurisdiction.
examples: A Superintendent of Police may exercise powers that are ordinarily exercised by a Station House Officer.

faqs.question: Can senior police officers perform duties normally assigned to an SHO?
faqs.answer: Yes. Superior police officers may exercise the same powers within their jurisdiction.

title: Public when to assist Magistrates and police
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Every person is bound to assist a Magistrate or police officer reasonably demanding assistance in the prevention of a breach of the peace, arrest of offenders, prevention of escape, or protection of public property.
simpleExplanation: This section requires citizens to assist Magistrates and police officers when lawful assistance is requested for maintaining public order or enforcing the law.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: A citizen may be asked to help prevent the escape of a person lawfully arrested.

faqs.question: Am I legally required to assist the police if requested?
faqs.answer: Yes, when a lawful request is made for assistance in situations covered by this section.

title: Aid to person, other than police officer, executing warrant
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: When a warrant is directed to a person other than a police officer, any other person may assist in its execution if required.
simpleExplanation: This section permits assistance to persons authorized to execute warrants even if they are not police officers.
examples: A court officer executing a warrant may receive assistance from others when necessary.

faqs.question: Can non-police personnel execute warrants?
faqs.answer: Yes, in situations where the law permits a warrant to be directed to such persons.

title: Public to give information of certain offences
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Every person aware of the commission or intention to commit certain specified offences shall, in the absence of reasonable excuse, forthwith give information to the nearest Magistrate or police officer.
simpleExplanation: This section requires citizens to report certain serious offences or planned offences to the authorities.
punishment: Failure to report may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: A person who becomes aware of a planned dacoity should inform the police immediately.

faqs.question: Do I have a duty to report serious crimes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain serious offences must be reported to the police or a Magistrate.

title: Duty of officers employed in connection with affairs of a village to make certain reports
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Every officer employed in connection with the affairs of a village and every resident of a village shall communicate to the nearest Magistrate or police officer any information they may possess regarding the commission of certain offences, the presence of offenders, suspicious deaths, and other matters specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires village officials and residents to report certain crimes, suspicious activities, and important public safety matters to the authorities.
punishment: Failure to report may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: A village officer learns about a planned robbery and immediately informs the police.
A suspicious death in a village is reported to the nearest Magistrate.

faqs.question: Who has reporting obligations under this section?
faqs.answer: Village officers and residents who become aware of specified offences or suspicious activities.

title: When police may arrest without warrant
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Any police officer may arrest without an order from a Magistrate and without a warrant any person who commits a cognizable offence in his presence or against whom reasonable suspicion or credible information exists regarding involvement in a cognizable offence, subject to the conditions specified in this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the situations in which police can arrest a person without first obtaining a warrant from a court.
examples: A police officer witnesses a theft and immediately arrests the offender.
Police arrest a person based on credible evidence linking them to a serious cognizable offence.

faqs.question: Can police arrest anyone without a warrant?
faqs.answer: No. Arrest without a warrant is allowed only in situations specifically permitted by law.

title: Arrest on refusal to give name and residence
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person accused of committing a non-cognizable offence refuses to provide his name and residence, or provides information believed to be false, a police officer may arrest such person to ascertain his identity.
simpleExplanation: Police may arrest a person involved in a minor offence if the person refuses to disclose their identity or provides false details.
punishment: Detention may continue until the person's identity is established according to law.
examples: A person accused of a minor offence refuses to reveal his name and address and is taken into custody.

faqs.question: Why can police arrest someone under this section?
faqs.answer: To verify the person's identity when they refuse to cooperate or provide false information.

title: Arrest by private person and procedure on such arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Any private person may arrest or cause to be arrested any person who commits a non-bailable and cognizable offence in his presence, and shall without unnecessary delay hand over the arrested person to a police officer.
simpleExplanation: In certain serious situations, an ordinary citizen may detain an offender and hand them over to the police.
examples: A shopkeeper catches a robber committing a serious offence and immediately hands him over to the police.

faqs.question: Can ordinary citizens arrest someone?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only in limited circumstances specified by law.

title: Arrest by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When an offence is committed in the presence of a Magistrate within his local jurisdiction, he may himself arrest or order the arrest of the offender and may commit the offender to custody.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate who directly witnesses an offence can arrest the offender or direct others to do so.
examples: A person creates violence inside a courtroom, and the Magistrate orders the person's immediate arrest.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate personally order an arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. A Magistrate may arrest or order the arrest of a person committing an offence in the Magistrate's presence.

title: Protection of members of the Armed Forces from arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Members of the Armed Forces of the Union shall not be arrested for anything done or purported to be done in the discharge of their official duties except with the consent of the Central Government. The State Government may extend similar protection to specified classes of forces responsible for maintaining public order.
simpleExplanation: This section protects members of the Armed Forces from arrest for actions performed during official duties unless prior approval is obtained from the appropriate Government.
examples: A soldier performing an official operation cannot ordinarily be arrested for actions connected with that duty without the required government approval.

faqs.question: Can a member of the Armed Forces be arrested without permission?
faqs.answer: Generally no, when the act relates to official duties and the law requires prior government consent.

title: Arrest how made
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: An arrest is made by actually touching or confining the body of the person to be arrested, unless there is submission to custody by word or action. Reasonable force may be used if necessary, subject to the limitations provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the legal procedure for making an arrest and the circumstances in which force may be used.
examples: A person voluntarily accompanies the police after being informed of the arrest and submits to custody.
Police may use reasonable force if a suspect attempts to escape.

faqs.question: Can police use force while making an arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only reasonable force necessary to effect the arrest.

title: Search of place entered by person sought to be arrested
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: If a person to be arrested is believed to have entered or to be within any place, any person residing in or in charge of that place shall allow free ingress thereto, and police officers may search the place in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section permits police to search premises where a person sought for arrest is believed to be hiding.
examples: Police receive information that a wanted person is hiding inside a house and conduct a lawful search.

faqs.question: Can police enter a house to arrest a suspect?
faqs.answer: Yes, when they reasonably believe the suspect is inside and follow the procedure prescribed by law.

title: Pursuit of offenders into other jurisdictions
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer may, for the purpose of arresting a person whom he is authorized to arrest without warrant, pursue such person into any place in India.
simpleExplanation: This section allows police to continue pursuing a person across district or State boundaries when making a lawful arrest.
examples: A suspect flees to another State, but the pursuing police officers may continue the chase and arrest the suspect.

faqs.question: Can police pursue a suspect outside their district?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may pursue and arrest the suspect anywhere in India if the arrest is legally authorized.

title: No unnecessary restraint
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: The person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape.
simpleExplanation: This section protects arrested persons from excessive or unnecessary physical restraint while in custody.
examples: Police should not use excessive restraints on a cooperative person who poses no risk of escape.

faqs.question: Can police use excessive force or restraints after arrest?
faqs.answer: No. Only the minimum restraint necessary to prevent escape is permitted.

title: Person arrested to be informed of grounds of arrest and of right to bail
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Every police officer or other person arresting any person without warrant shall forthwith communicate to him full particulars of the offence for which he is arrested or other grounds for such arrest. Where the arrest is for a bailable offence, the person shall also be informed of his right to be released on bail.
simpleExplanation: This section requires that a person who is arrested must be told the reason for the arrest. If the offence is bailable, the person must also be informed about the right to obtain bail.
examples: A person arrested for a bailable offence is informed about the offence and told that bail may be obtained.

faqs.question: Must police tell me why I am being arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes. The grounds of arrest must be communicated immediately.

title: Obligation of person making arrest to inform about the arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Every police officer or other person making an arrest shall immediately give information regarding the arrest and the place where the arrested person is being held to any friend, relative or other person nominated by the arrested person.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that a friend, family member or another nominated person is informed when someone is arrested.
examples: A person arrested by police asks that a family member be informed, and the police communicate the arrest details.

faqs.question: Can my family be informed if I am arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes. The police must inform a nominated relative, friend or other person.

title: Search of arrested person
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Whenever a person is arrested, the person making the arrest may search the arrested individual and place in safe custody all articles found upon him, other than necessary wearing apparel, and provide a receipt where required.
simpleExplanation: This section allows police to search an arrested person and take custody of items found on them for legal and security purposes.
examples: Police seize a knife and mobile phone found on an arrested person and record the items.

faqs.question: Can police search me after arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police may lawfully search an arrested person and secure relevant articles.

title: Power to seize offensive weapons
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer or other person making an arrest may seize any offensive weapons found in the possession of the arrested person and shall deliver them to the court or officer before whom the arrested person is required to be produced.
simpleExplanation: This section allows police to confiscate dangerous weapons found on an arrested person.
examples: A knife found on an arrested individual is seized and submitted as evidence.

faqs.question: What happens to weapons found during arrest?
faqs.answer: They may be seized and produced before the appropriate authority or court.

title: Examination of accused by medical practitioner at the request of police officer
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person is arrested on a charge of committing an offence and there are reasonable grounds for believing that an examination of the person's body will afford evidence of the commission of the offence, a registered medical practitioner may examine the person at the request of a police officer.
simpleExplanation: This section permits medical examination of an accused person when it may help gather evidence related to an offence.
examples: A medical examination is conducted to collect evidence in an assault investigation.

faqs.question: Can police require a medical examination after arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. A medical examination may be conducted when it is likely to provide evidence relevant to the offence.

title: Examination of person accused of rape by medical practitioner
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person is arrested on a charge of committing rape or an attempt to commit rape and there are reasonable grounds for believing that examination of such person will afford evidence relating to the offence, a registered medical practitioner employed in a hospital run by the Government or a local authority shall conduct the examination and prepare a report.
simpleExplanation: This section permits the medical examination of a person accused of rape when the examination may provide evidence relevant to the investigation.
examples: A person accused of rape undergoes a medical examination to collect forensic evidence.

faqs.question: Why is a medical examination conducted in rape cases?
faqs.answer: To collect scientific and forensic evidence that may assist the investigation and trial.

title: Examination of arrested person by medical officer
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person who has been arrested alleges that examination of his body will afford evidence which will disprove the commission of any offence by him or establish the commission of an offence against his body, the Magistrate may direct a medical examination of such person.
simpleExplanation: This section gives an arrested person the right to request a medical examination when it may help prove innocence or reveal injuries suffered while in custody.
examples: An arrested person claims to have been assaulted and requests a medical examination to document injuries.

faqs.question: Can an arrested person request a medical examination?
faqs.answer: Yes. A medical examination may be ordered when it can provide evidence relevant to the case.

title: Identification of person arrested
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Where the identification of a person arrested is considered necessary for the investigation of an offence, the court may direct the holding of an identification parade or adopt other lawful methods of identification.
simpleExplanation: This section allows authorities to identify an arrested person through lawful procedures such as identification parades.
examples: Witnesses participate in an identification parade to identify a suspect arrested for robbery.

faqs.question: What is an identification parade?
faqs.answer: It is a procedure in which witnesses attempt to identify a suspect from a group of individuals.

title: Procedure when police officer deputes subordinate to arrest without warrant
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a police officer in charge of a police station or an investigating officer directs a subordinate officer to arrest a person without a warrant, the subordinate officer shall be given a written order specifying the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for the arrest.
simpleExplanation: This section requires written authorization when a subordinate police officer is directed to make an arrest without a warrant.
examples: A senior police officer issues written instructions to a subordinate officer to arrest a suspect.

faqs.question: Must a subordinate officer carry proof of authorization?
faqs.answer: Yes. The officer should carry the written order and produce it when required.

title: Health and safety of arrested person
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: It shall be the duty of the person having custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police and other authorities to protect the health, safety and well-being of persons in custody.
examples: Police ensure that a person in custody receives necessary medical attention when required.

faqs.question: Who is responsible for the safety of an arrested person?
faqs.answer: The authority or officer having custody of the arrested person.

title: Person arrested to be taken before Magistrate or officer in charge of police station
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer making an arrest without warrant shall, without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions relating to bail, take or send the arrested person before a Magistrate having jurisdiction or before the officer in charge of a police station.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police to promptly present an arrested person before the appropriate authority and not keep the person in custody unnecessarily.
examples: A person arrested without a warrant is taken to the police station and then produced before a Magistrate as required by law.

faqs.question: Can police keep an arrested person indefinitely without producing them before a Magistrate?
faqs.answer: No. The arrested person must be taken before the appropriate authority without unnecessary delay.

title: Person arrested not to be detained more than twenty-four hours
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: No police officer shall detain in custody a person arrested without warrant for a period longer than twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court, unless a special order of a Magistrate authorizes further detention.
simpleExplanation: Police generally cannot keep a person in custody for more than 24 hours without obtaining authorization from a Magistrate.
examples: A suspect arrested on Monday must ordinarily be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours.

faqs.question: What is the 24-hour rule?
faqs.answer: An arrested person must normally be produced before a Magistrate within 24 hours of arrest.

title: Police to report apprehensions
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Officers in charge of police stations shall report to the District Magistrate or Sub-Divisional Magistrate all cases of persons arrested without warrant within the limits of their respective stations.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures oversight by requiring police stations to report arrests made without warrants to senior magistrates.
examples: A police station submits a report of all warrantless arrests made during a specified period.

faqs.question: Why must arrests be reported to Magistrates?
faqs.answer: To ensure accountability and supervision of police powers.

title: Discharge of person apprehended
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: No person who has been arrested by a police officer shall be discharged except on his own bond, on bail, or under the special order of a Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the lawful methods by which an arrested person may be released from custody.
examples: A person arrested for a bailable offence is released after furnishing bail.

faqs.question: How can an arrested person be released?
faqs.answer: Through bail, bond, or a lawful order from a Magistrate.

title: Arrest to be made strictly according to the Code
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: No arrest shall be made except in accordance with the provisions of this Code or any other law for the time being in force providing for arrest.
simpleExplanation: This section makes it clear that arrests can only be carried out according to procedures established by law.
examples: Police cannot arrest a person arbitrarily without legal authority or prescribed procedure.

faqs.question: Can police arrest someone without following legal procedure?
faqs.answer: No. Every arrest must comply with the requirements of law.

title: Form of summons
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Every summons issued by a Court under this Code shall be in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer of the Court or by such other officer as the High Court may direct, and shall bear the seal of the Court.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the legal format that a court summons must follow to be valid.
examples: A court issues a written summons requiring a witness to appear on a specified date.

faqs.question: What makes a summons valid?
faqs.answer: It must be in writing, properly signed, and bear the seal of the Court.

title: Summons how served
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Every summons shall, if practicable, be served personally on the person summoned by delivering or tendering one of the duplicates of the summons.
simpleExplanation: This section explains that a summons should normally be delivered directly to the person who is required to appear before the court.
examples: A court officer personally hands a summons to an accused person.

faqs.question: How is a summons usually served?
faqs.answer: It is normally served personally on the individual named in the summons.

title: Service of summons on corporate bodies and societies
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Service of a summons on a corporation may be effected by serving it on the secretary, local manager or principal officer of the corporation, or by registered post addressed to the chief officer of the corporation.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a summons can be served on companies, corporations, and registered societies.
examples: A summons intended for a company is delivered to its authorized manager.

faqs.question: Can a company receive a summons?
faqs.answer: Yes. A summons may be served on designated officers of the company.

title: Service when persons summoned cannot be found
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Where the person summoned cannot, by the exercise of due diligence, be found, the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates with an adult male member of the family residing with the person summoned.
simpleExplanation: This section provides an alternative method of serving a summons when the person cannot be located despite reasonable efforts.
examples: A summons is left with an adult family member when the intended recipient is unavailable.

faqs.question: What happens if the person is not available to receive the summons?
faqs.answer: The summons may be delivered to an eligible family member residing with that person.

title: Procedure when service cannot be effected as before provided
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: If service cannot be effected by the methods previously provided, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates of the summons to a conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which the person ordinarily resides, and the Court may declare that the summons has been duly served.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a summons to be affixed to a visible part of a person's residence when normal methods of service are unsuccessful.
examples: A court summons is pasted on the front door of a person's residence after repeated failed attempts at personal service.

faqs.question: Can a summons be pasted on a house?
faqs.answer: Yes. If ordinary service is not possible, the law permits substituted service by affixing the summons.

title: Service on Government servant
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Where the person summoned is in the active service of the Government, the Court issuing the summons shall ordinarily send it in duplicate to the head of the office in which such person is employed, and that officer shall cause the summons to be served.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a special procedure for serving summons on Government employees through their department or office.
examples: A summons for a government teacher is sent to the school headmaster for service.

faqs.question: How is a summons served on a Government employee?
faqs.answer: It is generally sent through the head of the office where the employee works.

title: Service of summons outside local limits
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a Court desires that a summons issued by it shall be served at any place outside its local jurisdiction, it shall ordinarily send the summons in duplicate to a Magistrate within whose jurisdiction the person summoned resides or is located.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a summons is served when the person is located outside the court's territorial jurisdiction.
examples: A court in Delhi sends a summons through a Magistrate in Mumbai because the recipient lives there.

faqs.question: Can a court serve a summons outside its district?
faqs.answer: Yes. The summons may be routed through the appropriate Magistrate in the other area.

title: Proof of service in such cases and when serving officer not present
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a summons is served outside the local jurisdiction of the issuing Court, an affidavit or endorsement regarding service may be accepted as evidence of proper service, subject to the provisions of this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how courts verify that a summons has been properly served when the serving officer is not present before the court.
examples: A court accepts a certified report confirming that a summons was delivered in another district.

faqs.question: How does a court verify service outside its jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: It may rely on official endorsements, affidavits, or certificates proving service.

title: Service of summons on witness by post
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in the preceding sections, a Court may, in addition to and simultaneously with the issue of a summons, direct a copy of the summons to be served by registered post addressed to the witness at the place where he ordinarily resides or carries on business.
simpleExplanation: This section permits courts to serve summons on witnesses through registered post in addition to ordinary methods.
examples: A witness receives a court summons through registered post at their home address.

faqs.question: Can a witness receive a summons by post?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may use registered post as an additional method of service.

title: Form of warrant of arrest and duration
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Every warrant of arrest issued by a Court shall be in writing, signed by the presiding officer of the Court and shall bear the seal of the Court. Such warrant shall remain in force until it is cancelled by the Court which issued it or until it is executed.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the legal requirements for an arrest warrant and states that the warrant remains valid until executed or cancelled.
examples: A court issues a signed arrest warrant against an accused who repeatedly fails to appear.

faqs.question: How long does an arrest warrant remain valid?
faqs.answer: It remains valid until executed or cancelled by the issuing Court.

title: Power to direct security to be taken
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Any Court issuing a warrant for the arrest of any person may, in its discretion, direct by endorsement on the warrant that if such person executes a bond with sufficient sureties for attendance before the Court at a specified time and thereafter until otherwise directed, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall take such security and release the person from custody.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a court to authorize the release of a person arrested under a warrant if the person provides a bond and sureties guaranteeing future appearance before the court.
examples: A court issues a warrant but permits the accused to be released after furnishing a bond and sureties.

faqs.question: Can a person arrested under a warrant be released immediately?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the court has endorsed the warrant permitting release upon furnishing security.

title: Warrants to whom directed
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: A warrant of arrest shall ordinarily be directed to one or more police officers, but the Court may direct it to any other person if immediate execution by a police officer is not practicable.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies who may receive and execute an arrest warrant issued by a court.
examples: A warrant is normally directed to the local police station for execution.

faqs.question: Who usually executes an arrest warrant?
faqs.answer: Police officers generally execute arrest warrants.

title: Warrant may be directed to any person
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: A Chief Judicial Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class may direct a warrant to any person within his local jurisdiction for the arrest of an escaped convict, proclaimed offender, or a person accused of a non-bailable offence who is evading arrest.
simpleExplanation: This section allows certain Magistrates to authorize private individuals to execute warrants in limited situations.
examples: A Magistrate authorizes a person to assist in arresting a proclaimed offender hiding in the locality.

faqs.question: Can a warrant be issued to someone other than the police?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specific circumstances permitted by law.

title: Warrant directed to police officer
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: A warrant directed to a police officer may also be executed by any other police officer whose name is endorsed upon the warrant by the officer to whom it is directed.
simpleExplanation: This section permits a police officer to authorize another police officer to execute a warrant when necessary.
examples: A Station House Officer authorizes another officer to execute an arrest warrant.

faqs.question: Can another police officer execute a warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes, if properly endorsed by the officer to whom the warrant was originally directed.

title: Notification of substance of warrant
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: The police officer or other person executing a warrant shall notify the substance thereof to the person to be arrested and, if required, shall show the warrant to that person.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the executing officer to inform the person being arrested about the warrant and show it if requested.
examples: A police officer informs the accused that an arrest warrant has been issued and shows a copy upon request.

faqs.question: Can I ask to see the arrest warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes. The executing officer must show the warrant if you request it.

title: Person arrested to be brought before Court without delay
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall, without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions relating to security, bring the arrested person before the Court before which he is required by law to produce such person. The delay shall not exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Court.
simpleExplanation: This section requires that a person arrested under a warrant be produced before the appropriate Court as quickly as possible and ordinarily within twenty-four hours.
examples: A person arrested under a warrant in one district is promptly brought before the issuing Court.

faqs.question: How quickly must a person arrested under a warrant be produced before a Court?
faqs.answer: Without unnecessary delay and generally within twenty-four hours, excluding travel time.

title: Where warrant may be executed
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: A warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in India.
simpleExplanation: This section allows an arrest warrant issued by a Court to be executed anywhere within India.
examples: A warrant issued by a Court in Delhi can be executed in Kerala if the accused is located there.

faqs.question: Is an arrest warrant limited to the district where it was issued?
faqs.answer: No. A valid arrest warrant may be executed anywhere in India.

title: Warrant forwarded for execution outside jurisdiction
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a warrant is to be executed outside the local jurisdiction of the Court issuing it, the Court may forward the warrant to an Executive Magistrate, District Superintendent of Police, Commissioner of Police, or other appropriate authority within whose jurisdiction it is to be executed.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the procedure for executing a warrant outside the Court's local area.
examples: A Court sends a warrant to police authorities in another State for execution.

faqs.question: How is a warrant executed in another State?
faqs.answer: The issuing Court forwards it to the appropriate authority in that area.

title: Warrant directed to police officer for execution outside jurisdiction
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a warrant directed to a police officer is to be executed outside the jurisdiction of the Court issuing it, the officer may execute it directly or obtain endorsement from a local Magistrate or police authority as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes police officers to execute warrants outside their jurisdiction while following prescribed procedures.
examples: A police officer travels to another State and executes a warrant after complying with local legal requirements.

faqs.question: Can a police officer execute a warrant outside his jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to the procedures laid down in the Code.

title: Procedure on arrest of person against whom warrant issued
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a person is arrested under a warrant outside the district where it was issued, the arrested person shall ordinarily be taken before the nearest Magistrate unless the issuing Court is nearby or the officer has sufficient authority to produce the person directly before the issuing Court.
simpleExplanation: This section sets out the procedure to be followed after a person is arrested under a warrant outside the issuing Court's district.
examples: A person arrested in Bengaluru under a warrant issued in Chennai is first produced before the nearest Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens after an out-of-district arrest under a warrant?
faqs.answer: The arrested person is usually taken before the nearest Magistrate before being transferred to the issuing Court.

title: Procedure by Magistrate before whom arrested person is brought
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a person arrested under a warrant is brought before a Magistrate other than the Court which issued the warrant, the Magistrate shall satisfy himself that the person arrested is the person named in the warrant and may order his transfer to the issuing Court or release him on bail where the law permits.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to verify the identity of the arrested person and decide whether to transfer the person to the issuing Court or grant bail if permitted.
examples: A Magistrate confirms the identity of an accused arrested in another district before arranging transfer to the issuing Court.

faqs.question: Can a local Magistrate grant bail when a warrant was issued by another Court?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the law and the terms of the warrant permit such release.

title: Proclamation for person absconding
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: If a Court has reason to believe that a person against whom a warrant has been issued has absconded or is concealing himself so that the warrant cannot be executed, the Court may publish a written proclamation requiring him to appear at a specified place and time not less than thirty days from the date of publication.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to publicly declare that an absconding person must appear before it within a specified period.
punishment: Failure to appear may result in further legal action, including attachment of property and declaration as a proclaimed offender in certain cases.
examples: An accused who repeatedly evades arrest may be declared absconding through a court proclamation.

faqs.question: What is a proclamation under this section?
faqs.answer: It is a public notice requiring an absconding accused to appear before the Court.

title: Attachment of property of person absconding
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: After issuing a proclamation under section 82, the Court may order attachment of any property, movable or immovable, belonging to the proclaimed person.
simpleExplanation: This section permits a Court to seize or attach the property of a person who is absconding and avoiding court proceedings.
punishment: Property may be attached and placed under court control.
examples: The Court orders attachment of an absconding accused's house and bank accounts.

faqs.question: Can a Court seize property of an absconding accused?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may order attachment of property after following the required procedure.

title: Claims and objections to attachment
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Any person other than the proclaimed person claiming an interest in attached property may file a claim or objection before the Court within the period prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the rights of third parties who may have a legal interest in property attached by the Court.
examples: A family member proves ownership of property that was mistakenly attached and seeks its release.

faqs.question: Can someone challenge an attachment order?
faqs.answer: Yes. A person claiming an interest in the property may file an objection before the Court.

title: Release, sale and restoration of attached property
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: The Court may order release, sale, disposal or restoration of attached property depending upon whether the proclaimed person appears, the claims made, and the circumstances of the case.
simpleExplanation: This section explains what happens to property attached by the Court after the absconding person's case is resolved.
examples: Property attached during an absconding period is restored after the accused appears before the Court and satisfies legal requirements.

faqs.question: Will attached property always remain with the Court?
faqs.answer: No. The Court may release, restore, sell or otherwise dispose of the property according to law.

title: Appeal from order rejecting application for restoration of attached property
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Any person whose claim or objection regarding attached property has been rejected may appeal to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from the decisions of the Court that made the order.
simpleExplanation: This section gives a person the right to appeal when a Court rejects a request to restore or release attached property.
examples: A property owner whose claim is rejected files an appeal before the appropriate appellate court.

faqs.question: Can I challenge a Court's refusal to restore attached property?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section provides a right of appeal against such orders.

title: Issue of warrant in lieu of, or in addition to, summons
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: A Court may issue a warrant of arrest either instead of or in addition to a summons if it has reason to believe that the person summoned will not obey the summons or is likely to abscond.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to issue an arrest warrant when a summons alone may not be sufficient to secure a person's appearance.
examples: A Court issues a warrant after receiving information that the accused is planning to flee.

faqs.question: Can a Court issue a warrant without waiting for a summons to fail?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may do so if there are valid reasons to believe the person will not appear.

title: Power to take bond for appearance
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When any person for whose appearance or arrest a Court is empowered to issue a summons or warrant is present in Court, the Court may require such person to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for future appearance.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to require a person to provide a bond promising to appear before the Court when required.
punishment: Failure to comply may result in forfeiture of the bond and other legal consequences.
examples: A witness present in Court is required to execute a bond to ensure attendance on the next hearing date.

faqs.question: What is a bond for appearance?
faqs.answer: It is a legal undertaking to appear before the Court when required.

title: Arrest on breach of bond for appearance
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: When a person bound by a bond for appearance fails to appear, the Court may issue a warrant directing that the person be arrested and brought before it.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to order the arrest of a person who violates a bond requiring attendance before the Court.
punishment: Arrest and possible forfeiture of bond amount according to law.
examples: An accused fails to appear despite executing a bond, and the Court issues a warrant.

faqs.question: What happens if I violate a bond for appearance?
faqs.answer: The Court may issue a warrant for your arrest and take action regarding the bond.

title: Provisions of this Chapter generally applicable to summonses and warrants of arrest
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: The provisions contained in this Chapter relating to summonses and warrants shall, so far as may be, apply to every summons and warrant issued under this Code.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that the procedural rules in this chapter apply broadly to all summonses and arrest warrants issued under the Code unless a different provision applies.
examples: The rules regarding service and execution generally apply to all summonses and warrants issued under the Code.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It ensures uniform application of procedural rules relating to summonses and warrants.

title: Summons to produce document or other thing
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Whenever any Court or any officer in charge of a police station considers that the production of any document or other thing is necessary or desirable for the purposes of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Code, such Court may issue a summons, or such officer may issue a written order, requiring the person in whose possession or power such document or thing is believed to be to attend and produce it.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court or police officer to require a person to produce documents or objects that may be relevant to an investigation or court proceeding.
punishment: Failure to comply may result in legal action under applicable laws.
examples: Police direct a bank to produce account records relevant to a fraud investigation.
A Court summons a person to produce a contract needed as evidence in a case.

faqs.question: Can the police ask for documents without a search warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may issue a written order under this section requiring production of relevant documents or things.

title: Procedure as to letters and telegrams
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: If any document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal or telegraph authority is required for the purpose of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding, a District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Court of Session or High Court may require its production.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a special procedure for obtaining postal articles, telegrams and similar communications needed in criminal proceedings.
examples: A Court directs the postal authority to produce a parcel relevant to a criminal investigation.

faqs.question: Can police directly obtain postal communications?
faqs.answer: Production generally requires an order from the authorities specified in this section.

title: When search warrant may be issued
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: A Court may issue a search warrant when it has reason to believe that a person will not produce a document or thing as required, or when the document or thing is not known to be in the possession of any person, or when a general search is necessary for the purposes of an investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers a Court to issue a search warrant when documents or evidence cannot be obtained through ordinary means.
examples: A Court issues a search warrant because a suspect refuses to produce important financial records.

faqs.question: When can a search warrant be issued?
faqs.answer: When there are valid reasons to believe that evidence cannot be obtained through a normal summons or production order.

title: Search of place suspected to contain stolen property, forged documents, etc.
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: If a District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the First Class has reason to believe that a place is being used for the deposit or sale of stolen property or for the deposit, sale or production of forged documents or counterfeit items, he may authorize a search of that place.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to authorize searches of places suspected of being connected with stolen property, forged documents or counterfeit items.
examples: Police obtain authorization to search a warehouse suspected of storing stolen goods.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate authorize a search for stolen property?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section specifically permits such searches when reasonable grounds exist.

title: Power to declare certain publications forfeited and to issue search warrants for the same
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Where any publication appears to contain matter punishable under specified provisions of law relating to sedition, promoting enmity, obscenity or similar offences, the State Government may declare every copy of such publication forfeited to the Government and authorize search warrants for its seizure.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Government to prohibit and seize certain publications that contain unlawful content specified by law.
punishment: Copies of the publication may be forfeited and seized.
examples: A publication containing prohibited content is declared forfeited and seized under a Government notification.

faqs.question: Can the Government seize unlawful publications?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section provides the legal framework for forfeiture and seizure of specified publications.

title: Application to High Court to set aside declaration of forfeiture
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Any person having an interest in a publication in respect of which a declaration of forfeiture has been made under section 95 may apply to the High Court to set aside the declaration on the grounds specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows affected persons to challenge a Government order forfeiting a publication by applying to the High Court.
examples: A publisher files an application before the High Court challenging the forfeiture of a book.

faqs.question: Can a forfeiture order be challenged?
faqs.answer: Yes. An application may be made to the High Court seeking to set aside the declaration.

title: Search for persons wrongfully confined
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: If any District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the First Class has reason to believe that any person is confined under circumstances amounting to an offence, he may issue a search warrant, and the person found shall be immediately brought before a Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section enables a Magistrate to order a search when there is reason to believe that someone is being illegally confined.
examples: A Magistrate issues a search warrant after receiving information that a person is being unlawfully detained in a building.

faqs.question: What can be done if someone is being illegally confined?
faqs.answer: A Magistrate may issue a search warrant to locate and rescue the person.

title: Power to compel restoration of abducted females
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Upon complaint and after inquiry, a Magistrate may make an order for the immediate restoration of a woman or female child who has been unlawfully abducted or detained for unlawful purposes.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers a Magistrate to order the rescue and restoration of women or female children who have been unlawfully abducted or detained.
examples: A Magistrate orders the immediate restoration of a minor girl unlawfully detained by another person.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order the rescue of an abducted woman or girl?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section provides authority for such orders.

title: Direction, etc., of search warrants
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
