officialText: The provisions relating to the direction, execution and return of warrants of arrest shall, so far as applicable, apply to search warrants issued under this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: This section states that many of the procedural rules applicable to arrest warrants also apply to search warrants.
examples: A search warrant must be executed and returned to the issuing Court according to prescribed procedures.

faqs.question: Do search warrants follow the same procedural rules as arrest warrants?
faqs.answer: Yes, to the extent those rules are applicable.

title: Persons in charge of closed place to allow search
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Whenever any place liable to search is closed, any person residing in or in charge of such place shall, on demand by the officer executing the warrant and on production of the warrant, allow free ingress and afford all reasonable facilities for a search. If access is denied, the officer may proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires occupants of premises to cooperate with lawful searches and permit entry when a valid search warrant is presented.
punishment: Obstructing a lawful search may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: Police officers executing a search warrant are allowed entry into a house to conduct the search.

faqs.question: Can I refuse entry to officers executing a valid search warrant?
faqs.answer: No. A person in charge of the premises must allow lawful access and facilitate the search.

title: Disposal of things found in search beyond jurisdiction
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: When a search is conducted outside the jurisdiction of the Court that issued the warrant, any thing seized shall be immediately taken before the Court issuing the warrant or before the nearest Magistrate, unless otherwise directed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how property or evidence seized during a search outside the issuing Court's jurisdiction must be handled.
examples: Documents seized in another State are produced before the appropriate Magistrate and then forwarded to the issuing Court.

faqs.question: What happens to items seized during a search outside the issuing Court's area?
faqs.answer: They must be produced before the appropriate judicial authority as required by law.

title: Power of police officer to seize certain property
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Any police officer may seize any property which may be alleged or suspected to have been stolen, or which may be found under circumstances creating suspicion of the commission of any offence. The officer shall report the seizure to the Magistrate having jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes police to seize stolen property or property connected to a suspected offence and requires them to report the seizure to the Magistrate.
examples: Police seize a vehicle suspected of being used in a robbery.
Cash believed to be connected with criminal activity is seized during an investigation.

faqs.question: Can police seize property without a court order?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police may seize property suspected to be stolen or linked to a crime, subject to legal safeguards.

title: Magistrate may direct search in his presence
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Any Magistrate may direct that a search be made in his presence for the purpose of ensuring the proper conduct of the search and the integrity of the proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section permits a Magistrate to personally supervise a search when considered necessary.
examples: A Magistrate attends a search operation involving sensitive evidence.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate personally supervise a search?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may direct that the search be conducted in his presence.

title: Power to impound document or thing produced
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Any Court may, if it thinks fit, impound any document or thing produced before it under this Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to retain custody of documents or objects produced before it when necessary for the proceedings.
examples: A Court impounds an original agreement that is important evidence in a criminal case.

faqs.question: What does it mean to impound a document?
faqs.answer: It means the Court takes custody of the document or object for legal purposes.

title: Reciprocal arrangements regarding processes
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Central Government may enter into reciprocal arrangements with foreign countries for service of summonses, warrants and other judicial processes, and for assistance in criminal investigations and proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section enables cooperation between India and foreign countries in criminal investigations and legal proceedings.
examples: Indian authorities seek assistance from another country to serve a summons on a person residing there.

faqs.question: Can India seek help from foreign countries in criminal cases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reciprocal arrangements allow international cooperation in investigations and legal proceedings.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: In this Chapter, unless the context otherwise requires, terms such as contracting State, identifying, tracing, proceeds of crime, property, and related expressions shall have the meanings assigned to them.
simpleExplanation: This section defines important terms used in the provisions dealing with international cooperation, attachment and forfeiture of property connected with crime.
examples: The term 'proceeds of crime' refers to property derived or obtained from criminal activity.

faqs.question: Why are definitions included in this section?
faqs.answer: They ensure consistent interpretation of international cooperation provisions.

title: Assistance in securing transfer of persons
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Central Government may make arrangements with contracting States for the transfer of persons in custody to assist investigations, inquiries or criminal proceedings, subject to prescribed conditions and safeguards.
simpleExplanation: This section allows India and foreign countries to transfer persons in custody for purposes connected with criminal investigations or court proceedings.
examples: A prisoner in another country may be temporarily transferred to India to provide evidence in a criminal case.

faqs.question: Can a person in custody be transferred between countries for a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. Such transfers are permitted under reciprocal arrangements and legal safeguards.

title: Assistance in relation to orders of attachment or forfeiture of property
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where a Court in India has made an order for attachment or forfeiture of property derived from criminal activity, assistance may be sought from a contracting State for enforcing such order, and similar requests from contracting States may be acted upon in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section enables India and foreign countries to help each other enforce attachment and forfeiture orders relating to criminal property.
punishment: Property may be attached or forfeited in accordance with law.
examples: India requests a foreign country to freeze assets believed to be proceeds of crime.

faqs.question: Can criminal assets located abroad be attached?
faqs.answer: Yes. Assistance may be requested from foreign countries under reciprocal arrangements.

title: Identifying unlawfully acquired property
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: For the purpose of tracing and identifying property believed to be derived from criminal activity, an officer conducting an investigation may exercise powers specified by law, subject to judicial oversight where required.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes investigators to identify and trace property suspected of being obtained through criminal activity.
examples: Investigators trace bank accounts believed to contain money obtained through fraud.

faqs.question: Can investigators trace criminal assets?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section provides authority to identify and trace suspected proceeds of crime.

title: Seizure or attachment of property
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where an investigating officer has reason to believe that property is derived from criminal activity and may be concealed, transferred or dealt with in a manner that could frustrate proceedings, the officer may seek seizure or attachment of such property in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section permits the seizure or attachment of property suspected to be connected with criminal activity in order to prevent its disposal or concealment.
punishment: Property may be seized or attached pending investigation and court proceedings.
examples: Authorities freeze a bank account suspected of holding proceeds of a large-scale fraud.

faqs.question: Why is property attached during an investigation?
faqs.answer: To prevent it from being hidden, transferred or disposed of before the case is decided.

title: Management of properties seized or forfeited
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Central Government may appoint an Administrator to receive, manage and dispose of properties that have been attached, seized or forfeited under the provisions of this Chapter, subject to orders of the competent court.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the appointment of an Administrator to manage property that has been seized, attached or forfeited in criminal proceedings.
examples: A government-appointed Administrator manages an attached property until the court decides the case.

faqs.question: Who manages property that has been seized or attached?
faqs.answer: An Administrator appointed by the Government may manage such property.

title: Notice of forfeiture of property
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Before property is forfeited, notice shall be given to the person affected, requiring that person to explain the source of the property and show cause why it should not be declared unlawfully acquired and forfeited.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that a person is given an opportunity to explain ownership of property before it is forfeited.
punishment: Property may be forfeited if the explanation is unsatisfactory and legal requirements are met.
examples: A person receives notice to explain how certain assets were acquired before forfeiture proceedings continue.

faqs.question: Can property be forfeited without notice?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The affected person must be given an opportunity to respond.

title: Forfeiture of property in certain cases
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where the competent authority is satisfied that any property represents proceeds of crime or has been unlawfully acquired, it may declare such property forfeited to the Government in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows unlawful property or proceeds of crime to be permanently forfeited to the Government after due process.
punishment: Forfeiture of property to the Government.
examples: Assets purchased using money obtained through organized crime may be forfeited.

faqs.question: What happens to property proved to be derived from crime?
faqs.answer: It may be forfeited to the Government after legal proceedings.

title: Fine in lieu of forfeiture
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: In certain circumstances, the competent authority may permit the affected person to pay a fine representing the value of the property instead of forfeiting the property itself.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a monetary payment to be accepted instead of forfeiture when permitted by law.
punishment: Payment of a fine equivalent to the value of the property.
examples: A person pays the assessed value of disputed property instead of losing ownership of the asset.

faqs.question: Can a person avoid forfeiture by paying money?
faqs.answer: In certain legally permitted circumstances, a fine may be accepted in place of forfeiture.

title: Certain transfers to be null and void
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Any transfer of property made after the issuance of notice under this Chapter, with the intention of defeating forfeiture proceedings, shall be ignored and treated as null and void for the purposes of such proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents a person from transferring property to someone else in order to avoid forfeiture proceedings.
punishment: The transfer may be declared ineffective and ignored during forfeiture proceedings.
examples: A person transfers property to a relative after receiving a forfeiture notice, but the transfer is disregarded by the authorities.

faqs.question: Can I transfer property after receiving a forfeiture notice?
faqs.answer: A transfer intended to avoid forfeiture may be treated as invalid.

title: Procedure in respect of letter of request
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where a letter of request is received from a Court or authority in a contracting State seeking assistance in an investigation, inquiry or proceeding, the Central Government may forward the request to the appropriate authority in India for execution in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how India handles requests from foreign countries seeking assistance in criminal investigations or court proceedings.
examples: A foreign court requests Indian authorities to obtain evidence located in India.

faqs.question: What is a letter of request?
faqs.answer: It is a formal request from a foreign authority seeking legal assistance in a criminal matter.

title: Application of this Chapter
chapter: Chapter VIIA - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall apply to proceedings under any law dealing with criminal matters where reciprocal arrangements have been made with a contracting State, unless a contrary intention appears in such law.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies the scope and applicability of the provisions relating to international cooperation and forfeiture of criminal property.
examples: The Chapter applies when India and another country have entered into a reciprocal legal assistance arrangement.

faqs.question: When does this Chapter apply?
faqs.answer: When criminal matters involve countries with which India has reciprocal legal assistance arrangements.

title: Security for keeping the peace on conviction
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a person is convicted of certain offences involving a breach of the peace or disturbance of public tranquillity, the Court may order that person to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding three years.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Court to require a convicted person to promise, through a legal bond, to maintain peace and good conduct after conviction.
punishment: Execution of a bond for keeping the peace for up to three years.
examples: A person convicted of participating in a violent public disturbance may be ordered to execute a peace bond.

faqs.question: What is a peace bond?
faqs.answer: It is a legal undertaking requiring a person to maintain peace and avoid unlawful conduct.

title: Security for keeping the peace in other cases
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When an Executive Magistrate receives information that any person is likely to commit a breach of the peace or disturb public tranquillity, the Magistrate may require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding one year.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to take preventive action against a person who is likely to cause violence, public disorder or a breach of peace.
punishment: Execution of a bond for keeping the peace for up to one year.
examples: Two rival groups are preparing for a violent clash, and the Magistrate directs their leaders to execute peace bonds.

faqs.question: Can action be taken before a crime actually occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section allows preventive action when there is a likelihood of a breach of peace.

title: Security for good behaviour from persons disseminating seditious matters
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When an Executive Magistrate receives information that a person is disseminating, attempting to disseminate, or assisting in the dissemination of certain unlawful or seditious matters, the Magistrate may require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond for good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers a Magistrate to seek security from persons suspected of spreading unlawful content that may threaten public order.
punishment: Execution of a bond for good behaviour for a period prescribed by law.
examples: A person repeatedly circulates material intended to provoke public disorder and is directed to show cause before a Magistrate.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of this section?
faqs.answer: It aims to prevent the spread of unlawful material that may endanger public peace or security.

title: Security for good behaviour from suspected persons
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Magistrate receives information that a person is taking precautions to conceal his presence with a view to committing a cognizable offence and cannot satisfactorily account for himself, the Magistrate may require him to show cause why he should not execute a bond for good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: This section permits preventive action against persons who appear to be preparing to commit a serious offence and cannot explain their conduct.
punishment: Execution of a bond for good behaviour.
examples: A person repeatedly surveils a property at night under suspicious circumstances and fails to provide a reasonable explanation.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate act against a suspicious person before a crime occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preventive measures may be taken when circumstances indicate preparation for a cognizable offence.

title: Security for good behaviour from habitual offenders
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Magistrate receives information that a person is by habit a robber, house-breaker, thief, receiver of stolen property, kidnapper, extortioner, cheat, or habitually commits offences involving breach of peace or public safety, the Magistrate may require such person to execute a bond for good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to require habitual offenders to provide security for maintaining good behaviour.
punishment: Execution of a bond for good behaviour for a period prescribed by law.
examples: A habitual thief with multiple prior offences is directed to execute a bond for good behaviour.

faqs.question: Who is considered a habitual offender?
faqs.answer: A person who repeatedly engages in criminal conduct of the type specified in the section.

title: Order to be made
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Magistrate acting under sections 107, 108, 109 or 110 deems it necessary to require a person to show cause, the Magistrate shall make an order in writing setting forth the substance of the information received, the amount of bond to be executed, the term for which it is to be in force and the number and character of sureties required.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to issue a written order explaining why a person is being asked to furnish a bond and what conditions apply.
examples: A Magistrate issues a written notice specifying the bond amount and sureties required from a habitual offender.

faqs.question: Must the Magistrate give reasons before requiring a bond?
faqs.answer: Yes. A written order containing the required details must be issued.

title: Procedure in respect of person present in Court
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If the person in respect of whom an order under section 111 is made is present in Court, the order shall be read over to him, or if he so desires, its substance shall be explained to him.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that a person present before the Magistrate is informed about the order requiring security and understands its contents.
examples: A person present in Court is informed about the bond amount and the reasons for the proceedings.

faqs.question: Must the person be informed about the order?
faqs.answer: Yes. The order must be read out or explained to the person.

title: Summons or warrant in case of person not so present
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If the person against whom proceedings are initiated is not present in Court, the Magistrate may issue a summons requiring his appearance, or a warrant if immediate arrest is considered necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to compel the appearance of a person who is not present when preventive proceedings are initiated.
examples: A Magistrate issues a summons requiring a suspected habitual offender to appear before the Court.

faqs.question: What happens if the person is not present in Court?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may issue a summons or, in appropriate cases, a warrant.

title: Copy of order under section 111 to accompany summons or warrant
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: Every summons or warrant issued under section 113 shall be accompanied by a copy of the order made under section 111.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that the person receiving a summons or warrant is also provided with a copy of the Magistrate's order explaining the proceedings.
examples: A summons includes a copy of the written order describing the reasons for the preventive action.

faqs.question: Will the person receive details of the allegations?
faqs.answer: Yes. A copy of the order under section 111 must accompany the summons or warrant.

title: Power to dispense with personal attendance
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The Magistrate may dispense with the personal attendance of a person called upon to show cause and permit appearance through a pleader where appropriate.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to excuse personal attendance and permit representation through a lawyer in suitable cases.
examples: A person living far away is permitted to appear through an advocate.

faqs.question: Can a lawyer appear on behalf of the person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may allow representation through a pleader.

title: Inquiry as to truth of information
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a person appears or is brought before a Magistrate under this Chapter, the Magistrate shall proceed to inquire into the truth of the information upon which action has been taken and may take evidence as in a summons case.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to conduct an inquiry and examine evidence before deciding whether security should be required.
examples: The Magistrate hears witnesses before deciding whether a peace bond should be imposed.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate require a bond without conducting an inquiry?
faqs.answer: No. An inquiry into the truth of the allegations must first be conducted.

title: Order to give security
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If, upon completion of the inquiry under section 116, the Magistrate is satisfied that it is necessary for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour, the Magistrate may order the person concerned to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for such period as permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to require a person to furnish a bond after finding sufficient grounds during the inquiry.
punishment: Execution of a bond with or without sureties for the period specified by law.
examples: After hearing evidence, the Magistrate directs a person likely to cause public disorder to execute a peace bond.

faqs.question: When can a Magistrate require a security bond?
faqs.answer: After completing the inquiry and being satisfied that preventive action is necessary.

title: Discharge of person informed against
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If, on completion of the inquiry, the Magistrate finds that it is not proved that security should be required, the Magistrate shall make an order discharging the person.
simpleExplanation: This section protects individuals by requiring their discharge when the allegations are not substantiated during the inquiry.
examples: The Magistrate finds insufficient evidence of a likely breach of peace and closes the proceedings.

faqs.question: What happens if the allegations are not proved?
faqs.answer: The person must be discharged and no bond can be required.

title: Commencement of period for which security is required
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If a person ordered to give security is, at the time the order is made, serving a sentence of imprisonment, the period for which security is required shall commence upon the expiration of that sentence.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies when the bond period begins if the person is already in prison.
examples: A prisoner ordered to execute a peace bond begins the bond period only after release from prison.

faqs.question: Does the bond period run while the person is in jail?
faqs.answer: No. It normally starts after the imprisonment ends.

title: Contents of bond
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The bond to be executed under this Chapter shall bind the person making it to keep the peace or maintain good behaviour, as the case may be, and shall contain such conditions as required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies what must be included in a bond executed under preventive proceedings.
examples: A peace bond requires the person to avoid unlawful conduct and maintain public order.

faqs.question: What does a peace bond contain?
faqs.answer: It contains an undertaking to keep the peace or maintain good behaviour.

title: Power to reject sureties
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: A Magistrate may refuse to accept any surety offered under this Chapter if the Magistrate considers the surety to be unsuitable, insufficient or otherwise unacceptable under the law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to reject a person offered as a surety if that person is not suitable to guarantee compliance with the bond.
examples: A Magistrate rejects a surety who lacks the financial means to satisfy the bond obligations.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate reject a surety?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may reject a surety who is not considered appropriate or reliable.

title: Imprisonment in default of security
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If a person ordered to give security under this Chapter fails to furnish the required bond or sureties, the Magistrate may order that person to be detained in prison until the period for which security was required expires or until the security is furnished, subject to the provisions of law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows imprisonment when a person fails to provide the bond or sureties ordered by the Magistrate.
punishment: Detention in prison until security is furnished or the prescribed period expires.
examples: A person directed to execute a peace bond refuses to do so and is detained as permitted by law.

faqs.question: What happens if a person refuses to furnish the required bond?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may order detention in prison according to the provisions of this section.

title: Release of persons imprisoned for failing to give security
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: A person imprisoned for failure to furnish security may be released if the required security is subsequently furnished or if the competent authority determines that continued detention is no longer necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a mechanism for releasing a person who was detained for not providing the required bond or sureties.
examples: A person detained for failure to furnish sureties is released after providing the required bond.

faqs.question: Can a detained person be released later?
faqs.answer: Yes. Release may occur when the required security is furnished or detention is no longer justified.

title: Security for unexpired period of bond
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a person bound by a bond under this Chapter is convicted of an offence involving a breach of the conditions of the bond, the Court may require fresh security for the unexpired portion of the original period.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to demand a new bond if a person violates the conditions of an existing bond.
punishment: Requirement to furnish fresh security and possible detention in default.
examples: A person bound by a good behaviour bond commits another offence and is required to furnish fresh security.

faqs.question: What happens if a bond holder violates the bond conditions?
faqs.answer: The Court may require fresh security for the remaining period.

title: Power to direct levy of amount due on certain recognizances
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: Where an amount becomes payable under a bond or recognizance executed under this Chapter, the Court may recover the amount in the manner provided by law for the recovery of fines.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to recover money payable under a bond if its conditions are breached.
punishment: Recovery of the bond amount according to law.
examples: The Court recovers the bond amount after determining that the bond conditions were violated.

faqs.question: How is a bond amount recovered?
faqs.answer: It may be recovered in the same manner as a criminal fine.

title: Security for keeping the peace and good behaviour by habitual offenders and others
chapter: Chapter VIII - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter relating to bonds, sureties, inquiries, enforcement and related matters shall apply to persons against whom proceedings are initiated for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour under the relevant sections of the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section serves as a general procedural provision ensuring that preventive security proceedings are administered consistently.
examples: A habitual offender subjected to preventive proceedings must comply with the same bond and surety requirements established in this Chapter.

faqs.question: Why is this section included?
faqs.answer: It ensures consistent application of the procedural rules governing preventive security proceedings.

title: Order for maintenance of wives, children and parents
chapter: Chapter IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
officialText: If any person having sufficient means neglects or refuses to maintain his wife, legitimate or illegitimate minor child, adult child unable to maintain itself due to physical or mental abnormality, or father or mother unable to maintain themselves, a Magistrate may order such person to make a monthly allowance for maintenance.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers a Magistrate to order financial support for dependent family members who are neglected by a person capable of maintaining them.
punishment: Failure to comply may result in enforcement proceedings and other consequences provided by law.
examples: A father with sufficient income refuses to support his minor child, and the Magistrate orders monthly maintenance.

faqs.question: Can parents seek maintenance from their children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Parents who cannot maintain themselves may seek maintenance under this provision.

title: Enforcement of order of maintenance
chapter: Chapter IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
officialText: A copy of the maintenance order shall be supplied to the person in whose favour it is made, and such order may be enforced by any Magistrate in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the procedure for enforcing maintenance orders passed by a Magistrate.
punishment: Failure to obey a maintenance order may lead to recovery proceedings and imprisonment as provided by law.
examples: A maintenance order issued in one district is enforced in another district where the respondent resides.

faqs.question: How can a maintenance order be enforced?
faqs.answer: It may be enforced through the Magistrate's Court using the procedures prescribed by law.

title: Alteration in allowance
chapter: Chapter IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
officialText: On proof of a change in circumstances, a Magistrate may alter, vary, increase, decrease or cancel a maintenance allowance previously ordered under this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: This section allows maintenance amounts to be modified when circumstances change, such as income changes or new financial needs.
examples: A maintenance amount is increased because the cost of living has significantly risen.

faqs.question: Can a maintenance order be changed later?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may modify it if circumstances have changed.

title: Transfer of maintenance orders
chapter: Chapter IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
officialText: Maintenance orders may be transmitted and enforced through the competent Magistrate having jurisdiction over the area where the person liable to pay maintenance resides or is located.
simpleExplanation: This section facilitates enforcement of maintenance orders across different jurisdictions.
examples: A maintenance order issued in Kerala is transmitted for enforcement in Karnataka where the respondent resides.

faqs.question: Can a maintenance order be enforced in another State?
faqs.answer: Yes. The order may be transmitted to the appropriate Magistrate for enforcement.

title: Power of Magistrate to make orders in maintenance proceedings
chapter: Chapter IX - Order for Maintenance of Wives, Children and Parents
officialText: A Magistrate exercising jurisdiction under this Chapter may pass such interim and final orders as are necessary to secure the effective implementation of maintenance proceedings in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section recognizes the Magistrate's authority to issue appropriate orders during maintenance proceedings.
examples: A Magistrate grants interim maintenance while the main maintenance application is pending.

faqs.question: Can interim maintenance be granted before the final order?
faqs.answer: Yes. Appropriate interim orders may be issued where justified.

title: Protection against prosecution for acts done under preceding sections
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: No prosecution shall be instituted against any person for acts purported to be done under sections relating to the dispersal of unlawful assemblies except with the sanction of the appropriate Government, where such sanction is required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides legal protection to officials and others acting in good faith while dispersing unlawful assemblies, subject to the safeguards provided by law.
examples: A police officer acting lawfully during the dispersal of a violent mob receives statutory protection from unauthorized prosecution.

faqs.question: Does this section give complete immunity to officials?
faqs.answer: No. It provides protection only in circumstances specified by law and may require government sanction before prosecution.

title: Conditional order for removal of nuisance
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever a District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or other Executive Magistrate specially empowered receives information of the existence of a public nuisance, the Magistrate may make a conditional order requiring the person responsible to remove the nuisance, regulate the activity, or appear and show cause against the order.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers Magistrates to take action against public nuisances affecting health, safety, convenience or public rights.
punishment: Failure to comply may result in further proceedings and enforcement measures.
examples: A factory causing dangerous pollution may be directed to remove the nuisance or explain why action should not be taken.

faqs.question: What is a public nuisance?
faqs.answer: It is an act or condition that harms or inconveniences the public or a section of the public.

title: Service or notification of order
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The conditional order made under section 133 shall be served upon the person against whom it is made in the manner provided for service of summons, or, where such service is not practicable, by public notification.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a nuisance removal order must be communicated to the affected person.
examples: A nuisance order is personally served on the owner of a property causing obstruction to a public road.

faqs.question: How is a nuisance order communicated?
faqs.answer: It is served personally or, if necessary, through public notification.

title: Person to whom order is addressed to obey or show cause
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The person against whom a conditional order is made shall either comply with the order within the time specified or appear before the Magistrate and show cause why the order should not be made absolute.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the affected person a choice: obey the order or appear before the Magistrate and challenge it.
punishment: Failure to comply or appear may result in further legal action.
examples: A business owner either removes an obstruction from a public pathway or appears before the Magistrate to contest the order.

faqs.question: Can a person challenge a nuisance order?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person may appear before the Magistrate and show cause against the order.

title: Consequences of failure to obey order
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the person against whom a conditional order has been made does not perform the act directed by the order and does not appear and show cause, the order shall be made absolute and may be enforced according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to enforce a nuisance order when the affected person neither complies with it nor contests it.
punishment: The order becomes final and may be enforced; additional penalties may apply under law.
examples: A person ignores a lawful order to remove an obstruction from a public road, and the Magistrate orders enforcement.

faqs.question: What happens if someone ignores the Magistrate's order?
faqs.answer: The order may become final and be enforced by the authorities.

title: Procedure where existence of public right is denied
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When a person against whom an order under section 133 has been made denies the existence of any public right in respect of the way, river, channel or place concerned, the Magistrate shall inquire into the matter and determine whether there is reliable evidence supporting such denial.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when a person argues that the public does not have the right claimed by the Magistrate, such as the right to use a road or pathway.
examples: A landowner claims that a pathway is private property and not a public road, requiring the Magistrate to examine the claim.

faqs.question: What if someone disputes the existence of a public right?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate must examine the evidence before proceeding further.

title: Procedure where person appears to show cause
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the person against whom the conditional order is made appears and shows cause, the Magistrate shall take evidence and conduct an inquiry in the manner prescribed for summons cases.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to hear evidence and conduct a proper inquiry when a person challenges a nuisance order.
examples: A factory owner appears before the Magistrate and presents evidence contesting allegations of pollution.

faqs.question: Will the person get a chance to present evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate must conduct an inquiry and consider the evidence presented.

title: Power of Magistrate to direct local investigation and examination of expert
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: For the purposes of an inquiry under this Chapter, a Magistrate may direct a local investigation to be conducted and may summon and examine experts regarding matters relevant to the proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to obtain expert opinions or conduct site inspections before making a decision.
examples: A Magistrate orders an environmental inspection to determine whether a factory is causing pollution.

faqs.question: Can experts be involved in nuisance proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may seek expert assistance when necessary.

title: Power of Magistrate to furnish written instructions
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Where a local investigation is directed under section 139, the Magistrate may furnish written instructions to the person conducting the investigation regarding the matters to be examined and reported.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to specify the scope and objectives of a local investigation.
examples: The Magistrate instructs an engineer to assess whether a building creates a danger to public safety.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate guide the investigation process?
faqs.answer: Yes. Written instructions may be issued to the investigator.

title: Procedure on order being made absolute and consequences of disobedience
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When an order under section 133 is made absolute, the person concerned shall comply with it within the time specified. If the order is disobeyed, the Magistrate may cause the required act to be performed and recover the costs from the person responsible, in addition to any legal consequences.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes enforcement of nuisance orders and allows authorities to recover expenses from those who fail to comply.
punishment: Enforcement action, recovery of costs, and other legal consequences as provided by law.
examples: Authorities remove an illegal obstruction from a public road and recover the costs from the responsible person.

faqs.question: What happens if a nuisance order is ignored?
faqs.answer: The authorities may enforce the order themselves and recover the costs from the responsible person.

title: Injunction pending inquiry
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If immediate measures are necessary to prevent imminent danger, serious injury to the public, or serious damage to property, the Magistrate may issue an injunction directing a person to abstain from a particular act pending completion of the inquiry.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to issue temporary directions to prevent harm while a nuisance inquiry is still in progress.
punishment: Violation of the injunction may result in legal action and enforcement measures.
examples: A Magistrate orders a factory to temporarily stop discharging waste into a river until the inquiry is completed.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate issue temporary orders before completing an inquiry?
faqs.answer: Yes. Temporary injunctions may be issued when urgent action is necessary to protect the public.

title: Magistrate may prohibit repetition or continuance of public nuisance
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: A District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or other Executive Magistrate specially empowered may order any person not to repeat or continue a public nuisance when necessary for preventing danger, injury or annoyance to the public.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers Magistrates to stop ongoing or repeated public nuisances.
punishment: Disobedience may attract enforcement action and penalties under applicable law.
examples: A Magistrate directs a business owner to stop repeatedly blocking a public pathway.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate permanently stop a nuisance?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may order a person not to continue or repeat the nuisance.

title: Power to issue order in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: In urgent cases where immediate prevention or speedy remedy is desirable, a District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or other specially empowered Executive Magistrate may issue a written order directing any person to abstain from a certain act or regulating certain activities if such direction is likely to prevent obstruction, annoyance, injury, danger to human life, health or safety, disturbance of public tranquillity, or a riot or affray.
simpleExplanation: This is the famous Section 144. It allows Magistrates to impose restrictions in urgent situations to maintain public order and prevent violence or danger.
punishment: Violation of an order may attract prosecution under applicable criminal laws.
examples: A Magistrate prohibits large public gatherings during a period of communal tension.
Restrictions are imposed in an area experiencing violent protests.

faqs.question: What is Section 144 commonly used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to prevent riots, violence, public disorder and other urgent threats to public safety.

title: Procedure where dispute concerning land or water is likely to cause breach of peace
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When an Executive Magistrate is satisfied from a police report or other information that a dispute concerning land, water, boundaries or related rights is likely to cause a breach of the peace, the Magistrate may require the parties to appear and submit written statements regarding actual possession.
simpleExplanation: This section helps prevent violence arising from land and property disputes by allowing the Magistrate to determine who was in possession until the matter is resolved by a competent court.
examples: Two families claim possession of the same agricultural land, creating tension in the village.

faqs.question: Does this section decide ownership of land?
faqs.answer: No. It only addresses possession for maintaining public peace and does not determine ownership rights.

title: Power to attach subject of dispute and appoint receiver
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the Magistrate considers the case to be one of emergency or decides that none of the parties was in possession, or is unable to determine possession, the Magistrate may attach the property in dispute until a competent court determines the rights of the parties and may appoint a receiver to manage the property.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to temporarily take control of disputed property in order to prevent violence and preserve peace.
examples: A disputed parcel of land is attached by the Magistrate until a civil court resolves the ownership dispute.

faqs.question: What is attachment under this section?
faqs.answer: It means the property is placed under official control until the dispute is resolved.

title: Dispute concerning right of use of land or water
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When an Executive Magistrate is satisfied that a dispute likely to cause a breach of the peace exists regarding any alleged right of use of land, water, or related resources, the Magistrate may inquire into the matter and make appropriate orders concerning the exercise of such right until determined by a competent court.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with disputes about rights to use land, water, pathways, wells, canals, or similar resources when such disputes may lead to violence.
examples: Two villages dispute the right to use water from a canal, creating tension and risk of violence.

faqs.question: Does this section decide ownership of the land?
faqs.answer: No. It addresses temporary use rights to prevent breaches of peace until a competent court decides the matter.

title: Local inquiry
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever a local inquiry is necessary for proceedings under sections relating to disputes concerning land, water or rights of use, the Magistrate may depute a subordinate Magistrate or other competent person to conduct the inquiry and submit a report.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to order a local investigation to better understand facts relating to a dispute.
examples: A local inspection is conducted to determine who is actually using a disputed pathway.

faqs.question: Why is a local inquiry conducted?
faqs.answer: To gather facts from the location and assist the Magistrate in making an informed decision.

title: Police to prevent cognizable offences
chapter: Chapter XI - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: Every police officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing, and shall to the best of his ability prevent, the commission of any cognizable offence.
simpleExplanation: This section imposes a duty on police officers to take preventive action to stop cognizable offences before they occur.
examples: Police intervene after receiving information that a planned assault is about to take place.

faqs.question: Do police have a duty to prevent crimes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police officers are legally required to prevent cognizable offences whenever possible.

title: Information of design to commit cognizable offences
chapter: Chapter XI - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: Every police officer receiving information regarding a plan or design to commit a cognizable offence shall communicate such information to the police officer to whom he is subordinate and to any other officer whose duty it is to prevent or investigate the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police officers to report information about planned crimes to the appropriate authorities.
examples: A police constable learns about a planned robbery and immediately informs senior officers.

faqs.question: What should police do if they learn about a planned crime?
faqs.answer: They must promptly communicate the information to the appropriate authorities.

title: Arrest to prevent the commission of cognizable offences
chapter: Chapter XI - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: A police officer knowing of a design to commit any cognizable offence may arrest, without orders from a Magistrate and without a warrant, the person designing to commit the offence if it appears that the commission of the offence cannot otherwise be prevented.
simpleExplanation: This section allows preventive arrest when police reasonably believe a person is about to commit a cognizable offence and arrest is necessary to prevent it.
examples: Police arrest a person carrying weapons who is about to participate in a planned violent attack.

faqs.question: Can police arrest someone before a crime is committed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preventive arrest is permitted when it is necessary to stop a cognizable offence from occurring.

title: Prevention of injury to public property
chapter: Chapter XI - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: A police officer may take such measures as may be necessary to prevent injury to public property, including roads, bridges, public buildings and other property belonging to or controlled by the Government or local authorities.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes police officers to act immediately to protect public property from damage or destruction.
examples: Police intervene to stop a mob from damaging a government office during a protest.

faqs.question: Can police act before public property is damaged?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police may take preventive measures to protect public property.

title: Inspection of weights and measures
chapter: Chapter XI - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: A police officer may, when authorized by law, inspect weights, measures or instruments used in trade where there is reason to believe that an offence relating to fraudulent use or manipulation is being committed.
simpleExplanation: This section allows inspection of weighing and measuring devices to prevent cheating and fraud in commercial transactions.
examples: Authorities inspect weighing machines at a marketplace following complaints of short weighing.

faqs.question: Why are weights and measures inspected?
faqs.answer: To ensure fairness in trade and prevent fraudulent practices.

title: Information in cognizable cases
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be reduced to writing, read over to the informant, and signed by the informant. The substance of such information shall be entered in a prescribed record.
simpleExplanation: This is the FIR provision. It requires police to record information about cognizable offences and begin the legal process of investigation.
examples: A victim reports a robbery at a police station, and the information is recorded as an FIR.

faqs.question: What is an FIR?
faqs.answer: An FIR (First Information Report) is the first formal record of information about a cognizable offence.

title: Information as to non-cognizable cases and investigation of such cases
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When information is given regarding a non-cognizable offence, the police shall enter the substance of the information in a prescribed register and refer the informant to the Magistrate. Investigation cannot ordinarily begin without the Magistrate's order.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how police handle non-cognizable offences, where court authorization is generally required before investigation.
examples: A complaint involving a minor non-cognizable offence is recorded and referred to the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can police investigate a non-cognizable offence without permission?
faqs.answer: Generally no. A Magistrate's order is usually required.

title: Police officer's power to investigate cognizable case
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Any officer in charge of a police station may investigate any cognizable case without the order of a Magistrate, subject to the provisions of the Code. Such proceedings shall not be questioned merely on the ground that the officer lacked territorial jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: This section gives police the authority to investigate cognizable offences without first obtaining permission from a Magistrate.
examples: Police begin investigating a murder case immediately after registration of the FIR.

faqs.question: Do police need a Magistrate's approval to investigate a cognizable offence?
faqs.answer: No. Police may start the investigation directly.

title: Procedure for investigation
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If, from information received or otherwise, an officer in charge of a police station has reason to suspect the commission of a cognizable offence, he shall forthwith send a report to the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence and shall proceed in person, or depute a subordinate officer, to investigate the facts and circumstances of the case.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the steps police must take after receiving information about a cognizable offence, including informing the Magistrate and starting the investigation.
examples: After receiving an FIR about burglary, the police send a report to the Magistrate and visit the crime scene.

faqs.question: What happens after an FIR is registered?
faqs.answer: Police begin investigating and send a report to the appropriate Magistrate.

title: Report how submitted
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Reports submitted under section 157 shall be forwarded through such superior police officer as the State Government may appoint, and such officer may issue instructions or make observations as deemed appropriate.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how investigation reports are transmitted through the police hierarchy before reaching the Magistrate.
examples: An investigation report is reviewed by a senior police officer before being forwarded.

faqs.question: Can senior police officers review investigation reports?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reports may pass through superior officers who can provide directions.

title: Power to hold investigation or preliminary inquiry
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A Magistrate receiving a report under section 157 may direct an investigation, hold a preliminary inquiry, or otherwise proceed according to law for determining whether there is sufficient ground for further action.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the Magistrate authority to supervise or initiate further inquiry after receiving a police report.
examples: A Magistrate orders further inquiry after reviewing a police report that lacks sufficient details.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order further investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may direct investigation or conduct an inquiry.

title: Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A police officer conducting an investigation may require the attendance of any person acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case, provided such person resides within the limits prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows investigating officers to call witnesses and other knowledgeable persons to assist in an investigation.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract legal consequences as provided by law.
examples: Police ask a shopkeeper who witnessed a theft to appear and provide information.

faqs.question: Can police require witnesses to attend an investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persons familiar with the facts of the case may be required to attend.

title: Examination of witnesses by police
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Any police officer making an investigation may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case, and such person shall answer truly all questions relating to the case, except those that may expose him to a criminal charge, penalty or forfeiture.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes police to question witnesses during an investigation and record relevant information.
examples: Police interview eyewitnesses who saw a road accident.

faqs.question: Must a witness answer police questions?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, except where the answer may incriminate the witness.

title: Statements to police not to be signed; use of statements in evidence
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: No statement made by any person to a police officer in the course of an investigation shall, if reduced to writing, be signed by the person making it. Such statements shall not ordinarily be used as evidence except for the purpose of contradicting the witness in the manner provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents police statements from being treated as substantive evidence in court and restricts their use mainly for contradiction during trial.
examples: A witness's police statement is used to highlight inconsistencies when the witness gives different testimony in court.

faqs.question: Can a police statement be used as evidence in court?
faqs.answer: Generally no. It is mainly used for contradiction or limited purposes allowed by law.

title: No inducement to be offered
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: No police officer or person in authority shall offer any inducement, threat or promise to any person examined during an investigation for the purpose of obtaining a statement or confession.
simpleExplanation: This section protects individuals from being pressured, threatened or bribed into making statements during an investigation.
punishment: Improper conduct may affect the admissibility and reliability of evidence and may attract disciplinary or legal action.
examples: A police officer cannot promise leniency in exchange for a statement.

faqs.question: Can police threaten someone to obtain a statement?
faqs.answer: No. Threats, inducements and promises are prohibited.

title: Recording of confessions and statements
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Any Metropolitan Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate may record a confession or statement made during an investigation. Before recording a confession, the Magistrate shall explain that the person is not bound to confess and that any confession may be used as evidence against him. The Magistrate must ensure that the confession is voluntary.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the recording of confessions and important statements before a Magistrate to ensure they are voluntary and legally valid.
examples: An accused voluntarily confesses before a Magistrate after being informed of the legal consequences.

faqs.question: Can police record a confession that will be used in court?
faqs.answer: A confession intended for evidentiary use is generally recorded by a Magistrate under this section.

title: Search by police officer
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Whenever a police officer making an investigation has reasonable grounds for believing that anything necessary for the purposes of the investigation may be found in any place within the limits of the police station and that obtaining a search warrant would cause undue delay, the officer may conduct a search in accordance with the provisions of law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows police to conduct searches during investigations without first obtaining a warrant when immediate action is necessary.
examples: Police immediately search a suspect's premises to prevent destruction of crucial evidence.

faqs.question: Can police search without a warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes, in certain urgent situations specified by law.

title: When officer in charge of police station may require another to issue search warrant
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a police officer requires a search to be conducted outside his jurisdiction, he may request the officer in charge of the police station having jurisdiction over the area to carry out the search or take necessary action in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a procedure for conducting searches outside the investigating officer's local jurisdiction.
examples: Police in Bengaluru request police in Chennai to conduct a search relating to an ongoing investigation.

faqs.question: Can police conduct searches outside their jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may seek assistance from the local police having jurisdiction.

title: Letter of request to competent authority for investigation in a country or place outside India
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If, during an investigation in India, evidence is believed to be available in a country or place outside India, the Court may issue a letter of request to the competent authority in that country seeking assistance in obtaining such evidence in accordance with applicable treaties and laws.
simpleExplanation: This section allows Indian authorities to seek help from foreign countries when evidence relevant to a criminal investigation is located abroad.
examples: Indian investigators seek bank records located in a foreign country through a letter of request.

faqs.question: Can Indian courts seek evidence from another country?
faqs.answer: Yes. A letter of request may be issued to the competent foreign authority.

title: Letter of request from a country or place outside India to a Court or authority for investigation in India
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a letter of request is received from a foreign Court or authority seeking assistance in an investigation or proceeding, the competent authority in India may execute the request in accordance with law and applicable reciprocal arrangements.
simpleExplanation: This section enables India to assist foreign countries by gathering evidence or conducting investigative actions within India.
examples: Indian authorities collect witness statements in India at the request of a foreign court.

faqs.question: Can foreign countries request investigative assistance from India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Such requests may be executed according to Indian law and international arrangements.

title: Procedure when investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Whenever an investigation cannot be completed within twenty-four hours and there are grounds for believing that the accusation is well-founded, the police shall forward the accused to the nearest Judicial Magistrate along with the case diary and request authorization for further detention in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs remand. If police cannot finish an investigation within twenty-four hours, they must seek permission from a Magistrate to keep the accused in custody.
examples: Police seek judicial remand for an accused in a complex fraud investigation requiring additional inquiry.

faqs.question: Can police keep an accused in custody beyond twenty-four hours?
faqs.answer: Only with authorization from a Magistrate under this section.

title: Report of investigation by subordinate police officer
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a subordinate police officer conducts an investigation, he shall report the results to the officer in charge of the police station who may then take further action according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires investigating officers working under supervision to submit their findings to the station officer in charge.
examples: A sub-inspector completes witness interviews and submits a report to the station house officer.

faqs.question: Who receives the investigation report of a subordinate officer?
faqs.answer: The officer in charge of the police station.

title: Release of accused when evidence deficient
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If, upon investigation, there is insufficient evidence or reasonable ground of suspicion to justify forwarding the accused to a Magistrate, the officer in charge shall release the accused on executing a bond to appear before a Magistrate if and when required.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police to release an accused person when there is not enough evidence to proceed with prosecution.
examples: A suspect is released after investigation reveals no evidence connecting him to the alleged offence.

faqs.question: Can police release an accused during investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. If evidence is insufficient, the accused may be released on bond.

title: Cases to be sent to Magistrate when evidence is sufficient
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If, upon investigation, there is sufficient evidence or reasonable ground to proceed against the accused, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward the accused to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence and proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police to send the case to the Magistrate when sufficient evidence exists to prosecute the accused.
examples: After collecting witness statements, forensic reports and other evidence, police forward the accused to the Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens when police find sufficient evidence?
faqs.answer: The accused and the case are forwarded to the Magistrate for further proceedings.

title: Complainant and witnesses not to accompany police officer and not to be subjected to restraint
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: No complainant or witness shall be required to accompany a police officer during an investigation, and no such person shall be subjected to unnecessary restraint or inconvenience merely because he is assisting the investigation.
simpleExplanation: This section protects complainants and witnesses from being unnecessarily detained or compelled to accompany police officers during investigations.
examples: A witness providing information cannot be forced to remain with the police throughout the investigation.

faqs.question: Can police force a witness to accompany them during an investigation?
faqs.answer: No. Witnesses and complainants should not be subjected to unnecessary restraint.

title: Diary of proceedings in investigation
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every police officer conducting an investigation shall maintain a case diary recording the time at which information was received, the places visited, the circumstances discovered, and the actions taken during the investigation.
simpleExplanation: This section requires investigating officers to maintain a detailed record of investigative activities.
examples: A police officer records witness interviews, site visits and evidence collection in the case diary.

faqs.question: What is a case diary?
faqs.answer: It is the official record maintained by police documenting the progress of an investigation.

title: Report of police officer on completion of investigation
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every investigation shall be completed without unnecessary delay. Upon completion, the officer in charge shall forward a report to the Magistrate containing prescribed details, including the names of parties, nature of information, evidence collected, and whether the accused should be prosecuted.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the filing of the police final report or charge sheet after completion of an investigation.
examples: Police file a charge sheet before the Magistrate after completing a murder investigation.

faqs.question: What is a charge sheet?
faqs.answer: It is the final report submitted by police after completing an investigation and finding sufficient evidence.

title: Police to inquire and report on suicide, etc.
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a police officer receives information that a person has committed suicide, has been killed by another, has died under suspicious circumstances, or has died in circumstances requiring investigation, the officer shall inform the Executive Magistrate and conduct an inquest according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires police to investigate suspicious, unnatural or unexplained deaths through an inquest procedure.
examples: Police conduct an inquest after a person is found dead under suspicious circumstances.

faqs.question: What is an inquest?
faqs.answer: It is a preliminary inquiry into the apparent cause of an unnatural or suspicious death.

title: Power to summon persons
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A police officer conducting proceedings under section 174 may summon and examine persons acquainted with the facts of the case and require them to provide relevant information concerning the death or occurrence under inquiry.
simpleExplanation: This section gives police authority to question persons who may have information about a suspicious or unnatural death.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract consequences provided by law.
examples: Neighbours are summoned and questioned regarding the circumstances surrounding a suspicious death.

faqs.question: Can police summon people during an inquest investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Persons with relevant information may be required to provide evidence.

title: Inquiry by Magistrate into cause of death
chapter: Chapter XII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a person dies, disappears, or is alleged to have been subjected to rape while in police custody or other custody authorized by law, or when circumstances otherwise require, the nearest Judicial Magistrate or Metropolitan Magistrate shall hold an inquiry into the cause of death or the circumstances of the occurrence.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for an independent judicial inquiry into custodial deaths, suspicious deaths, and certain other serious incidents.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate conducts an inquiry after a death occurs in police custody.

faqs.question: Why is a Magistrate's inquiry required in custodial death cases?
faqs.answer: To ensure an independent and impartial investigation into the circumstances of the death.

title: Ordinary place of inquiry and trial
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Every offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a Court within whose local jurisdiction it was committed.
simpleExplanation: This section lays down the general rule that a criminal case should be investigated and tried in the area where the offence occurred.
examples: A theft committed in Bengaluru is ordinarily tried by a court having jurisdiction over Bengaluru.

faqs.question: Which court usually tries a criminal case?
faqs.answer: The court located in the area where the offence was committed.

title: Place of inquiry or trial
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where it is uncertain in which local area an offence was committed, or where an offence is committed partly in one area and partly in another, or where it is a continuing offence, it may be inquired into or tried by a Court having jurisdiction over any of such areas.
simpleExplanation: This section provides flexibility when an offence spans multiple locations or when the exact location of the offence is uncertain.
examples: An online fraud affecting victims in multiple cities may be tried in any court having lawful jurisdiction.

faqs.question: What if a crime occurs in more than one place?
faqs.answer: Any court having jurisdiction over one of those places may try the case.

title: Offence triable where act is done or consequence ensues
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an act is an offence by reason of anything done and of a consequence that has ensued, the offence may be inquired into or tried by a Court within whose jurisdiction either the act was done or the consequence occurred.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a case to be tried either where the act occurred or where its harmful effect was felt.
examples: A defamatory message sent from one city and received in another may be tried in either location.

faqs.question: Can a case be tried where the harm occurred?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jurisdiction may exist where the consequence of the act occurred.

title: Place of trial where act is offence by reason of relation to other offence
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an act is an offence because of its connection with another offence, it may be tried by the Court having jurisdiction over either offence.
simpleExplanation: This section allows related offences to be tried in the same jurisdiction for convenience and consistency.
examples: A person accused of receiving stolen property may be tried in the jurisdiction where the theft occurred.
