faqs.question: Can related offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jurisdiction may be based on the connection between the offences.

title: Place of trial in case of certain offences
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Offences such as being a thug, murder committed during a journey, dacoity, kidnapping, abduction, theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, receiving stolen property and certain other offences may be inquired into or tried by a Court having jurisdiction as specified under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section provides special jurisdictional rules for certain offences that may involve multiple locations or special circumstances.
examples: A theft committed in one city and recovery of stolen property in another may be tried in either jurisdiction as permitted by law.

faqs.question: Why are special jurisdiction rules needed for some offences?
faqs.answer: Because some crimes involve multiple places, victims or stages of criminal conduct.

title: Offences committed by means of letters, electronic communications, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an offence is committed through letters, electronic communications, telecommunications or similar means, it may be inquired into or tried by a Court having jurisdiction over the place where the communication was sent, received or where its consequences occurred, as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section helps determine jurisdiction for offences committed through communication systems, including electronic communications.
examples: A fraudulent email sent from one State and received in another may be tried in either location.

faqs.question: Where can a cyber-related offence be tried?
faqs.answer: It may be tried where the communication originated, was received, or caused harm.

title: Offence committed on journey or voyage
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an offence is committed while a person is travelling on a journey or voyage, the offence may be inquired into or tried by a Court through or into whose local jurisdiction the person or the vehicle, vessel or aircraft passed during the journey.
simpleExplanation: This section provides jurisdictional rules for offences committed while travelling.
examples: A theft committed on a train journey may be tried by a court in any jurisdiction through which the train travelled.

faqs.question: Which court handles offences committed during travel?
faqs.answer: Any court having jurisdiction over a place through which the journey passed may try the case.

title: Place of trial for offences triable together
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When several offences are committed by the same person and may be charged and tried together under the Code, they may be inquired into or tried by any Court competent to try any one of those offences.
simpleExplanation: This section allows related offences to be tried together in a single court for convenience and judicial efficiency.
examples: A person accused of forgery and cheating arising from the same transaction may face trial for both offences in the same court.

faqs.question: Can multiple related offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes. A competent court may try all such offences together.

title: Power to order cases to be tried in different sessions divisions
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The State Government may direct that any case or class of cases committed for trial in one sessions division be tried in another sessions division when considered necessary in the interests of justice or administrative convenience.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the transfer of cases between sessions divisions when required.
examples: A sensitive criminal case is transferred to another sessions division to ensure a fair trial.

faqs.question: Can a criminal case be transferred to another sessions division?
faqs.answer: Yes. The State Government may order such transfer in appropriate circumstances.

title: High Court to decide, in case of doubt, district where inquiry or trial shall take place
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When two or more Courts have taken cognizance of the same offence and a question arises regarding which Court should conduct the inquiry or trial, the High Court shall decide the appropriate Court having jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the High Court to resolve jurisdictional disputes between criminal courts.
examples: Two courts claim jurisdiction over the same cheating case, and the High Court decides which court will proceed.

faqs.question: Who resolves disputes about which court should try a case?
faqs.answer: The High Court decides when there is uncertainty or conflict regarding jurisdiction.

title: Power to issue summons or warrant for offence committed beyond local jurisdiction
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When a Magistrate receives information regarding an offence committed outside his local jurisdiction but is competent to take cognizance of the offence, he may issue a summons or warrant and forward the accused to the appropriate Court having jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to take necessary preliminary action even when the offence occurred outside his territorial jurisdiction.
examples: A Magistrate issues a warrant against an accused located within his area for an offence committed elsewhere.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate act if the offence occurred elsewhere?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain preliminary actions may be taken before transferring the matter to the proper court.

title: Offence committed outside India
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an offence is committed outside India by an Indian citizen, by a person on an Indian-registered ship or aircraft, or in circumstances where Indian law applies, the offender may be dealt with as if the offence had been committed within India, subject to any sanction required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section permits Indian courts to deal with certain offences committed outside India when Indian law applies.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence involved.
examples: An Indian citizen commits an offence abroad and is later prosecuted in India according to law.

faqs.question: Can Indian courts try offences committed outside India?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain offences committed abroad may be prosecuted in India.

title: Receipt of evidence relating to offences committed outside India
chapter: Chapter XIII - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an offence committed outside India is being investigated or tried in India, evidence collected abroad and duly authenticated may be received in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows Indian courts to use evidence obtained from foreign countries in appropriate cases.
examples: Bank records obtained from a foreign country are produced during a criminal trial in India.

faqs.question: Can evidence collected abroad be used in Indian courts?
faqs.answer: Yes. Properly authenticated foreign evidence may be admitted according to law.

title: Cognizance of offences by Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Subject to the provisions of the Code, any Magistrate of the First Class and any Magistrate of the Second Class specially empowered may take cognizance of an offence upon receiving a complaint, upon a police report, or upon information received from any person other than a police officer, or upon the Magistrate's own knowledge.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a Magistrate formally takes notice of a criminal offence and starts judicial proceedings.
examples: A Magistrate takes cognizance after receiving a police charge sheet in a cheating case.
A Magistrate takes cognizance based on a private complaint filed by a citizen.

faqs.question: What does 'taking cognizance' mean?
faqs.answer: It means the Magistrate officially recognizes an alleged offence and begins judicial proceedings.

title: Transfer on application of accused
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: When a Magistrate takes cognizance of an offence upon information received otherwise than on a police report or upon his own knowledge, the accused shall be informed of his right to have the case tried by another Magistrate, and if the accused objects, the case shall be transferred according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the accused by allowing transfer of a case when the Magistrate has initiated proceedings based on personal knowledge or private information.
examples: A Magistrate starts proceedings based on personal knowledge, and the accused requests transfer to another Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can an accused seek transfer of a case from a Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Yes, in the circumstances specified by this section.

title: Making over of cases to Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Any Chief Judicial Magistrate may, after taking cognizance of an offence, make over the case for inquiry or trial to any competent Magistrate subordinate to him. A Magistrate receiving such a case may proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows senior Magistrates to assign criminal cases to other competent Magistrates.
examples: A Chief Judicial Magistrate assigns a criminal complaint to another Magistrate for trial.

faqs.question: Can one Magistrate transfer a case to another Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Yes. Senior Magistrates may assign cases to subordinate Magistrates.

title: Cognizance of offences by Courts of Session
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Except as otherwise expressly provided by law, a Court of Session shall not take cognizance of any offence as a court of original jurisdiction unless the case has been committed to it by a Magistrate under the provisions of the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section generally requires cases to be committed by a Magistrate before a Sessions Court can conduct the trial.
examples: A murder case is first committed by a Magistrate before being tried by the Sessions Court.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Court directly start a criminal trial?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The case must first be committed by a Magistrate unless the law provides otherwise.

title: Additional and Assistant Sessions Judges to try cases made over to them
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: An Additional Sessions Judge or Assistant Sessions Judge shall try only such cases as are made over to him by the Sessions Judge in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that Additional and Assistant Sessions Judges hear cases assigned to them by the Sessions Judge.
examples: The Sessions Judge assigns a criminal appeal to an Additional Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: How do Additional Sessions Judges receive cases?
faqs.answer: Cases are assigned to them by the Sessions Judge.

title: Prosecution for contempt of lawful authority of public servants, offences against public justice and offences relating to documents given in evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No Court shall take cognizance of specified offences relating to contempt of lawful authority of public servants, offences against public justice, or offences concerning documents produced in court, except upon a complaint in writing by the public servant concerned, the Court concerned, or another authority authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section restricts prosecution for certain offences unless initiated by the affected public authority or court.
examples: A prosecution for giving false evidence in court may require a complaint from the concerned court.

faqs.question: Why is special permission required under this section?
faqs.answer: To prevent misuse of criminal proceedings in matters affecting public administration and judicial processes.

title: Procedure for witnesses in case of threatening, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: A witness or any person may file a complaint before the Court if he is threatened, coerced, induced or otherwise influenced to give false evidence. The Court may conduct an inquiry and take appropriate action according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects witnesses from intimidation and allows courts to act when someone tries to influence testimony improperly.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence established under the applicable penal law.
examples: A witness reports that he has been threatened to change his testimony in a criminal trial.

faqs.question: What should a witness do if threatened?
faqs.answer: The witness may approach the Court and seek protection or appropriate legal action.

title: Prosecution for offences against the State and for criminal conspiracy
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No Court shall take cognizance of certain offences against the State, offences affecting national security, or specified criminal conspiracies except with the previous sanction of the Central Government, State Government or other competent authority as required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires prior governmental approval before courts can take cognizance of certain serious offences affecting the State.
examples: A prosecution involving certain offences against the State proceeds only after obtaining the required sanction.

faqs.question: Why is government sanction required?
faqs.answer: Because the offences involve matters of public importance, security or governmental interests.

title: Prosecution of Judges and public servants
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: When a Judge, Magistrate or public servant is accused of an offence alleged to have been committed while acting or purporting to act in the discharge of official duties, no Court shall take cognizance without the previous sanction of the competent Government, where such sanction is required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects public officials from prosecution for acts connected with their official duties unless prior sanction is obtained.
examples: Sanction may be required before prosecuting a public servant for an act performed during official duties.

faqs.question: Does every case against a public servant require sanction?
faqs.answer: No. Sanction is generally required only when the alleged act is connected with official duties.

title: Prosecution for offences against marriage
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No Court shall take cognizance of offences relating to marriage except upon a complaint made by a person aggrieved by the offence or by another person authorized by law in the circumstances specified by the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section limits who can initiate criminal proceedings for certain offences relating to marriage.
examples: A spouse files a complaint regarding an offence relating to marriage.

faqs.question: Can anyone file a complaint for a marriage-related offence?
faqs.answer: No. Only the aggrieved person or an authorized person may generally do so.

title: Prosecution for defamation
chapter: Chapter XIV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No Court shall take cognizance of an offence of defamation except upon a complaint made by the person aggrieved by the offence. Special provisions apply where the alleged victim is a public servant or holds certain public offices.
simpleExplanation: This section provides that defamation cases must generally be initiated by the person whose reputation is alleged to have been harmed.
punishment: Punishment depends upon the applicable provisions of the penal law governing defamation.
examples: A person whose reputation is harmed by false allegations files a complaint for defamation.

faqs.question: Can someone else file a defamation case on my behalf?
faqs.answer: Generally, the complaint must be made by the person aggrieved, subject to certain exceptions.

title: Examination of complainant
chapter: Chapter XV - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: A Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence on complaint shall examine upon oath the complainant and the witnesses present, if any, and the substance of such examination shall be reduced to writing and signed by the complainant, witnesses and the Magistrate, except in cases where examination is dispensed with by law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to record the statement of the complainant and witnesses before proceeding with a private criminal complaint.
examples: A citizen files a private complaint for cheating, and the Magistrate records the complainant's sworn statement.

faqs.question: What happens after a private complaint is filed?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate usually examines the complainant and any available witnesses on oath.

title: Procedure by Magistrate not competent to take cognizance of the case
chapter: Chapter XV - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: If a complaint is made to a Magistrate who is not competent to take cognizance of the offence, the Magistrate shall return the complaint for presentation to the proper Court or direct the complainant to the appropriate Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section explains what happens when a complaint is filed before the wrong Magistrate.
examples: A complaint is returned because another Magistrate has the proper territorial jurisdiction.

faqs.question: What if I file a complaint in the wrong court?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may return it or direct you to the competent court.

title: Postponement of issue of process
chapter: Chapter XV - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: A Magistrate may postpone the issuance of process against the accused and either inquire into the case himself or direct an investigation by a police officer or other person to determine whether sufficient grounds exist for proceeding.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to verify the complaint before summoning the accused.
examples: The Magistrate orders a preliminary police inquiry before issuing summons in a complaint case.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate investigate a complaint before issuing summons?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may conduct an inquiry or order an investigation.

title: Dismissal of complaint
chapter: Chapter XV - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: If, after considering the statements on oath and the result of any inquiry or investigation under section 202, the Magistrate finds no sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall dismiss the complaint and briefly record the reasons for doing so.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to reject a complaint when there is insufficient material to proceed.
examples: A complaint lacking evidence is dismissed after inquiry.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate reject a complaint?
faqs.answer: Yes. A complaint may be dismissed if there are no sufficient grounds for proceeding.

title: Issue of process
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: If, in the opinion of the Magistrate taking cognizance, there is sufficient ground for proceeding, the Magistrate shall issue process against the accused, either by summons or warrant, depending on the nature of the case and the applicable law.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the Magistrate to formally begin proceedings against an accused person by issuing summons or a warrant.
examples: The Magistrate issues summons after finding sufficient evidence in a complaint case.

faqs.question: When does a criminal case formally begin against an accused?
faqs.answer: When the Magistrate issues process under this section.

title: Magistrate may dispense with personal attendance of accused
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: Whenever a Magistrate issues a summons, he may, if he sees reason to do so, dispense with the personal attendance of the accused and permit the accused to appear through a pleader, subject to such conditions as the Magistrate thinks fit.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to excuse the accused from personally appearing in court and permit representation through a lawyer.
examples: An accused residing in another city is permitted to appear through an advocate.

faqs.question: Must an accused always appear personally in court?
faqs.answer: No. The Magistrate may allow appearance through a lawyer in appropriate cases.

title: Special summons in cases of petty offence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: In cases involving petty offences, the Magistrate may issue a special summons allowing the accused to plead guilty by post or through an authorized representative and pay the specified fine without personally appearing before the Court.
simpleExplanation: This section simplifies court procedures for minor offences by allowing payment of fines without attending court.
examples: An accused in a minor traffic-related case pays the fine without appearing personally in court.

faqs.question: Can petty cases be settled without attending court?
faqs.answer: Yes. In eligible cases, the accused may plead guilty and pay the fine through the procedure provided.

title: Supply to the accused of copy of police report and other documents
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: In cases instituted on a police report, the Magistrate shall, without delay and free of cost, furnish to the accused copies of the police report, first information report, witness statements, confessions, and other relevant documents as required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that the accused receives copies of important case documents to prepare a proper defence.
examples: An accused receives copies of the FIR, charge sheet and witness statements before trial.

faqs.question: Does the accused have a right to receive the charge sheet and related documents?
faqs.answer: Yes. These documents must generally be supplied free of cost.

title: Supply of copies of statements and documents to accused in other cases
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: In cases instituted otherwise than on a police report and triable by a Court of Session, the Magistrate shall furnish to the accused copies of statements, confessions and other documents relied upon by the prosecution.
simpleExplanation: This section provides similar document-supply rights to accused persons in certain complaint cases.
examples: An accused in a Sessions-triable complaint case receives copies of prosecution documents.

faqs.question: Do document disclosure rules apply only to police cases?
faqs.answer: No. Similar protections exist in specified complaint cases.

title: Commitment of case to Court of Session when offence is triable exclusively by it
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: When it appears to the Magistrate that an offence is exclusively triable by a Court of Session, the Magistrate shall commit the case to the Court of Session after complying with the requirements of the Code and remand the accused if necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section requires serious cases, such as murder and certain major offences, to be transferred from the Magistrate to the Sessions Court for trial.
examples: A murder case is committed by the Magistrate to the Sessions Court for trial.

faqs.question: Why are some cases sent to the Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: Because the law requires certain serious offences to be tried only by a Sessions Court.

title: Procedure to be followed when there is a complaint case and police investigation in respect of the same offence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: When a complaint case is pending before a Magistrate and it appears that a police investigation is in progress in relation to the same offence, the Magistrate shall stay the complaint proceedings and call for a report from the investigating police officer.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents parallel proceedings by coordinating private complaints and police investigations relating to the same offence.
examples: A private complaint for cheating is stayed when police are already investigating the same allegations.

faqs.question: What happens if both a complaint case and police investigation exist for the same offence?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate generally pauses the complaint case and seeks a police report.

title: Contents of charge
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: Every charge shall state the offence with which the accused is charged. If the law creating the offence gives it a specific name, that name shall be used. The charge shall also contain particulars necessary to give the accused notice of the matter with which he is charged.
simpleExplanation: This section explains what information must be included in a criminal charge so that the accused clearly understands the allegations.
examples: A charge for theft must clearly specify the offence and relevant details.

faqs.question: Why is a charge important?
faqs.answer: It informs the accused of the exact allegations and helps ensure a fair trial.

title: Particulars as to time, place and person
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: The charge shall contain such particulars regarding the time, place and person involved in the alleged offence as are reasonably sufficient to give the accused notice of the matter charged.
simpleExplanation: This section requires a charge to include key details such as when, where and against whom the offence was allegedly committed.
examples: A charge for assault mentions the date, location and identity of the victim.

faqs.question: Must a charge contain details of the alleged offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Essential particulars must be included to inform the accused.

title: When manner of committing offence must be stated
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: When the nature of the case is such that the particulars mentioned in sections 211 and 212 do not sufficiently inform the accused of the matter charged, the charge shall also contain particulars regarding the manner in which the offence was committed.
simpleExplanation: This section requires additional details in a charge whenever basic information is insufficient to explain the accusation.
examples: A complex fraud charge includes details of the method used to deceive victims.

faqs.question: When are additional details required in a charge?
faqs.answer: When the basic description does not adequately explain the allegations.

title: Words in charge taken in sense of law under which offence is punishable
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: Words used in a charge describing an offence shall be deemed to have been used in the sense attached to them by the law under which the offence is punishable.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that legal terms used in a charge carry their legal meaning as defined in the relevant criminal law.
examples: The term 'theft' in a charge carries the legal meaning assigned to it by criminal law.

faqs.question: How are legal terms in a charge interpreted?
faqs.answer: They are interpreted according to their meaning under the relevant law.

title: Effect of errors
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: No error in stating either the offence or the particulars required to be stated in the charge shall be regarded as material unless the accused was in fact misled by such error and it occasioned a failure of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section states that minor mistakes in a charge do not automatically invalidate proceedings unless they prejudice the accused.
examples: A minor clerical error in the date mentioned in a charge does not invalidate the trial if the accused understands the allegation.

faqs.question: Can a trial fail because of a small mistake in the charge?
faqs.answer: Not usually. The mistake must have caused prejudice or a failure of justice.

title: Court may alter charge
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: Any Court may alter or add to any charge at any time before judgment is pronounced. Every such alteration or addition shall be read and explained to the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to correct, modify or add charges before the final judgment if required by the evidence.
examples: During trial, the Court adds an additional charge after new evidence emerges.

faqs.question: Can charges be changed after a trial starts?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may alter or add charges before delivering judgment.

title: Recall of witnesses when charge altered
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: Whenever a charge is altered or added by the Court, the prosecutor and the accused shall be allowed to recall or re-examine witnesses with reference to the altered or added charge, unless the Court considers such recall unnecessary.
simpleExplanation: This section protects fairness by allowing both sides to address the changed charge through additional questioning of witnesses.
examples: A witness is recalled after a new charge is added during trial.

faqs.question: Can witnesses be recalled after a charge is changed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both prosecution and defence may seek further examination of witnesses.

title: Separate charges for distinct offences
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: For every distinct offence of which a person is accused, there shall be a separate charge and every such charge shall be tried separately, except in cases otherwise provided by the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the general rule that different offences should be charged and tried separately.
examples: Separate charges are framed for theft and assault committed on different occasions.

faqs.question: Must each offence have a separate charge?
faqs.answer: Yes. Separate charges are the general rule unless an exception applies.

title: Three offences of same kind within year may be charged together
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: When a person is accused of more offences than one of the same kind committed within a period of twelve months from the first to the last of such offences, he may be charged with and tried at one trial for any number of them not exceeding three.
simpleExplanation: This section allows up to three similar offences committed within one year to be tried together.
examples: Three thefts committed by the same accused within one year are tried together.

faqs.question: Can multiple similar offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to three offences of the same kind committed within one year may be joined.

title: Trial for more than one offence
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: If a series of acts are so connected together as to form the same transaction, more offences than one committed by the same person may be charged and tried together at one trial. Offences arising from the same transaction may be jointly tried in accordance with the provisions of the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows multiple offences arising from the same incident or transaction to be tried together.
examples: An accused commits house-breaking and theft during the same incident, and both offences are tried together.

faqs.question: Can different offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes, when they form part of the same transaction.

title: Where it is doubtful what offence has been committed
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: If a single act or series of acts is of such a nature that it is doubtful which of several offences the facts will constitute, the accused may be charged with all or any of such offences, or charged in the alternative.
simpleExplanation: This section allows alternative charges when the exact legal nature of the offence is uncertain at the beginning of the trial.
examples: An accused may be charged alternatively with theft or criminal breach of trust when the facts are unclear.

faqs.question: What is an alternative charge?
faqs.answer: It is a charge framed when the exact offence cannot be determined initially.

title: When offence proved included in offence charged
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: When a person is charged with an offence consisting of several particulars and only some of those particulars are proved, he may be convicted of the minor offence constituted by the proved facts. A person charged with a major offence may also be convicted of a minor offence included within it, if the facts justify such conviction.
simpleExplanation: This section allows conviction for a lesser offence even if the major offence charged is not fully proved.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence ultimately proved.
examples: An accused charged with robbery may be convicted of theft if robbery is not fully established.

faqs.question: Can someone be convicted of a lesser offence than the one charged?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the evidence proves the lesser offence.

title: What persons may be charged jointly
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: Persons accused of the same offence committed in the course of the same transaction, persons accused of an offence and those accused of abetment or attempt, and certain other categories specified by law may be charged and tried together.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies when multiple accused persons can be tried together in one proceeding.
examples: Several accused involved in the same robbery are jointly tried.

faqs.question: Can multiple accused be tried in one case?
faqs.answer: Yes, when they fall within the categories specified by this section.

title: Withdrawal of remaining charges on conviction on one of several charges
chapter: Chapter XVII - The Charge
officialText: When a conviction has been obtained on one or more charges and the Court considers that the remaining charges need not be pursued, it may stay or withdraw proceedings on those charges with the consent required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to avoid unnecessary trials on additional charges after a conviction has already been secured.
examples: After conviction on the principal charge, the prosecution withdraws lesser connected charges.

faqs.question: Can some charges be dropped after conviction on another charge?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Court considers further proceedings unnecessary.

title: Trial to be conducted by Public Prosecutor
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: In every trial before a Court of Session, the prosecution shall be conducted by a Public Prosecutor.
simpleExplanation: This section requires that prosecutions in Sessions Court be handled by a Public Prosecutor representing the State.
examples: A murder trial before the Sessions Court is conducted by the Public Prosecutor.

faqs.question: Who conducts the prosecution in a Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: The Public Prosecutor conducts the prosecution on behalf of the State.

title: Opening case for prosecution
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: When the accused appears or is brought before the Court in pursuance of a commitment, the Public Prosecutor shall open the case by describing the charge and stating the evidence proposed to prove the guilt of the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the prosecutor to explain the allegations and summarize the evidence at the beginning of the Sessions trial.
examples: The Public Prosecutor outlines the prosecution's case at the start of a murder trial.

faqs.question: What happens at the start of a Sessions trial?
faqs.answer: The prosecutor explains the charges and the evidence against the accused.

title: Discharge
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If, upon consideration of the record of the case and the documents submitted, and after hearing the submissions of the accused and the prosecution, the Judge considers that there is not sufficient ground for proceeding against the accused, the Judge shall discharge the accused and record the reasons.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Sessions Judge to release the accused before trial if the evidence is insufficient.
examples: The Sessions Court discharges an accused because the prosecution materials do not disclose a prima facie case.

faqs.question: What is a discharge order?
faqs.answer: It is an order ending the case before trial due to insufficient grounds to proceed.

title: Framing of charge
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If, after consideration of the record and hearing the parties, the Judge is of the opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence, the Judge shall frame a charge in writing and proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Court to formally frame charges when there is sufficient material to proceed to trial.
examples: The Sessions Judge frames charges of murder after finding sufficient grounds to proceed.

faqs.question: When does the Court frame charges?
faqs.answer: When there is enough material to presume that the accused committed the offence.

title: Conviction on plea of guilty
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Judge shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict the accused on that plea.
simpleExplanation: This section permits a Sessions Court to convict an accused who voluntarily pleads guilty.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence for which the guilty plea is entered.
examples: An accused voluntarily pleads guilty, and the Court records the plea before passing judgment.

faqs.question: Can a person be convicted solely on a guilty plea?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may convict based on a voluntary plea of guilty.

title: Date for prosecution evidence
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the accused refuses to plead, does not plead, or claims to be tried, the Judge shall fix a date for the examination of prosecution witnesses and may issue process for compelling their attendance or the production of documents.
simpleExplanation: This section begins the evidence stage of a Sessions trial by setting a date for prosecution witnesses to testify.
examples: After pleading not guilty, the accused faces trial and the Court schedules prosecution evidence.

faqs.question: What happens after an accused pleads not guilty?
faqs.answer: The Court fixes a date for prosecution evidence and witness examination.

title: Evidence for prosecution
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: On the date fixed for examination of witnesses, the Judge shall take all evidence produced in support of the prosecution. The Judge may permit cross-examination and may defer it where permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the presentation of evidence by the prosecution during a Sessions trial.
examples: Eyewitnesses and investigating officers testify for the prosecution during trial.

faqs.question: When is prosecution evidence presented?
faqs.answer: After charges are framed and the case proceeds to trial.

title: Acquittal
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If, after considering the evidence for the prosecution and hearing the parties, the Judge finds that there is no evidence that the accused committed the offence, the Judge shall record an order of acquittal.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to acquit the accused if the prosecution fails to produce evidence linking the accused to the offence.
examples: The accused is acquitted because no evidence connects him with the alleged crime.

faqs.question: Can the Court acquit the accused before the defence presents evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. If there is no evidence against the accused, an acquittal may be recorded.

title: Entering upon defence
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the accused is not acquitted under section 232, he shall be called upon to enter upon his defence and produce any evidence he may have in support of his case.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the accused an opportunity to present defence witnesses and documents after the prosecution has completed its case.
examples: The accused produces alibi witnesses to support his defence.

faqs.question: Does the accused have the right to present evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused may produce witnesses and documents in defence.

title: Arguments
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: When the examination of witnesses is complete, the prosecutor shall sum up the case and the accused or his advocate shall be entitled to reply. The Court shall thereafter proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for final arguments by both sides before the Court decides the case.
examples: Both prosecution and defence present closing arguments after all evidence has been recorded.

faqs.question: When are final arguments heard?
faqs.answer: After the evidence stage of the trial is completed.

title: Judgment of acquittal or conviction
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: After hearing arguments and considering the evidence, the Judge shall give a judgment in the case. If the accused is convicted, the Judge shall hear the accused on the question of sentence before passing sentence according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Court to deliver its judgment and, in case of conviction, hear the accused before deciding the punishment.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence for which the accused is convicted.
examples: After finding the accused guilty, the Court hears submissions regarding an appropriate sentence.

faqs.question: Does the accused get an opportunity to speak before sentencing?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court must hear the accused on the question of sentence.

title: Previous conviction
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: When a previous conviction is charged and the accused does not admit it, the Judge may take evidence regarding the previous conviction after conviction for the current offence and before passing sentence.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with proving previous convictions that may affect sentencing.
examples: The prosecution produces records of a prior conviction to seek enhanced punishment.

faqs.question: Why are previous convictions important?
faqs.answer: They may influence the sentence imposed by the Court.

title: Procedure in cases instituted under section 199(2)
chapter: Chapter XIX - Special Procedure for Certain Defamation Cases
officialText: Cases instituted upon complaints for defamation under the special provisions relating to certain public servants shall be tried in accordance with the procedure prescribed under this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the procedure for certain special defamation cases involving public officials.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence of defamation proved.
examples: A defamation complaint concerning a public servant proceeds under the special procedure provided by law.

faqs.question: Why are some defamation cases treated differently?
faqs.answer: Special procedures apply where the law provides additional safeguards for specified public officials.

title: Compliance with section 207
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When, in any warrant case instituted on a police report, the accused appears or is brought before a Magistrate, the Magistrate shall satisfy himself that the provisions relating to the supply of police reports and documents to the accused have been complied with.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that the accused receives all necessary documents before the trial begins.
examples: The Magistrate confirms that the accused has received copies of the FIR and charge sheet.

faqs.question: Why must documents be supplied before trial?
faqs.answer: To allow the accused to prepare an effective defence.

title: When accused shall be discharged
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If, upon considering the police report and accompanying documents and after hearing the accused and the prosecution, the Magistrate considers the charge against the accused to be groundless, the Magistrate shall discharge the accused and record the reasons.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to end a warrant case before trial if there is no sufficient basis for proceeding.
examples: The accused is discharged after the Magistrate finds that the evidence does not support the allegations.

faqs.question: What is discharge in a warrant case?
faqs.answer: It is the termination of proceedings because the allegations lack sufficient foundation.

title: Framing of charge
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If, upon consideration of the police report, documents and submissions of the parties, the Magistrate is of the opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence triable as a warrant case, the Magistrate shall frame a written charge and read and explain it to the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to formally frame charges when sufficient material exists to proceed with the trial.
examples: The Magistrate frames a charge of cheating after finding a prima facie case from the police report.

faqs.question: When is a charge framed in a warrant case?
faqs.answer: When the Magistrate finds sufficient grounds to presume the accused committed the offence.

title: Conviction on plea of guilty
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict the accused on that plea.
simpleExplanation: This section permits conviction based on a voluntary plea of guilty by the accused.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence involved.
examples: An accused voluntarily admits guilt after the charge is explained.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate convict solely on a guilty plea?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may convict if satisfied that the plea is voluntary.

title: Evidence for prosecution
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused refuses to plead, does not plead, or claims to be tried, the Magistrate shall fix a date for examination of prosecution witnesses and take all evidence produced in support of the prosecution.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the prosecution evidence stage in warrant cases tried by Magistrates.
examples: Witnesses testify and documents are produced by the prosecution during trial.

faqs.question: What happens after the accused pleads not guilty?
faqs.answer: The prosecution presents its evidence before the Court.

title: Evidence for defence
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After the prosecution evidence is completed, the accused shall be called upon to enter upon his defence and may produce witnesses, documents and other evidence in support of his case.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the accused the opportunity to present evidence in defence.
examples: The accused calls witnesses to prove that he was elsewhere when the offence occurred.

faqs.question: Can the accused produce witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused has the right to present defence evidence.

title: Evidence for prosecution in warrant cases instituted otherwise than on police report
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: In a warrant case instituted otherwise than on a police report, when the accused appears or is brought before the Magistrate, the Magistrate shall hear the prosecution and take all evidence produced in support of the complaint.
simpleExplanation: This section applies to private complaint cases and requires the Magistrate to hear the complainant's evidence before deciding how to proceed.
examples: A complainant presents witnesses and documents in support of a private complaint for cheating.

faqs.question: How are complaint-based warrant cases handled?
faqs.answer: The complainant first presents evidence before the Magistrate.

title: When accused shall be discharged
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If, upon taking all the evidence referred to in section 244, the Magistrate considers that no case against the accused has been made out which, if unrebutted, would warrant conviction, the Magistrate shall discharge the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to discharge the accused if the complainant's evidence is insufficient to justify a conviction.
examples: A private complaint is dismissed after the Magistrate finds that the evidence is too weak to support a conviction.

faqs.question: Can an accused be discharged before charges are framed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may discharge the accused if the complainant's evidence is insufficient.

title: Procedure where accused is not discharged
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the Magistrate is of the opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence triable as a warrant case, the Magistrate shall frame a charge and proceed with the trial according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires charges to be framed when sufficient evidence exists in a complaint-based warrant case.
examples: The Magistrate frames a charge after finding sufficient evidence presented by the complainant.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused is not discharged?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate frames charges and the trial continues.

title: Evidence for prosecution
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After a charge is framed under section 246, the prosecution witnesses shall be recalled if necessary for cross-examination and further evidence may be taken in support of the prosecution case.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that prosecution witnesses can be questioned again after charges are framed.
examples: A witness who testified before the charge was framed is recalled for cross-examination.

faqs.question: Can witnesses be recalled after charges are framed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused has a right to cross-examine prosecution witnesses.

title: Acquittal or conviction
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After considering all evidence and hearing the parties, the Magistrate shall either acquit or convict the accused. If convicted, the accused shall be heard on the question of sentence before punishment is imposed.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the final judgment in warrant cases tried by Magistrates.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved.
examples: After completing the trial, the Magistrate convicts the accused and hears arguments regarding sentencing.

faqs.question: What happens after the trial is completed?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate delivers judgment and, if necessary, determines the sentence.

title: Absence of complainant
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When proceedings have been instituted upon a complaint and the complainant is absent on a day fixed for hearing, the Magistrate may discharge the accused unless there is reason to adjourn the hearing or proceed in another manner permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to dismiss a complaint and discharge the accused if the complainant does not appear.
examples: A complaint case is dismissed because the complainant repeatedly fails to attend court.

faqs.question: What happens if the complainant does not appear?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may discharge the accused, unless there is sufficient reason to continue the proceedings.

title: Compensation for accusation without reasonable cause
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If a Magistrate acquits or discharges an accused and finds that there was no reasonable ground for making the accusation, the Magistrate may call upon the complainant or informant to show cause why compensation should not be awarded to the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section protects people from false or baseless criminal accusations by allowing compensation to be awarded in appropriate cases.
punishment: Compensation may be ordered against the complainant or informant.
examples: A person files a knowingly baseless complaint, and the Court orders compensation to the accused after acquittal.

faqs.question: Can an accused receive compensation after a false case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may award compensation if the accusation lacked reasonable grounds.

title: Substance of accusation to be stated
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When the accused appears or is brought before the Magistrate in a summons case, the particulars of the offence shall be stated to him, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty or has any defence to make.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to explain the accusation to the accused in a summons case.
examples: The Magistrate explains a minor offence charge and asks the accused whether he admits guilt.

faqs.question: Is a formal charge framed in every summons case?
faqs.answer: No. The substance of the accusation is usually explained instead.

title: Conviction on plea of guilty
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict the accused on that plea.
simpleExplanation: This section allows conviction when the accused voluntarily admits guilt in a summons case.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence involved.
examples: An accused admits guilt in a minor criminal case and is convicted accordingly.

faqs.question: Can a guilty plea lead directly to conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may convict on the basis of the plea.

title: Conviction on plea of guilty in absence of accused in petty cases
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: In petty cases where a special summons has been issued, the accused may plead guilty by post or through an authorized representative and remit the fine specified, and the Magistrate may convict accordingly.
simpleExplanation: This section allows simple disposal of petty cases without requiring personal appearance in court.
examples: A person charged with a minor offence pays the fine and pleads guilty without attending court.

faqs.question: Can petty cases be resolved without appearing in court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain petty cases may be disposed of through a written guilty plea and payment of fine.

title: Procedure when not convicted
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the Magistrate does not convict the accused under sections relating to a guilty plea, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution, take evidence and hear the accused before deciding the case.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the normal trial procedure in a summons case when the accused does not plead guilty.
examples: An accused denies the allegations, and the Court proceeds with witness examination and trial.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused pleads not guilty?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate conducts a regular trial by recording evidence and hearing both sides.

title: Acquittal or conviction
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After taking the evidence and hearing the parties in a summons case, the Magistrate shall either acquit or convict the accused according to law. If convicted, the Magistrate shall pass an appropriate sentence.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the Magistrate to deliver the final judgment after completing the trial of a summons case.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved.
examples: After hearing witnesses and examining evidence, the Magistrate convicts the accused and imposes a fine.

faqs.question: What happens after the trial in a summons case?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate delivers a judgment of acquittal or conviction.

title: Non-appearance or death of complainant
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the complainant does not appear on the day fixed for hearing, or any subsequent day, the Magistrate shall ordinarily acquit the accused unless there is a valid reason to adjourn the hearing or proceed in another lawful manner. Similar provisions apply where the complainant dies.
simpleExplanation: This section protects an accused from indefinite proceedings when the complainant fails to pursue the case.
examples: A complaint case is dismissed after the complainant repeatedly fails to attend court.

faqs.question: Can the accused be acquitted if the complainant is absent?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may acquit the accused unless there is sufficient reason to continue the case.

title: Withdrawal of complaint
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: Before a final order is passed, the complainant may apply to withdraw the complaint. If the Magistrate is satisfied that there are sufficient grounds for permitting withdrawal, he may allow it and acquit the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section permits a complainant to withdraw a criminal complaint before judgment.
examples: The complainant settles the dispute and seeks permission to withdraw the complaint.

faqs.question: Can a complaint be withdrawn after filing?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may allow withdrawal if appropriate.

title: Power to stop proceedings in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: In summons cases instituted otherwise than upon complaint, a Magistrate of the First Class or another specially empowered Magistrate may stop proceedings at any stage without pronouncing judgment. Depending on the stage reached, the accused may be released or acquitted.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Magistrate to terminate certain summons proceedings when continuation is unnecessary or inappropriate.
examples: The Magistrate stops proceedings after determining that continuing the case serves no useful purpose.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate stop a summons case without a judgment?
faqs.answer: Yes. In specified circumstances, proceedings may be stopped under this section.

title: Power of Court to convert summons cases into warrant cases
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Summons Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When a summons case appears to the Magistrate to be of such a nature that it should be tried as a warrant case, the Magistrate may convert the proceedings and thereafter follow the procedure applicable to warrant cases.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to adopt the more detailed warrant-case procedure when the circumstances of the case require it.
examples: A seemingly simple case becomes more serious during trial, prompting the Magistrate to convert it into a warrant case.

faqs.question: Can a summons case become a warrant case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may convert it if the nature of the case justifies a more detailed procedure.

title: Power to try summarily
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: Certain Magistrates, including Chief Judicial Magistrates, Metropolitan Magistrates and specially empowered Magistrates, may try specified offences in a summary manner, subject to the limitations and conditions prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows minor criminal cases to be tried through a simplified and faster procedure called summary trial.
punishment: Punishment is subject to the limitations applicable to summary trials.
examples: A minor theft case is tried summarily to ensure speedy disposal.

faqs.question: What is a summary trial?
faqs.answer: It is a simplified procedure used for certain minor offences to achieve quicker justice.

title: Power of Magistrate of the second class to try summarily
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: The High Court may empower a Magistrate of the Second Class to try specified offences summarily within the limits prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section enables certain Second Class Magistrates to conduct summary trials when specifically authorized.
examples: A Second Class Magistrate conducts summary trials after receiving authorization from the High Court.

faqs.question: Can a Second Class Magistrate conduct summary trials?
faqs.answer: Yes, if specifically empowered by the High Court.

title: Procedure for summary trials
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: In summary trials, the procedure prescribed for summons cases shall generally be followed, subject to the special provisions relating to summary proceedings and sentencing limits.
simpleExplanation: This section explains that summary trials broadly follow summons-case procedures but in a simplified form.
punishment: Punishment is restricted according to the limits prescribed for summary trials.
examples: A Magistrate follows simplified summons-case procedures while conducting a summary trial.

faqs.question: Which procedure applies in a summary trial?
faqs.answer: Generally, the procedure for summons cases applies with certain modifications.

title: Record in summary trials
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: In every summary trial, the Magistrate shall maintain a record containing prescribed particulars including the serial number, date, offence, plea, finding and sentence.
simpleExplanation: This section requires basic details of summary trials to be formally recorded.
examples: The Magistrate records the offence, plea and final order in a summary trial register.

faqs.question: Must records be maintained in summary trials?
faqs.answer: Yes. Essential details of every summary trial must be recorded.

title: Judgment in cases tried summarily
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: In every case tried summarily where the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall record the substance of the evidence and a brief statement of the reasons for the finding.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Magistrate to briefly record the evidence and reasons for the decision in contested summary trials.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved.
examples: The Magistrate records a concise summary of witness testimony before delivering judgment.

faqs.question: Is a detailed judgment required in a summary trial?
faqs.answer: No. A brief record of evidence and reasons is generally sufficient.

title: Language of record and judgment
chapter: Chapter XXI - Summary Trials
officialText: Every record and judgment in summary trials shall be written in the language of the Court or in such language as directed by the State Government or the High Court in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the language in which records and judgments of summary trials must be prepared.
examples: A Magistrate records the judgment in the official language prescribed for that court.

faqs.question: In which language are summary trial judgments recorded?
faqs.answer: They are recorded in the language authorized for use by the Court.

title: Application of the Chapter
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The provisions relating to plea bargaining shall apply to specified offences and cases as provided by law, subject to the exclusions and conditions prescribed in this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when plea bargaining can be used and identifies the categories of cases where it is permitted.
examples: An accused charged with an eligible offence applies for plea bargaining under this Chapter.

faqs.question: Does plea bargaining apply to every criminal case?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only to eligible offences and is subject to statutory restrictions.

title: Application for plea bargaining
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: An accused may file an application for plea bargaining before the Court in the prescribed manner, accompanied by a statement confirming that the application is voluntary and made with full understanding of its consequences.
simpleExplanation: This section sets out the procedure for requesting plea bargaining before the Court.
examples: An accused submits a written plea bargaining application acknowledging the consequences.

faqs.question: Who can apply for plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: The accused person may apply, subject to eligibility requirements.

title: Guidelines for mutually satisfactory disposition
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Upon receiving an application for plea bargaining, the Court shall facilitate a mutually satisfactory disposition of the case involving the accused, the victim and the prosecution in accordance with the procedure established by law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the framework for discussions aimed at reaching a mutually acceptable resolution of the case.
examples: The Court arranges discussions between the accused and victim to reach a lawful settlement.

faqs.question: What is a mutually satisfactory disposition?
faqs.answer: It is a negotiated resolution accepted by the parties and approved by the Court.

title: Report of the mutually satisfactory disposition
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Where a mutually satisfactory disposition has been worked out, the Court shall prepare a report of such disposition, signed by the presiding officer and the participants as required by law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the agreed settlement reached during plea bargaining to be formally documented.
examples: The Court records the terms agreed between the accused, victim and prosecution.

faqs.question: Is the plea bargaining agreement recorded officially?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court prepares a formal report of the settlement.

title: Disposal of the case
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: After the mutually satisfactory disposition is recorded, the Court shall dispose of the case by awarding compensation to the victim, releasing the accused on probation where permitted, or imposing an appropriate sentence in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how the Court finally disposes of a case after a successful plea bargaining process.
punishment: Sentence is determined according to the plea bargaining provisions and applicable law.
examples: The Court accepts a plea bargain, awards compensation to the victim and imposes a reduced sentence.

faqs.question: What happens after a plea bargain is accepted?
faqs.answer: The Court disposes of the case and passes an appropriate order or sentence.

title: Judgment of the Court
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The Court shall deliver its judgment in terms of the mutually satisfactory disposition and the judgment shall be signed by the presiding officer of the Court.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Court to formally pronounce judgment based on the plea bargain settlement.
examples: The Court pronounces judgment incorporating the agreed terms of the plea bargain.

faqs.question: Does the Court still deliver a judgment after plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: Yes. A formal judgment is issued based on the settlement.

title: Finality of judgment
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The judgment delivered by the Court in a plea bargaining case shall be final and no appeal shall lie against such judgment except through constitutional or extraordinary remedies available under law.
simpleExplanation: This section gives finality to judgments delivered under the plea bargaining process.
examples: An accused generally cannot file a regular appeal against a plea bargaining judgment.

faqs.question: Can a plea bargaining judgment be appealed?
faqs.answer: Ordinarily no, except through limited legal remedies available under the Constitution or other laws.

title: Power of Court in plea bargaining
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: For the purpose of implementing the plea bargaining provisions, the Court shall have all powers necessary to discharge its functions and to pass orders authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section confirms the Court's authority to carry out the plea bargaining process effectively.
examples: The Court issues directions necessary for completing a plea bargaining settlement.

faqs.question: What powers does the Court have during plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: The Court may exercise all powers necessary to implement the plea bargaining provisions.

title: Period of detention undergone by accused to be set off against sentence
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The period of detention already undergone by the accused in connection with the case shall be set off against the sentence of imprisonment imposed under the plea bargaining judgment, in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that time already spent in custody is credited against the final sentence.
examples: An accused who spent three months in custody receives credit for that period against the final sentence.

faqs.question: Will time already spent in jail be counted toward the sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Prior detention is generally set off against the sentence imposed.

title: Savings
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall be in addition to and not in derogation of any other provisions of law in force, except to the extent specifically provided under the plea bargaining provisions.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that plea bargaining provisions supplement existing criminal procedure laws unless expressly stated otherwise.
examples: Other criminal procedure provisions continue to apply unless specifically modified by plea bargaining rules.

faqs.question: Does plea bargaining replace the rest of criminal procedure law?
faqs.answer: No. It operates alongside existing laws except where specific provisions apply.

title: Statements of accused not to be used
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Statements or facts stated by the accused in an application for plea bargaining shall not be used for any purpose other than the plea bargaining proceedings, except as otherwise provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects accused persons by ensuring that statements made during plea bargaining are generally not used against them elsewhere.
examples: Admissions made during failed plea bargaining negotiations cannot ordinarily be used in a later trial.

faqs.question: Can statements made during plea bargaining be used later in court?
faqs.answer: Generally no, except where specifically permitted by law.

title: Non-application of the Chapter
chapter: Chapter XXIA - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall not apply to specified categories of offences, including offences affecting the socio-economic condition of the country and offences committed against women or children below the age prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies offences for which plea bargaining is not available.
examples: Certain serious offences against children are excluded from plea bargaining.

faqs.question: Are all offences eligible for plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: No. Several categories of serious offences are specifically excluded.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: For the purposes of this Chapter, expressions relating to prisoners, detention, prisons and other connected terms shall have the meanings assigned to them under the Code and applicable prison laws.
simpleExplanation: This section provides definitions used in the Chapter dealing with the attendance of prisoners before courts.
examples: The term 'prisoner' is interpreted according to the legal definition provided under the Code.

faqs.question: Why are definitions included in this Chapter?
faqs.answer: To ensure uniform interpretation of legal terms relating to prisoners and detention.

title: Power to require attendance of prisoners
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: Whenever a Criminal Court considers that the presence of a prisoner is necessary for answering a charge, giving evidence, or for any other purpose connected with a proceeding, the Court may require the officer in charge of the prison to produce the prisoner before it.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to summon prisoners from jail when their presence is needed in a legal proceeding.
examples: A prisoner is brought before court to testify as a witness in another criminal case.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner be required to appear in court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may order production of prisoners when legally necessary.

title: Power of State Government to exclude certain persons from operation of section 267
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: The State Government may direct that a prisoner or class of prisoners shall not be removed from prison for appearance before a Court when such removal is considered inexpedient on grounds such as public safety, security, public order or other reasons specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the State Government to prevent certain prisoners from being brought to court when security or public safety concerns exist.
examples: A high-risk prisoner is not produced in person because of serious security concerns.

faqs.question: Can the Government stop a prisoner from being brought to court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain prisoners may be excluded from production for security or public-interest reasons.

title: Officer in charge of prison to abstain from carrying out order in certain contingencies
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: The officer in charge of a prison may refrain from producing a prisoner before a Court if circumstances specified by law exist, including serious illness of the prisoner, security risks, or where production is otherwise impracticable.
simpleExplanation: This section allows prison authorities to refuse production of a prisoner in limited situations recognized by law.
examples: A prisoner suffering from a severe medical condition is not transported to court.

faqs.question: Can prison authorities refuse to produce a prisoner?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may do so in specific situations permitted by law.

title: Prisoner to be brought to Court in custody
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: Whenever a prisoner is produced before a Court under this Chapter, he shall remain in lawful custody during transportation and throughout the court proceedings unless otherwise directed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that prisoners remain under proper custody while being taken to and from court.
examples: Police escort a prisoner from jail to court and back under secure custody.

faqs.question: Does a prisoner remain in custody while attending court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The prisoner remains under lawful custody unless released by a competent authority.

title: Power to issue commission for examination of witness in prison
chapter: Chapter XXII - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: When it appears that a prisoner can more conveniently be examined within the prison as a witness, the Court may issue a commission for recording the prisoner's evidence in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows evidence of a prisoner to be recorded inside the prison instead of requiring physical production before the Court.
examples: A prisoner serving a sentence gives witness testimony through a commission issued by the Court.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner's testimony be recorded without bringing the prisoner to court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may issue a commission for examination within the prison.

title: Language of Courts
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The State Government may determine what shall be the language of each Court within the State, subject to any law in force and directions of the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes the State Government to specify the language used in courts within the State.
examples: A State Government notifies Kannada as the language to be used in certain courts within the State.

faqs.question: Who decides the language used by a court?
faqs.answer: The State Government, subject to applicable laws and High Court directions.

title: Evidence to be taken in presence of accused
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Except as otherwise expressly provided, all evidence taken during the course of a trial or other proceeding shall be taken in the presence of the accused, or, when personal attendance is dispensed with, in the presence of his pleader.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that the accused has an opportunity to hear and challenge the evidence presented against him.
examples: Witness testimony is recorded in the presence of the accused and his lawyer during trial.

faqs.question: Must evidence be recorded in the presence of the accused?
faqs.answer: Yes, except where the law specifically provides otherwise.

title: Record in summons cases and inquiries
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In summons cases and certain inquiries, the Magistrate shall make a memorandum of the substance of the evidence of each witness as the examination proceeds.
simpleExplanation: This section requires a brief record of witness testimony in summons cases and inquiries.
examples: The Magistrate records a summary of what each witness states during a summons trial.

faqs.question: Is every witness statement recorded verbatim in summons cases?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. A memorandum of the substance of the evidence may be recorded.

title: Record in warrant cases
