chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In all warrant cases tried before a Magistrate, the evidence of each witness shall be recorded in writing or by mechanical means in the manner prescribed by law and authenticated by the Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section lays down how witness evidence must be formally recorded in warrant cases.
examples: Witness testimony in a warrant case is recorded and authenticated by the Magistrate.

faqs.question: How is evidence recorded in warrant cases?
faqs.answer: It is recorded in the prescribed manner and authenticated by the Magistrate.

title: Record in trial before Court of Session
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In trials before a Court of Session, the evidence of each witness shall be recorded in the language of the Court or by mechanical means in accordance with the provisions of law and under the direction of the presiding Judge.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the recording of evidence during Sessions Court trials.
examples: The testimony of prosecution witnesses in a murder trial is recorded under the supervision of the Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Who supervises the recording of evidence in a Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: The presiding Sessions Judge supervises the recording process.

title: Language of record of evidence
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The evidence of each witness shall be recorded in the language of the Court. If the witness gives evidence in another language, it may be interpreted and recorded in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the language requirements for recording witness evidence in court proceedings.
examples: A witness testifying in Malayalam has the testimony interpreted and recorded in the court's official language.

faqs.question: What happens if a witness speaks a different language?
faqs.answer: The testimony may be interpreted and recorded according to legal procedures.

title: Procedure in regard to such evidence when completed
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When the evidence of a witness has been completed, it shall be read over to the witness in the presence of the accused or his pleader and, if necessary, interpreted. Any corrections or observations made by the witness shall be recorded.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that witnesses can verify the accuracy of their recorded testimony before it becomes part of the official record.
examples: A witness corrects a recording error after the statement is read back in court.

faqs.question: Can a witness correct mistakes in recorded testimony?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corrections and observations may be noted before the record is finalized.

title: Interpretation of evidence to accused or his pleader
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Whenever evidence is given in a language not understood by the accused and the accused is present in court, it shall be interpreted to him in open court in a language understood by him.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the accused's right to understand the evidence being presented against him.
examples: Evidence given in Hindi is interpreted into Malayalam for an accused who does not understand Hindi.

faqs.question: Must the accused understand the evidence presented in court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Evidence must be interpreted if the accused does not understand the language used.

title: Remarks respecting demeanour of witness
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Judge or Magistrate may record remarks concerning the demeanour, conduct or behaviour of a witness while under examination if such observations are considered relevant.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the court to note observations about a witness's behaviour that may help evaluate credibility.
examples: A Judge records that a witness appeared hesitant while answering important questions.

faqs.question: Why does a court record observations about a witness?
faqs.answer: Such observations may help assess the reliability and credibility of testimony.

title: Record of examination of accused
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Whenever the accused is examined by the Court, the questions and answers shall be recorded in full or in a manner prescribed by law, and the record shall be read over to the accused and signed where required.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the recording of statements made by the accused during examination by the Court.
examples: The accused explains his defence during examination and the statement is formally recorded.

faqs.question: How is the accused's statement recorded?
faqs.answer: The Court records the questions and answers and verifies the accuracy of the record.

title: Interpreter to be bound to interpret truthfully
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Every interpreter appointed by the Court shall make a declaration or take an oath to interpret truly and accurately the questions put and the answers given during judicial proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that interpreters provide accurate translations during court proceedings.
punishment: False interpretation may attract legal consequences under applicable law.
examples: A court-appointed interpreter takes an oath before translating witness testimony.

faqs.question: Must interpreters take an oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. They are required to faithfully and accurately interpret proceedings.

title: Record in High Court
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In trials before the High Court, the evidence of witnesses shall be recorded in accordance with the rules and practice of the High Court and applicable provisions of law.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes High Courts to follow their prescribed procedures for recording evidence.
examples: A High Court records witness testimony according to its procedural rules.

faqs.question: How is evidence recorded in the High Court?
faqs.answer: According to the rules and procedures prescribed by the High Court.

title: When attendance of witness may be dispensed with and commission issued
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If a witness cannot attend court because of illness, infirmity, public service duties or other sufficient cause, the Court may issue a commission for recording the witness's evidence.
simpleExplanation: This section allows evidence to be recorded outside the courtroom when a witness cannot appear personally.
examples: A seriously ill witness gives evidence through a commission issued by the Court.

faqs.question: Can evidence be recorded without the witness appearing in court?
faqs.answer: Yes. A commission may be issued in appropriate circumstances.

title: Commission to whom to be issued
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A commission for the examination of a witness may be issued to a Judicial Magistrate, Metropolitan Magistrate, other competent judicial authority or such person as authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies the authorities who may be appointed to record evidence under a commission.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate is appointed to record the evidence of a witness who cannot travel.

faqs.question: Who records evidence under a commission?
faqs.answer: The Court may appoint an authorized judicial officer or competent authority.

title: Execution of commissions
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Every person to whom a commission is issued shall execute it according to the directions of the Court and return the recorded evidence within the prescribed time.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the person receiving a commission to properly record and return the evidence.
examples: The appointed Magistrate records the witness's testimony and submits it to the trial court.

faqs.question: What must the commissioner do after recording evidence?
faqs.answer: The evidence must be returned to the Court along with the commission.

title: Parties may examine witnesses
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The parties to the proceeding, their pleaders and authorized representatives shall be entitled to appear before the commissioner and examine, cross-examine or re-examine witnesses during execution of the commission.
simpleExplanation: This section preserves the right of both sides to question witnesses whose evidence is recorded through a commission.
examples: The defence lawyer cross-examines a witness whose testimony is being recorded outside court.

faqs.question: Can cross-examination take place during commission proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both parties have the right to examine and cross-examine witnesses.

title: Return of commission
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: After execution, the commission together with the evidence recorded, documents collected and any observations made by the commissioner shall be returned to the Court that issued the commission.
simpleExplanation: This section requires all materials gathered during commission proceedings to be sent back to the Court.
examples: The commissioner submits the recorded testimony and exhibits to the trial court.

faqs.question: What happens after a commission is completed?
faqs.answer: The evidence and related materials are returned to the issuing Court.

title: Adjournment of proceeding
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Upon receipt of a commission, the Court may adjourn the proceeding for such time as may be necessary to obtain and consider the evidence recorded under the commission.
simpleExplanation: This section permits the Court to postpone proceedings until commissioned evidence is received and reviewed.
examples: The trial is adjourned while the Court waits for evidence being recorded in another city.

faqs.question: Can a case be postponed while commission evidence is being collected?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may adjourn proceedings when necessary.

title: Execution of foreign commissions
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When a commission issued by a foreign court is received for execution in India, the competent court may execute it in accordance with law and applicable international arrangements.
simpleExplanation: This section allows Indian courts to assist foreign courts by recording evidence or performing judicial acts requested through commissions.
examples: An Indian court records the testimony of a witness at the request of a foreign court.

faqs.question: Can Indian courts assist foreign courts in gathering evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Foreign commissions may be executed according to law.

title: Deposition of medical witness
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The deposition of a medical witness taken and attested by a Magistrate may be used as evidence in an inquiry, trial or other proceeding, subject to the right of the Court to summon and examine the witness if necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section permits prior recorded testimony of medical experts to be used in court under certain circumstances.
examples: A doctor's deposition recorded earlier is produced during trial without requiring immediate personal attendance.

faqs.question: Must a doctor always appear personally in court?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. A properly recorded deposition may sometimes be used.

title: Evidence of officers of Mint
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any document purporting to be a report under the hand of an officer of the Mint upon any matter submitted for examination may be used as evidence in proceedings, subject to the Court's power to summon the officer if required.
simpleExplanation: This section allows expert reports from Mint officials to be used as evidence without requiring their presence in every case.
examples: A Mint expert's report regarding the authenticity of coins is admitted into evidence.

faqs.question: Can expert reports be admitted without calling the expert?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain official reports may be used directly, though the Court may still summon the expert.

title: Reports of certain Government scientific experts
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Reports signed by specified Government scientific experts, including forensic specialists and other notified experts, may be used as evidence in criminal proceedings. The Court may summon and examine the expert if necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section allows forensic and scientific reports to be used directly in court without requiring the expert to appear in every case.
examples: A forensic laboratory report identifying fingerprints is admitted as evidence.

faqs.question: Can forensic reports be used without calling the expert to court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The report may be admitted, though the Court can summon the expert if needed.

title: No formal proof of certain documents
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where any document is filed before a Court and its genuineness is not disputed by the opposite party, the Court may dispense with formal proof of that document.
simpleExplanation: This section saves time by avoiding unnecessary proof of documents whose authenticity is not challenged.
examples: A certified bank statement is accepted without calling a witness because neither party disputes it.

faqs.question: Must every document be formally proved in court?
faqs.answer: No. Documents whose genuineness is admitted may be accepted without formal proof.

title: Affidavit in proof of conduct of public servants
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When proof of the conduct of a public servant is required, the matter may be proved by affidavit unless the Court directs otherwise.
simpleExplanation: This section permits certain facts relating to public servants to be proved through affidavits instead of oral testimony.
examples: An affidavit is filed to establish that a public official performed a particular official act.

faqs.question: Can affidavits be used as evidence in criminal proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes, in situations specifically permitted by law.

title: Evidence of formal character on affidavit
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Evidence of a formal character may be given by affidavit, and the Court may read such affidavit as evidence. The Court may, however, summon the deponent for examination if necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section allows routine or formal evidence to be presented through affidavits to simplify proceedings.
examples: A formal affidavit regarding custody of records is accepted without requiring personal testimony.

faqs.question: Can the Court require the person who made the affidavit to appear?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may summon the deponent for examination if required.

title: Authorities before whom affidavits may be sworn
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Affidavits to be used before a Court may be sworn or affirmed before a Judge, Magistrate, Commissioner of Oaths, Notary Public, or any other person authorized by law to administer oaths.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the authorities before whom affidavits may be legally executed.
examples: An affidavit is notarized before being submitted to the Court.

faqs.question: Who can administer an affidavit for use in court?
faqs.answer: A Judge, Magistrate, Notary or other authorized officer.

title: Previous conviction or acquittal how proved
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A previous conviction or acquittal may be proved by producing a certified copy of the judgment, order or record of the Court that delivered it, together with evidence identifying the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how prior criminal cases can be officially proved before a court.
examples: The prosecution produces a certified copy of an earlier conviction to establish a repeat offence.

faqs.question: How is a previous conviction proved in court?
faqs.answer: Usually by producing certified court records and proving the identity of the accused.

title: Record of evidence in absence of accused
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If an accused person has absconded and there is no immediate prospect of arresting him, the Court may record the evidence of prosecution witnesses in his absence. Such evidence may later be used against the accused if the witness cannot be produced when the accused is apprehended.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents important evidence from being lost when an accused person is absconding.
examples: A witness's testimony is recorded while the accused remains absconding for several years.

faqs.question: Can evidence be recorded without the accused being present?
faqs.answer: Yes, when the accused is absconding and cannot be arrested within a reasonable time.

title: Person once convicted or acquitted not to be tried for same offence
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A person who has once been tried by a competent Court and convicted or acquitted shall not be tried again for the same offence or on the same facts for any other offence for which a different charge could have been made in the earlier trial, subject to certain exceptions provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects people from being prosecuted repeatedly for the same offence after a lawful acquittal or conviction.
examples: A person acquitted of theft cannot ordinarily be tried again for the same theft incident.

faqs.question: Can someone be tried twice for the same offence?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The law protects against double jeopardy.

title: Appearance by Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor in charge of a case may appear and plead without written authority before any Court in which the case is under inquiry, trial or appeal. A private counsel instructed by a person may assist under the directions of the Public Prosecutor.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the role of Public Prosecutors and explains how private lawyers may assist in criminal prosecutions.
examples: A victim's lawyer assists the prosecution while acting under the directions of the Public Prosecutor.

faqs.question: Can a private lawyer conduct a criminal prosecution independently?
faqs.answer: Generally, the Public Prosecutor remains in charge, though private counsel may assist.

title: Permission to conduct prosecution
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any Magistrate inquiring into or trying a case may permit the prosecution to be conducted by any person other than a police officer below the rank specified by law, subject to the conditions prescribed by the Code.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to authorize a private person or complainant to conduct the prosecution in appropriate cases.
examples: A complainant is permitted by the Magistrate to conduct a private prosecution.

faqs.question: Can a private individual conduct a criminal prosecution?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Magistrate grants permission under this section.

title: Right of person against whom proceedings are instituted to be defended
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any person accused of an offence before a Criminal Court, or against whom proceedings are instituted under this Code, has the right to be defended by a pleader of his choice.
simpleExplanation: This section guarantees the fundamental right of an accused person to choose and be represented by a lawyer.
examples: A person accused of cheating appoints an advocate of his choice to defend him.

faqs.question: Can an accused choose any lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every accused has the right to be defended by a lawyer of his choice.

title: Legal aid to accused at State expense in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a trial before the Court of Session is conducted against an accused who lacks sufficient means to engage a pleader, the Court shall assign a pleader for his defence at the expense of the State.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures free legal assistance for indigent accused persons in serious criminal cases.
examples: An accused unable to afford a lawyer receives legal aid from the State.

faqs.question: Can a poor accused receive a free lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. The State must provide legal aid in eligible cases.

title: Procedure when corporation or registered society is an accused
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When a corporation or registered society is accused of an offence, it may appoint a representative for the purpose of inquiry or trial, and the representative may perform acts that an accused person is required or permitted to perform.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how companies and registered societies participate in criminal proceedings.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence charged.
examples: A company nominates an authorized representative to appear before the Magistrate.

faqs.question: How does a company appear in a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Through an authorized representative appointed for the proceedings.

title: Tender of pardon to accomplice
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Chief Judicial Magistrate, Metropolitan Magistrate or Magistrate of the First Class may tender a pardon to a person believed to have been directly or indirectly concerned in an offence on condition that he makes a full and true disclosure of all facts relating to the offence and the persons involved.
simpleExplanation: This section allows an accomplice to become an approver by fully cooperating with the investigation and prosecution.
punishment: Conditional pardon may be granted instead of prosecution.
examples: A member of a criminal conspiracy receives a pardon after providing complete information about the crime.

faqs.question: What is an approver?
faqs.answer: An accomplice who receives a pardon in exchange for truthful disclosure of facts.

title: Power to direct tender of pardon
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: At any time after commitment of a case and before judgment is passed, the Court to which the commitment is made may tender or direct the tender of a pardon to a person believed to have been concerned in the offence on the same conditions applicable to an approver.
simpleExplanation: This section gives higher courts the power to grant pardon to an accomplice after a case has been committed for trial.
examples: During a Sessions trial, an accomplice is granted pardon in return for truthful testimony.

faqs.question: Can pardon be granted after a case reaches the Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The trial court may tender or direct a pardon before judgment.

title: Trial of person not complying with conditions of pardon
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a person who has accepted a tender of pardon fails to comply with the condition of making a full and true disclosure of all relevant facts, he may be tried for the original offence as well as for giving false evidence, subject to the provisions of law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides consequences when an approver breaks the conditions of the pardon granted to him.
punishment: The person may be prosecuted for the original offence and other applicable offences.
examples: An approver conceals important facts and later loses the protection of the pardon.

faqs.question: What happens if an approver lies or hides information?
faqs.answer: The pardon may become ineffective and the person may be prosecuted.

title: Power to postpone or adjourn proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In every inquiry or trial, the Court may postpone or adjourn proceedings when necessary, recording reasons for doing so. Proceedings should continue from day to day as far as practicable until witnesses in attendance have been examined.
simpleExplanation: This section governs adjournments and encourages speedy disposal of criminal cases.
examples: A hearing is adjourned because a key witness is hospitalized.

faqs.question: Can criminal cases be adjourned?
faqs.answer: Yes, but courts must record reasons and avoid unnecessary delays.

title: Local inspection
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any Judge or Magistrate may, at any stage of an inquiry, trial or other proceeding, inspect any place in which an offence is alleged to have been committed or any other place relevant to the proceedings, and shall record a memorandum of such inspection.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to personally inspect locations relevant to a case.
examples: A Magistrate visits the scene of an alleged accident to better understand the evidence.

faqs.question: Can a Judge personally inspect a crime scene?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may conduct a local inspection when necessary.

title: Power to summon material witness, or examine person present
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any Court may, at any stage of an inquiry, trial or other proceeding, summon any person as a witness, examine any person in attendance, or recall and re-examine any witness if such evidence appears essential to the just decision of the case.
simpleExplanation: This section gives courts broad powers to call or recall witnesses whenever necessary for justice.
examples: The Court recalls a witness after discovering an important issue requiring clarification.

faqs.question: Can a witness be recalled after testifying?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may recall any witness if the evidence is important for deciding the case.

title: Expenses of complainants and witnesses
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Subject to rules made by the State Government, criminal courts may order payment of reasonable expenses incurred by complainants and witnesses attending court proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to reimburse travel and other reasonable expenses of witnesses and complainants.
examples: A witness traveling from another district receives reimbursement for attendance expenses.

faqs.question: Can witnesses receive travel expenses from the court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may order payment of reasonable attendance expenses.

title: Power to examine the accused
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: At any stage of an inquiry or trial, the Court may question the accused to explain any circumstances appearing in the evidence against him. After prosecution evidence is completed, the Court shall generally examine the accused personally before the defence is called upon.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the accused an opportunity to explain evidence and allegations appearing against him during the trial.
examples: The Court asks the accused to explain why his fingerprints were found at the scene of the crime.

faqs.question: Why does the Court question the accused during trial?
faqs.answer: To give the accused an opportunity to explain incriminating evidence.

title: Oral arguments and memorandum of arguments
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any party to a proceeding may address oral arguments and may also submit a memorandum summarizing the arguments. Such memorandum shall form part of the record.
simpleExplanation: This section allows lawyers to present oral submissions and file written summaries of their arguments.
examples: The defence counsel files written arguments summarizing the key legal issues in the case.

faqs.question: Can written arguments be submitted to the Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. A memorandum of arguments may be filed in addition to oral submissions.

title: Accused person to be competent witness
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any accused person shall be a competent witness for the defence and may give evidence on oath in disproof of the charges made against him. However, no adverse inference shall be drawn merely because the accused chooses not to testify.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the accused to testify as a witness in his own defence, but protects the right to remain silent.
examples: An accused voluntarily enters the witness box to explain his version of events.

faqs.question: Must an accused testify in his own case?
faqs.answer: No. The accused may testify voluntarily, but cannot be compelled to do so.

title: No influence to be used to induce disclosure
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: No influence, threat, promise or inducement shall be used to compel or encourage an accused person to disclose information or make statements against his will, except as permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects accused persons from being pressured into making involuntary statements.
examples: An accused cannot be threatened into making a confession during judicial proceedings.

faqs.question: Can an accused be forced to make statements?
faqs.answer: No. Statements must be voluntary and free from improper influence.

title: Provision for inquiries and trial being held in absence of accused in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If the Judge or Magistrate is satisfied that the personal attendance of the accused is not necessary in the interests of justice, the Court may dispense with attendance and proceed with the inquiry or trial in the presence of the accused's pleader, subject to conditions prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows certain proceedings to continue without requiring the accused to be personally present in court.
examples: An accused living far away is allowed to be represented by counsel during routine hearings.

faqs.question: Can a criminal case proceed without the accused being present?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may permit representation through a lawyer in appropriate circumstances.

title: Procedure where accused does not understand proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If the accused, though not of unsound mind, cannot understand the proceedings, the Court may proceed with the inquiry or trial and, in the event of a conviction, forward the proceedings to the High Court together with a report of the circumstances.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a special procedure when an accused person cannot understand the court proceedings due to reasons other than mental illness.
examples: A person unable to comprehend the language or nature of the proceedings is dealt with under this section.

faqs.question: What happens if an accused cannot understand the trial?
faqs.answer: The Court may continue the proceedings and report the matter to the High Court if a conviction results.

title: Power to proceed against other persons appearing to be guilty of offence
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where, in the course of an inquiry or trial, it appears from the evidence that any person not being an accused has committed an offence for which such person could be tried together with the accused, the Court may proceed against that person.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to add a new accused person if evidence during the trial shows their involvement in the offence.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved.
examples: During trial, evidence reveals another participant in the crime, and the Court summons that person as an accused.

faqs.question: Can a new accused be added during a trial?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may proceed against any person whose involvement becomes evident from the evidence.

title: Compounding of offences
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Certain offences specified by law may be compounded by the victim or other authorized person, either with or without the permission of the Court, resulting in the acquittal of the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section permits settlement of specified criminal offences between the parties.
punishment: Upon valid compounding, the accused is acquitted of the compounded offence.
examples: A minor hurt case is settled between the parties and compounded before the Court.

faqs.question: Can criminal cases be settled between the parties?
faqs.answer: Only certain offences listed in the law can be compounded.

title: Withdrawal from prosecution
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor may, with the consent of the Court, withdraw from the prosecution of any person before judgment is pronounced, either generally or in respect of one or more offences.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the prosecution to withdraw a criminal case with the approval of the Court.
examples: The Public Prosecutor seeks withdrawal of charges due to insufficient evidence.

faqs.question: Can the prosecution withdraw a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. Court approval is required before withdrawal.

title: Procedure in cases which Magistrate cannot dispose of
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If a Magistrate finds during an inquiry or trial that the case should be tried or dealt with by another Magistrate or Court, he shall stay proceedings and submit the case to the appropriate authority according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a mechanism for transferring cases when the Magistrate lacks jurisdiction or authority to dispose of them.
examples: A Magistrate discovers that the offence is triable exclusively by a Sessions Court and transfers the case.

faqs.question: What happens if a Magistrate cannot legally decide a case?
faqs.answer: The case is forwarded to the appropriate court or authority.

title: Procedure when, after commencement of inquiry or trial, Magistrate finds case should be committed
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If, during an inquiry or trial before a Magistrate, it appears that the case ought to be tried by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall commit the case to that Court and forward the accused and records in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires a Magistrate to transfer a case to the Sessions Court when it becomes clear that the offence should be tried there.
examples: During trial, facts emerge showing that the offence is exclusively triable by the Sessions Court, leading to commitment.

faqs.question: Can a case be transferred to the Sessions Court after the trial has started?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate must commit the case if it appears that the Sessions Court should try it.

title: Trial of persons previously convicted of offences against coinage, stamp-law or property
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Special procedural provisions apply to persons previously convicted of certain offences relating to coinage, stamps or property where enhanced punishment or special treatment is authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with repeat offenders convicted of specific categories of offences.
punishment: Punishment depends on the substantive law governing the offence and prior conviction.
examples: A repeat counterfeiting offender is prosecuted with reference to earlier convictions.

faqs.question: Why are previous convictions relevant in some cases?
faqs.answer: They may affect punishment or procedural treatment under the law.

title: Procedure when Magistrate cannot pass sentence sufficiently severe
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If a Magistrate is of the opinion that the accused deserves a punishment more severe than the Magistrate is authorized to impose, the Magistrate shall submit the proceedings to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or other competent authority for appropriate sentencing.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Magistrate to refer a case when the required punishment exceeds his sentencing powers.
punishment: Sentence determined by the competent higher court or authority.
examples: A Magistrate finds the offence warrants a sentence beyond his powers and forwards the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens if a Magistrate believes a harsher sentence is required?
faqs.answer: The case is referred to a court with adequate sentencing powers.

title: Conviction or commitment on evidence partly recorded by one Magistrate and partly by another
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When a Judge or Magistrate is succeeded by another, the successor may act upon evidence recorded by the predecessor and continue the inquiry or trial, subject to the power to recall witnesses if necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents criminal trials from restarting simply because the presiding officer changes.
examples: A newly transferred Magistrate continues a trial using evidence already recorded by the previous Magistrate.

faqs.question: Must a trial start again when a Magistrate is transferred?
faqs.answer: No. The successor Magistrate can continue the proceedings using the existing record.

title: Court to be open
chapter: Chapter XXIV - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Criminal Courts shall ordinarily be open to the public. However, the Court may exclude the public from proceedings or conduct in-camera proceedings in situations specified by law, including certain cases involving sexual offences.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the principle of open justice while permitting private hearings in sensitive cases.
examples: A sexual assault trial is conducted in camera to protect the privacy of the victim.

faqs.question: Are criminal trials open to the public?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, but sensitive cases may be heard privately.

title: Procedure in case of accused being of unsound mind and incapable of making defence
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: If a Magistrate conducting an inquiry has reason to believe that the accused is of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, the Magistrate shall inquire into the fact of such unsoundness and cause the accused to be examined by a medical expert as prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the Court to assess the mental condition of an accused who appears unable to understand or participate in the proceedings.
examples: A Magistrate orders psychiatric evaluation when an accused appears unable to understand court proceedings.

faqs.question: What happens if an accused appears mentally unfit to stand trial?
faqs.answer: The Court must conduct an inquiry and obtain a medical assessment.

title: Procedure in case of person of unsound mind tried before Court
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: If, during a trial before a Magistrate or Court of Session, the accused appears to be of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, the Court shall determine the issue and obtain medical evidence in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when mental incapacity becomes apparent during a trial that is already in progress.
examples: A Sessions Court pauses proceedings to determine whether the accused is mentally capable of defending himself.

faqs.question: Can a trial be paused because of the accused's mental condition?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court must first determine whether the accused can effectively defend himself.

title: Release of person of unsound mind pending investigation or trial
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: A person found to be of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence may be released on bail if adequate care and treatment arrangements exist, or otherwise detained in a suitable facility as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs custody, treatment and possible release of mentally unfit accused persons during proceedings.
examples: An accused suffering from severe mental illness is released to family supervision subject to medical treatment conditions.

faqs.question: Can a mentally ill accused be released during proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Release may be granted if proper care and treatment arrangements are available.

title: Resumption of inquiry or trial
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When a person previously found incapable of making a defence becomes capable of doing so, the Court shall resume the inquiry or trial and proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows criminal proceedings to restart once the accused regains the capacity to participate in the case.
examples: After successful treatment, an accused is declared fit to stand trial and proceedings resume.

faqs.question: What happens when the accused recovers mental capacity?
faqs.answer: The suspended inquiry or trial may continue.

title: Procedure on accused appearing before Magistrate or Court
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When an accused previously detained or released due to unsoundness of mind appears before the Magistrate or Court after recovery, the Court shall proceed in accordance with the provisions governing resumption of inquiry or trial.
simpleExplanation: This section sets out the procedure to be followed when a recovered accused returns before the Court.
examples: A recovered accused appears before the Court and the suspended proceedings are revived.

faqs.question: How does the Court deal with a recovered accused person?
faqs.answer: The Court verifies recovery and proceeds under the applicable procedural provisions.

title: When accused appears to have been of sound mind
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: If, after inquiry and medical examination, the accused is found to be of sound mind and capable of making his defence, the Magistrate or Court shall proceed with the inquiry or trial in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section requires the criminal case to continue normally when the accused is found mentally fit to stand trial.
examples: A psychiatric evaluation confirms that the accused is mentally fit, and the trial continues.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused is found mentally fit?
faqs.answer: The Court proceeds with the inquiry or trial as usual.

title: Judgment of acquittal on ground of unsoundness of mind
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: Whenever a person is acquitted on the ground that, at the time of committing the act, he was incapable of knowing the nature of the act due to unsoundness of mind, the finding shall specifically state whether he committed the act charged.
simpleExplanation: This section governs judgments where the accused is acquitted because of legal insanity.
examples: An accused is acquitted after the Court finds that he was legally insane when the act occurred.

faqs.question: Can a person be acquitted because of insanity?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the legal requirements for insanity are established.

title: Person acquitted on such ground to be detained in safe custody
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: A person acquitted on the ground of unsoundness of mind may be detained in safe custody or delivered to the care of a relative or friend subject to conditions imposed by the Court and applicable law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides measures for the safety and treatment of persons acquitted due to mental illness.
examples: A person acquitted because of insanity is placed under supervised medical care.

faqs.question: Is a person immediately released after an insanity acquittal?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. The Court may order custody or supervised care.

title: Power of State Government to empower officer in charge to discharge
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: The State Government may empower specified officers to discharge or release persons detained under this Chapter when conditions prescribed by law have been satisfied.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes the State Government to permit release of persons detained after insanity-related proceedings.
examples: A detained person is released after authorities are satisfied about recovery and public safety.

faqs.question: Who can authorize the release of a detained person under this Chapter?
faqs.answer: An officer empowered by the State Government may do so according to law.

title: Procedure where prisoner with mental illness is reported capable of making defence
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When a detained person suffering from mental illness is reported by competent medical authorities to be capable of making his defence, the report shall be submitted to the appropriate Court for further proceedings according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the process for bringing a recovered accused back before the Court.
examples: A psychiatric hospital reports that an accused has recovered sufficiently to face trial.

faqs.question: What happens when a mentally ill accused recovers?
faqs.answer: The Court is informed and may resume the criminal proceedings.

title: Procedure where lunatic detained is declared fit to be released
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When a person detained under this Chapter is certified by competent medical authority to be fit for release and no longer requires detention, the prescribed authority may order release in accordance with law and applicable conditions.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with the release of persons previously detained because of mental illness after they are declared fit.
examples: A person acquitted due to insanity is released after medical experts certify recovery.

faqs.question: Can a detained mentally ill person be released after recovery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Release may be ordered after medical certification and compliance with legal requirements.

title: Delivery of person of unsound mind to care of relative or friend
chapter: Chapter XXV - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: A person suffering from unsoundness of mind may, under appropriate safeguards and subject to prescribed conditions, be delivered to the care of a relative or friend who undertakes responsibility for the person's treatment and supervision.
simpleExplanation: This section permits mentally ill persons to be cared for by family members or trusted individuals instead of remaining in institutional custody.
examples: The Court allows a recovered patient to live with family members who undertake responsibility for care and supervision.

faqs.question: Can a mentally ill accused be released into family care?
faqs.answer: Yes. Subject to legal safeguards and undertakings by responsible persons.

title: Procedure in cases mentioned in section 195
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When it appears to a Court that an offence described in section 195 has been committed in relation to proceedings before it, the Court may conduct a preliminary inquiry and make a complaint in writing to the competent Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the procedure for prosecuting offences such as perjury and fabrication of evidence affecting court proceedings.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence alleged.
examples: A witness is prosecuted for knowingly giving false evidence during a trial.

faqs.question: What happens when someone commits perjury in court?
faqs.answer: The Court may initiate proceedings under this section after a preliminary inquiry.

title: Appeal
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: Any person against whom a complaint has been made under section 340, or whose application has been refused, may file an appeal before the court authorized by law within the prescribed period.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a right of appeal in matters relating to prosecution for offences affecting the administration of justice.
examples: A person against whom a perjury complaint has been ordered files an appeal challenging the decision.

faqs.question: Can a person challenge an order made under section 340?
faqs.answer: Yes. An appeal may be filed before the appropriate court.

title: Power to order costs
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: The appellate court may order payment of costs by any party in proceedings under this Chapter as it considers just and proper in the circumstances of the case.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers appellate courts to award costs in proceedings concerning offences affecting the administration of justice.
punishment: Payment of costs as ordered by the Court.
examples: An appellate court orders costs against a party who filed a frivolous challenge.

faqs.question: Can courts order payment of costs in these proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. Appellate courts may award costs where justified.

title: Procedure of Magistrate taking cognizance
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: A Magistrate receiving a complaint under section 340 or other applicable provisions shall proceed according to law as if the complaint had been made under the ordinary provisions relating to complaints before Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a Magistrate should deal with complaints relating to offences affecting the administration of justice.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence alleged and proved.
examples: A Magistrate receives a complaint regarding false evidence and proceeds with the case under the normal complaint procedure.

faqs.question: How does a Magistrate handle a complaint under section 340?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate follows the regular procedure applicable to criminal complaints.

title: Summary procedure for trial for giving false evidence
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: If a Court is satisfied that a witness has intentionally given false evidence or fabricated evidence during a proceeding before it, the Court may, after following the prescribed procedure, try the offender summarily and impose punishment authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to take immediate action against perjury committed during judicial proceedings.
punishment: Punishment as prescribed for giving or fabricating false evidence under substantive criminal law.
examples: A witness is prosecuted after deliberately lying under oath during a trial.

faqs.question: Can a court directly act against a witness who lies under oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may initiate summary proceedings in appropriate cases.

title: Procedure in certain cases of contempt
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When an offence such as intentional insult, interruption or misconduct occurs in the view or presence of a Criminal Court, the Court may record the facts and proceed summarily against the offender in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section gives courts the power to deal immediately with certain acts of contempt committed in the courtroom.
punishment: Punishment as prescribed by the Code for such contemptuous conduct.
examples: A person repeatedly disrupts court proceedings and is proceeded against for contempt.

faqs.question: Can a court punish misconduct occurring in the courtroom?
faqs.answer: Yes. Certain acts committed in the Court's presence may be dealt with summarily.

title: Cases not dealt with under section 345
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: If the Court considers that a case of contempt or misconduct should not be dealt with summarily under section 345, it may forward the matter to a Magistrate having jurisdiction for trial according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section applies when the Court decides that more serious contempt proceedings require a regular trial.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved after trial.
examples: A serious incident in court is referred to another Magistrate for detailed proceedings.

faqs.question: What if the Court considers the misconduct too serious for summary action?
faqs.answer: The matter may be referred for a regular criminal trial.

title: When Registrar or Sub-Registrar deemed to be a Civil Court
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: For the purposes of specified provisions relating to offences affecting the administration of justice, a Registrar or Sub-Registrar acting under the Registration Act shall be deemed to be a Civil Court.
simpleExplanation: This section extends certain protections and procedural provisions to Registrars and Sub-Registrars performing official duties.
examples: False statements made during registration proceedings may attract consequences similar to those applicable in court proceedings.

faqs.question: Why are Registrars treated like Civil Courts under this section?
faqs.answer: To ensure that offences affecting official registration proceedings can be appropriately addressed.

title: Discharge of offender on submission of apology
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When a person proceeded against for certain offences affecting the administration of justice makes a satisfactory apology, the Court may, in appropriate cases, discharge the offender or remit the punishment.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Court to forgive or reduce punishment when a sincere apology is offered.
punishment: The Court may discharge the offender or reduce/remit punishment.
examples: A person who disrupted court proceedings apologizes sincerely and is discharged by the Court.

faqs.question: Can an apology help in contempt-related proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. A genuine apology may lead to discharge or reduction of punishment.

title: Imprisonment or committal of person refusing to answer or produce document
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: If a witness or person legally bound to answer questions or produce documents refuses to do so without lawful excuse, the Court may order detention or take other measures authorized by law until compliance is secured.
simpleExplanation: This section helps courts enforce witness cooperation and production of documents.
punishment: Detention or other lawful measures may be ordered.
examples: A witness refuses to produce a document despite a court order and is detained until compliance.

faqs.question: What happens if a witness refuses to answer questions in court?
faqs.answer: The Court may take coercive action to compel compliance.

title: Summary procedure for punishment for non-attendance by a witness
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When a witness fails to attend in obedience to a summons without lawful excuse, the Court may proceed summarily and impose punishment in accordance with law after giving the witness an opportunity to explain.
simpleExplanation: This section allows quick action against witnesses who ignore court summonses.
punishment: Punishment as prescribed by law for non-attendance.
examples: A witness repeatedly ignores court summons and faces summary proceedings.

faqs.question: Can a witness be punished for ignoring a summons?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may take summary action if there is no valid excuse.

title: Appeal from conviction under sections 344, 345, 349 and 350
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: A person convicted under the summary provisions relating to false evidence, contempt or witness default may appeal to the court specified by law within the prescribed period.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a right of appeal against certain summary convictions made by courts.
examples: A witness punished for non-attendance files an appeal against the conviction.

faqs.question: Can a person appeal a summary conviction under these provisions?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law provides a right of appeal.

title: Certain Judges and Magistrates not to try certain offences when committed before themselves
chapter: Chapter XXVI - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: Except where specifically authorized by law, a Judge or Magistrate shall not try an offence committed before himself if he is personally involved in the matter in a manner affecting impartial adjudication.
simpleExplanation: This section protects fairness by preventing judges or magistrates from hearing certain cases in which they are directly concerned.
examples: A case involving personal insult to a Magistrate is transferred to another competent court.

faqs.question: Why can't a Judge always try offences committed before him?
faqs.answer: To ensure impartiality and maintain confidence in the judicial process.

title: Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: The judgment in every trial in a Criminal Court shall be pronounced in open Court by the presiding officer immediately after the termination of the trial or at some subsequent time of which notice shall be given to the parties or their pleaders.
simpleExplanation: This section requires criminal court judgments to be delivered openly and transparently after the trial is completed.
examples: After completing the trial, the Judge announces the judgment in open court.

faqs.question: Must criminal judgments be pronounced in open court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Judgments are generally required to be delivered in open court.

title: Language and contents of judgment
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: Every judgment shall be written in the language of the Court and shall contain the points for determination, the decision thereon, the reasons for the decision, and where applicable, the offence and sentence imposed.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies what information must be included in a criminal court judgment.
examples: A judgment explains the issues involved, evidence considered, findings reached and the sentence imposed.

faqs.question: What must a criminal judgment contain?
faqs.answer: It must include the issues, findings, reasons and sentence where applicable.

title: Metropolitan Magistrate's judgment
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: In cases tried by a Metropolitan Magistrate, the judgment may be recorded in a concise form containing the particulars prescribed by law instead of a detailed judgment.
simpleExplanation: This section permits simplified judgments in cases decided by Metropolitan Magistrates.
examples: A Metropolitan Magistrate records a concise judgment in a routine criminal case.

faqs.question: Can some judgments be recorded in a shorter format?
faqs.answer: Yes. Metropolitan Magistrates may use a prescribed summary format.

title: Order for notifying address of previously convicted offender
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: A Court may require a person convicted of specified offences and previously convicted of certain offences to notify his place of residence and any change of address for a prescribed period after release.
simpleExplanation: This section helps authorities monitor certain repeat offenders after conviction.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract legal consequences under applicable law.
examples: A repeat offender is directed to inform authorities whenever he changes residence.

faqs.question: Why must some offenders report their address?
faqs.answer: To assist law enforcement in supervising certain repeat offenders.

title: Order to pay compensation
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: When a Court imposes a sentence of fine or passes a sentence including a fine, the Court may order that the whole or any part of the fine be applied towards compensation to victims for loss or injury caused by the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to award compensation to victims using fines collected from offenders.
punishment: Compensation may be ordered in addition to other lawful punishments.
examples: A victim of cheating receives compensation from the fine imposed on the offender.

faqs.question: Can a victim receive compensation in a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may direct payment of compensation from the fine imposed.

title: Victim compensation scheme
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: Every State Government, in coordination with the Central Government, shall prepare a scheme for providing funds to compensate victims or their dependents who have suffered loss or injury as a result of crime and who require rehabilitation.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes government-funded compensation schemes for victims of crime and their families.
examples: A victim of a serious assault receives financial assistance under the State Victim Compensation Scheme.

faqs.question: What is the Victim Compensation Scheme?
faqs.answer: It is a government program that provides financial support and rehabilitation assistance to eligible crime victims.

title: Compensation to be in addition to fine
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: Compensation payable to victims under specified laws shall be in addition to any fine imposed upon the offender and any compensation awarded under section 357.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that victim compensation is separate from and additional to criminal fines.
examples: A victim receives compensation under a government scheme in addition to compensation awarded from the offender's fine.

faqs.question: Does victim compensation replace the offender's fine?
faqs.answer: No. Compensation is paid in addition to any fine or other compensation ordered by the Court.

title: Treatment of victims
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: All hospitals, whether public or private, shall immediately provide first aid and medical treatment, free of cost, to victims of specified offences and shall promptly inform the police regarding such incidents.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures immediate medical care for victims of serious offences, regardless of their ability to pay.
punishment: Failure to comply may attract penalties under applicable law.
examples: A hospital provides free emergency treatment to a victim of sexual assault and informs the police.

faqs.question: Can a hospital refuse treatment to a victim because of inability to pay?
faqs.answer: No. Immediate treatment must be provided free of cost in specified cases.

title: Right of victim to take assistance of an advocate
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: A victim shall have the right to engage an advocate of choice to assist the prosecution during investigation and trial, subject to the provisions of law.
simpleExplanation: This section recognizes the victim's right to be assisted by a lawyer during criminal proceedings.
examples: A victim appoints a lawyer to assist the Public Prosecutor during trial.

faqs.question: Can a victim have a private lawyer in a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. A victim may engage an advocate to assist during proceedings.

title: Witness protection scheme
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: Every State Government shall implement a Witness Protection Scheme to safeguard witnesses facing threats, intimidation or danger due to their participation in criminal proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section provides legal support and protection measures for vulnerable witnesses.
examples: A witness in an organized crime case is provided security under the Witness Protection Scheme.

faqs.question: Who can receive protection under the Witness Protection Scheme?
faqs.answer: Witnesses facing genuine threats because of their involvement in criminal proceedings.

title: Compensation to persons groundlessly arrested
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: Where a person is arrested without sufficient grounds and brought before a Magistrate, the Magistrate may award compensation to the person against whom the arrest was made if the arrest appears to have been unnecessary or unjustified.
simpleExplanation: This section allows compensation to be awarded when a person is wrongfully or unnecessarily arrested.
punishment: Compensation may be ordered against the responsible person.
examples: A person arrested without any reasonable basis is awarded compensation by the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can a person receive compensation for a wrongful arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may award compensation if the arrest lacked sufficient grounds.

title: Order to pay costs in non-cognizable cases
chapter: Chapter XXVII - The Judgment
officialText: When a conviction is obtained in a non-cognizable case instituted upon complaint or information given by a private person, the Court may order the accused to pay reasonable costs incurred by the complainant.
simpleExplanation: This section allows reimbursement of legal and related expenses incurred by complainants in certain cases.
punishment: Payment of costs as directed by the Court.
examples: A complainant is reimbursed for expenses after successfully prosecuting a non-cognizable offence.

faqs.question: Can a complainant recover expenses from the convicted accused?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may award reasonable costs in eligible cases.

title: Order to release on probation of good conduct or after admonition
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: In appropriate cases involving certain offenders, particularly first-time offenders and persons convicted of less serious offences, the Court may release the offender on probation of good conduct or after due admonition instead of sentencing them to imprisonment.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to give deserving offenders an opportunity for rehabilitation rather than imprisonment.
punishment: Release on probation subject to conditions imposed by the Court.
examples: A first-time offender convicted of a minor offence is released on probation.

faqs.question: What is probation of good conduct?
faqs.answer: It is a release subject to conditions, allowing an offender to avoid imprisonment if they maintain good behavior.

title: Special reasons to be recorded in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: Where the Court could have dealt with an offender under probation-related provisions but chooses not to do so, it shall record special reasons for imposing a sentence instead.
simpleExplanation: This section requires courts to explain why probation was not granted when it was legally available.
examples: A Court records reasons for refusing probation due to the seriousness of the offender's conduct.

faqs.question: Must a Court explain why probation was denied?
faqs.answer: Yes. Special reasons must be recorded when probation could have been granted.

title: Court not to alter judgment
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: Except for correcting clerical or arithmetical errors, no Court shall alter or review its judgment or final order after it has been signed.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents courts from changing final judgments after they have been officially signed, except for minor mistakes.
examples: A Court corrects a typographical error in a judgment but cannot change the final decision.

faqs.question: Can a Court change its final judgment later?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Only clerical or mathematical mistakes may be corrected.

title: Copy of judgment to be given to the accused and other persons
chapter: Chapter XXIX - Copies of Judgments and Orders
officialText: When an accused is sentenced to imprisonment, a copy of the judgment shall be provided free of cost. Copies may also be supplied to other eligible persons in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that convicted persons and certain other persons receive copies of judgments, often without charge.
examples: A convicted person receives a free copy of the judgment to help prepare an appeal.

faqs.question: Is a convicted person entitled to a copy of the judgment?
faqs.answer: Yes. A free copy must generally be provided when imprisonment is imposed.

title: Judgment when to be translated
chapter: Chapter XXIX - Copies of Judgments and Orders
officialText: If the accused does not understand the language in which the judgment is written, a translated copy or translation of the essential portions shall be provided in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the accused's right to understand the judgment delivered against them.
examples: A translated version of the judgment is provided to an accused who does not understand the court language.

faqs.question: Can an accused receive a translated judgment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Translation must be provided when necessary for understanding the judgment.

title: Court of Session to send copy of finding and sentence to District Magistrate
chapter: Chapter XXIX - Copies of Judgments and Orders
officialText: Every Court of Session shall send a copy of its finding and sentence to the District Magistrate or other authority specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that important criminal judgments are communicated to the appropriate administrative authorities.
examples: A Sessions Court forwards details of a conviction and sentence to the District Magistrate.

faqs.question: Why are judgments sent to the District Magistrate?
faqs.answer: For official records, administration and implementation of legal requirements.

title: Sentence of death to be submitted by Court of Session for confirmation
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: Whenever a Court of Session passes a sentence of death, the proceedings shall be submitted to the High Court for confirmation, and the sentence shall not be executed unless confirmed by the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section requires High Court review before any death sentence can be carried out.
punishment: Death sentence subject to High Court confirmation.
examples: A death penalty imposed by a Sessions Court is automatically reviewed by the High Court.

faqs.question: Can a death sentence be executed immediately after being imposed?
faqs.answer: No. It must first be confirmed by the High Court.

title: Power to direct further inquiry to be made or additional evidence to be taken
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: While considering a death sentence for confirmation, the High Court may direct further inquiry, take additional evidence itself, or order such evidence to be taken by a subordinate court.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the High Court to gather more evidence before deciding whether to confirm a death sentence.
examples: The High Court orders additional evidence regarding the accused's involvement before confirming the sentence.

faqs.question: Can the High Court seek additional evidence in a death sentence case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court may direct further inquiry or collect additional evidence.

title: Power of High Court to confirm sentence or annul conviction
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: In a case submitted for confirmation of a death sentence, the High Court may confirm the sentence, pass any other sentence warranted by law, annul the conviction, order a new trial, or acquit the accused.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the High Court broad powers when reviewing a death sentence imposed by a Sessions Court.
punishment: The High Court may confirm, modify, reduce or set aside the sentence.
examples: The High Court reduces a death sentence to life imprisonment after reviewing the evidence.

faqs.question: Can the High Court change a death sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court may confirm, reduce, alter or even set aside the conviction.

title: Confirmation or new sentence to be signed by two Judges
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: Every order confirming a death sentence or passing a new sentence in such proceedings shall be made, signed and dated by at least two Judges of the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that decisions involving death sentence confirmation are made collectively by at least two High Court Judges.
examples: Two High Court Judges sign the order confirming a death sentence.

faqs.question: How many Judges must approve a death sentence confirmation?
faqs.answer: At least two Judges of the High Court.

title: Procedure in case of difference of opinion
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: If Judges hearing a reference differ in opinion on any material point, the case shall be decided in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law, including reference to another Judge where necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a mechanism for resolving disagreements among High Court Judges hearing a criminal reference.
examples: Two Judges disagree on whether a death sentence should be confirmed, leading to reference before another Judge.

faqs.question: What happens if High Court Judges disagree?
faqs.answer: The matter is decided according to the procedure for resolving judicial differences of opinion.

title: Procedure in cases submitted to High Court for confirmation
chapter: Chapter XXX - Reference and Revision
officialText: In proceedings for confirmation of a death sentence, the High Court shall consider the entire record, hear the accused if necessary, and decide the matter in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the general procedure followed by the High Court while reviewing death sentence cases.
examples: The High Court reviews the complete trial record before deciding whether to confirm the sentence.

faqs.question: Does the High Court review the full case record in death sentence matters?
faqs.answer: Yes. The entire record is examined before a decision is made.

title: No appeal unless otherwise provided
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: No appeal shall lie from any judgment or order of a criminal court except as expressly provided by this Code or any other law for the time being in force.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes that appeals are allowed only when specifically authorized by law.
examples: A person cannot file an appeal unless a legal provision grants a right of appeal.

faqs.question: Is there always a right to appeal in criminal cases?
faqs.answer: No. Appeals are permitted only where the law specifically provides for them.

title: Appeal from orders requiring security or refusing acceptance of surety
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Any person ordered to furnish security, or whose surety has been rejected or refused by a criminal court, may appeal against such order to the court authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section gives a right to challenge court orders relating to security bonds and sureties.
examples: A person appeals after the court refuses to accept the proposed surety.

faqs.question: Can a person appeal if a surety is rejected?
faqs.answer: Yes. An appeal may be filed before the appropriate appellate court.

title: Appeals from convictions
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: A person convicted by a Sessions Court, Additional Sessions Court, or certain other criminal courts may appeal against the conviction and sentence before the appellate court specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the principal right of appeal for convicted persons.
examples: A person convicted by a Sessions Court files an appeal before the High Court.

faqs.question: Can a convicted person appeal against the conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law grants a right of appeal in specified cases.

title: No appeal in certain cases when accused pleads guilty
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Where an accused person has been convicted on a plea of guilty, no appeal shall ordinarily lie except as to the extent or legality of the sentence imposed.
simpleExplanation: This section limits appeals when a person has voluntarily admitted guilt.
examples: An accused who pleaded guilty challenges only the severity of the sentence.

faqs.question: Can a person appeal after pleading guilty?
faqs.answer: Generally only regarding the legality or extent of the sentence.

title: No appeal in petty cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: No appeal shall lie in certain petty cases involving minor sentences or fines, except where specifically provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents appeals in minor criminal cases to reduce unnecessary litigation.
examples: A person fined a very small amount for a petty offence may not have a right to appeal.

faqs.question: Can every criminal conviction be appealed?
faqs.answer: No. Some petty cases do not carry a statutory right of appeal.

title: Appeal by the State Government against sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: The State Government may direct the Public Prosecutor to present an appeal against a sentence on the ground that it is inadequate, before the court authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the State to challenge a sentence that it believes is too lenient.
examples: The State appeals seeking enhancement of a sentence imposed for a serious offence.

faqs.question: Can the Government appeal for a harsher sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The State may challenge an inadequate sentence through the Public Prosecutor.

title: Appeal in case of acquittal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
