officialText: Subject to the provisions of law, the State Government or Central Government may direct the Public Prosecutor to present an appeal against an order of acquittal. In specified cases, a complainant may also seek special leave to appeal against acquittal.
simpleExplanation: This section allows acquittal orders to be challenged before a higher court.
examples: The State files an appeal after a trial court acquits a person accused of a serious offence.

faqs.question: Can an acquittal be appealed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Government and, in some cases, the complainant may challenge an acquittal.

title: Appeal against conviction by High Court in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Where the High Court reverses an order of acquittal and convicts the accused, imposing a sentence of death, life imprisonment or imprisonment of a specified duration, the convicted person may appeal to the Supreme Court.
simpleExplanation: This section grants a right of appeal to the Supreme Court in serious cases where the High Court convicts a person after reversing an acquittal.
examples: The High Court overturns an acquittal and convicts the accused, who then appeals to the Supreme Court.

faqs.question: Can a person appeal to the Supreme Court after being convicted by the High Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. In specified serious cases, a direct appeal is available.

title: Special right of appeal in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Where more persons than one are convicted in a trial and an appealable judgment or order has been passed against one or more of them, the remaining convicted persons may also appeal even if the judgment would not otherwise have been appealable.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures fairness by extending appeal rights to co-accused in certain situations.
examples: Several accused are convicted together, and all receive the benefit of the available appeal.

faqs.question: Can co-accused benefit from another person's appeal rights?
faqs.answer: Yes. In specified circumstances, they may also file appeals.

title: Appeal to Court of Session how heard
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Appeals presented to the Court of Session shall be heard by the Sessions Judge or by an Additional Sessions Judge in accordance with the distribution of business prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains who hears appeals filed before the Sessions Court.
examples: An appeal against a Magistrate's conviction is heard by the Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Who hears appeals filed before the Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: The Sessions Judge or an authorized Additional Sessions Judge.

title: Petition of appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Every appeal shall be made in the form of a written petition presented by the appellant or his pleader and shall be accompanied by a copy of the judgment or order appealed against unless otherwise provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section lays down the basic requirements for filing a criminal appeal.
examples: A convicted person files a written appeal along with a copy of the trial court judgment.

faqs.question: How is a criminal appeal filed?
faqs.answer: By submitting a written appeal petition along with the required documents.

title: Procedure when appellant in jail
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: If the appellant is confined in jail, he may present the petition of appeal and related documents through the officer in charge of the prison, who shall forward them to the appropriate appellate court.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that prisoners can exercise their right to appeal without needing to appear personally in court to file it.
examples: A convicted prisoner submits an appeal through the jail superintendent.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner file an appeal from jail?
faqs.answer: Yes. The appeal may be forwarded through prison authorities.

title: Summary dismissal of appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: After examining the appeal petition and the record, the appellate court may summarily dismiss the appeal if it finds no sufficient ground for interference, subject to the safeguards provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows appellate courts to reject meritless appeals at an early stage.
examples: An appeal lacking any legal basis is dismissed without a full hearing.

faqs.question: Can an appeal be rejected without a full hearing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Appeals with no sufficient grounds may be summarily dismissed.

title: Procedure for hearing appeals not dismissed summarily
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: If an appeal is not dismissed summarily, the appellate court shall call for the record of the case, notify the parties and hear the appeal in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the next steps when an appeal is accepted for detailed consideration.
examples: The appellate court obtains the trial record and schedules arguments from both sides.

faqs.question: What happens after an appeal is admitted?
faqs.answer: The court obtains the record and hears the parties before deciding the appeal.

title: Powers of the appellate court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: After hearing an appeal, the appellate court may dismiss the appeal, reverse or alter the finding, reduce or enhance the sentence, order a retrial, acquit the accused, or pass any other order authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section gives broad powers to appellate courts to correct errors made by lower courts.
punishment: Sentence may be confirmed, modified, reduced or enhanced as permitted by law.
examples: The appellate court reduces a sentence after finding it excessive.

faqs.question: What can an appellate court do in a criminal appeal?
faqs.answer: It may confirm, modify, reverse or set aside the lower court's decision.

title: Judgments of subordinate appellate court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: The rules relating to judgments of criminal courts shall, so far as applicable, apply to judgments delivered by subordinate appellate courts.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that appellate judgments follow the same standards of clarity and reasoning as trial court judgments.
examples: An appellate court records its reasons for modifying a conviction.

faqs.question: Must appellate courts provide reasons for their decisions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Appellate judgments must generally comply with applicable judgment requirements.

title: Order of High Court on appeal to be certified to lower court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Whenever the High Court decides an appeal, it shall certify its judgment or order to the court from which the appeal originated, and that court shall take action in accordance with the decision.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that lower courts receive and implement decisions made by the High Court in appeals.
examples: After reducing a sentence on appeal, the High Court sends its order to the trial court for implementation.

faqs.question: How does a lower court learn about the outcome of an appeal?
faqs.answer: The High Court formally certifies its decision to the lower court.

title: Suspension of sentence pending the appeal; release of appellant on bail
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Pending an appeal, the appellate court may suspend the execution of the sentence or order appealed against and, if the appellant is in confinement, may release him on bail or on his own bond in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to pause the sentence and grant bail while an appeal is being decided.
examples: A convicted person is released on bail while the High Court hears the appeal.

faqs.question: Can a convicted person get bail after filing an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes. The appellate court may suspend the sentence and grant bail.

title: Arrest of accused in appeal from acquittal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: When an appeal against acquittal is filed, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or before a subordinate court, and may admit the accused to bail pending disposal of the appeal.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to secure the presence of a person whose acquittal is being challenged.
examples: The High Court orders the arrest of an acquitted person after admitting an appeal against acquittal.

faqs.question: Can an acquitted person be arrested during an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court may order arrest and then consider bail.

title: Appellate court may take further evidence or direct it to be taken
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: If the appellate court considers additional evidence necessary, it may take such evidence itself or direct a subordinate court to record and submit the evidence.
simpleExplanation: This section permits additional evidence to be considered during an appeal when necessary for justice.
examples: The appellate court permits production of a crucial document that was unavailable during the trial.

faqs.question: Can new evidence be introduced during an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Additional evidence may be permitted when necessary for a just decision.

title: Procedure where Judges of Court of Appeal are equally divided
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: When Judges of an appellate court are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be decided according to the procedure prescribed by law, including reference to another Judge where required.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a method for resolving deadlocks among appellate judges.
examples: Two Judges hearing an appeal disagree, so the matter is referred to another Judge.

faqs.question: What happens if appellate judges disagree?
faqs.answer: The matter is resolved through the statutory procedure for differences of opinion.

title: Finality of judgments and orders on appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Judgments and orders passed by an appellate court shall be final, except where further appeal, revision, review or other remedy is expressly provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section states that appellate decisions are generally final unless another legal remedy is specifically available.
examples: After the High Court decides an appeal, the matter ends unless a further appeal to the Supreme Court is permitted.

faqs.question: Are appellate court decisions always final?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, unless the law provides another remedy.

title: Abatement of appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Every appeal under this Chapter shall finally abate on the death of the accused or appellant, except in cases and circumstances specifically provided by law.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when criminal appeals come to an end because the appellant dies.
examples: A criminal appeal against conviction generally ends upon the death of the convicted person.

faqs.question: What happens to a criminal appeal if the appellant dies?
faqs.answer: The appeal usually abates, subject to certain legal exceptions.

title: Reference to High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: If a subordinate criminal court is of the opinion that a question regarding the validity of any law or an important legal issue should be decided by the High Court, it may state the case and refer the question to the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section allows lower courts to seek guidance from the High Court on important legal questions.
examples: A Magistrate refers a constitutional question regarding a statute to the High Court.

faqs.question: Can a lower criminal court seek legal guidance from the High Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Important legal questions may be referred to the High Court.

title: Disposal of case according to decision of High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: After receiving the High Court's decision on a reference, the referring court shall dispose of the case in conformity with that decision.
simpleExplanation: This section requires lower courts to follow the legal ruling given by the High Court on a referred issue.
examples: After receiving the High Court's interpretation of a law, the Magistrate completes the trial accordingly.

faqs.question: Must a lower court follow the High Court's decision on a reference?
faqs.answer: Yes. The case must be decided in accordance with the High Court's ruling.

title: Calling for records to exercise powers of revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: The High Court or Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before an inferior criminal court for the purpose of satisfying itself as to the correctness, legality or propriety of any finding, sentence or order.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers higher courts to review records of lower courts through revision proceedings.
examples: The Sessions Judge calls for records to examine whether a Magistrate's order was legally correct.

faqs.question: What is a criminal revision?
faqs.answer: It is a supervisory review by a higher court of the legality or correctness of lower court proceedings.

title: Power to order inquiry
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: On examining the record of a case under revision, the High Court or Sessions Judge may direct a subordinate Magistrate to make further inquiry into a complaint that has been dismissed or into a case where an accused has been discharged.
simpleExplanation: This section allows higher courts to order a fresh or further inquiry when they believe a complaint was improperly dismissed or an accused was wrongly discharged.
examples: A Sessions Judge orders further inquiry after finding that a complaint was dismissed prematurely.

faqs.question: Can a higher court order a fresh inquiry into a dismissed complaint?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court or Sessions Judge may direct further inquiry when justified.

title: Sessions Judge's powers of revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: In revision proceedings, a Sessions Judge may exercise all or any of the powers that may be exercised by the High Court under the revision provisions, subject to the limitations prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section grants significant revision powers to Sessions Judges over subordinate criminal courts.
examples: A Sessions Judge modifies an improper order passed by a Magistrate during revision proceedings.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge hear revision matters?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sessions Judges have extensive revision powers under the Code.

title: Power of Additional Sessions Judge
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: An Additional Sessions Judge may exercise revision powers in respect of cases made over to him by the Sessions Judge in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section authorizes Additional Sessions Judges to handle revision matters assigned to them.
examples: A revision petition is transferred by the Sessions Judge to an Additional Sessions Judge for disposal.

faqs.question: Can an Additional Sessions Judge hear revision cases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cases assigned by the Sessions Judge may be decided by an Additional Sessions Judge.

title: High Court's powers of revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: In revision proceedings, the High Court may exercise any of the powers conferred on a court of appeal and may pass such orders as are necessary to secure the ends of justice, subject to statutory limitations.
simpleExplanation: This section provides broad supervisory powers to the High Court in criminal revision matters.
examples: The High Court sets aside an unlawful order passed by a subordinate criminal court.

faqs.question: How extensive are the High Court's revision powers?
faqs.answer: The High Court may exercise wide powers similar to those of an appellate court.

title: Power of High Court to withdraw or transfer revision cases
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: The High Court may withdraw any revision case pending before a Sessions Judge or transfer such case from one Sessions Judge to another whenever it considers such action necessary.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the High Court to control the allocation and transfer of revision proceedings.
examples: The High Court transfers a revision petition to another Sessions Division to ensure fairness.

faqs.question: Can the High Court transfer a revision case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court may withdraw or transfer revision matters when necessary.

title: Option of Court to hear parties
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: In revision proceedings, the Court may, if it thinks fit, hear the parties either personally or through their advocates before making any order.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the revisional court discretion to hear the parties before deciding a revision petition.
examples: The High Court hears arguments from both sides before deciding a revision application.

faqs.question: Must parties always be heard in revision proceedings?
faqs.answer: Not always. The Court may decide whether a hearing is necessary, subject to legal requirements.

title: Statement by Metropolitan Magistrate of grounds of his decision to be considered by High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: When a Metropolitan Magistrate submits a case for revision or reference, the statement of grounds recorded by the Magistrate shall be considered by the High Court while deciding the matter.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that the High Court takes into account the reasoning provided by the Metropolitan Magistrate.
examples: The High Court reviews the Magistrate's written reasons before deciding the revision petition.

faqs.question: Does the High Court consider the Magistrate's explanation during revision?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate's recorded grounds form part of the materials considered.

title: High Court's order to be certified to lower court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: The High Court shall certify its order in revision or reference proceedings to the subordinate court concerned, which shall take action in accordance with that order.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures implementation of the High Court's decisions in revision and reference matters.
examples: The High Court sets aside a Magistrate's order and sends the revised decision back for compliance.

faqs.question: How are revision orders implemented?
faqs.answer: The High Court certifies the order to the lower court, which must act accordingly.

title: Power of Supreme Court to transfer cases and appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: Whenever it is expedient for the ends of justice, the Supreme Court may direct that any particular case or appeal be transferred from one High Court to another High Court or from a criminal court in one State to a criminal court in another State.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers the Supreme Court to transfer criminal cases across States to ensure justice and fairness.
examples: A high-profile criminal case is transferred to another State to ensure an impartial trial.

faqs.question: Can the Supreme Court transfer a criminal case to another State?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Supreme Court has authority to transfer cases when necessary for justice.

title: Power of High Court to transfer cases and appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: The High Court may transfer any case, appeal or proceeding from one criminal court subordinate to it to another criminal court whenever such transfer is necessary for the ends of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section gives High Courts authority to transfer criminal matters within their jurisdiction.
examples: The High Court transfers a case to another district court due to concerns about local influence.

faqs.question: Can a High Court transfer a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. A High Court may transfer cases between subordinate courts to ensure fairness and justice.

title: Power of Sessions Judge to transfer cases and appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: Whenever it appears expedient for the ends of justice, a Sessions Judge may transfer any particular case or appeal from one criminal court to another criminal court within his Sessions Division.
simpleExplanation: This section allows a Sessions Judge to move cases between courts within the same Sessions Division to ensure fair and efficient administration of justice.
examples: A Sessions Judge transfers a case to another Magistrate because of a potential conflict of interest.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge transfer a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cases and appeals can be transferred within the Sessions Division when justice requires it.

title: Withdrawal of cases and appeals by Sessions Judges
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A Sessions Judge may withdraw any case or appeal from an Assistant Sessions Judge or Chief Judicial Magistrate and either try or hear the matter himself or make it over to another competent court.
simpleExplanation: This section allows Sessions Judges to reassign or personally hear cases under their supervisory authority.
examples: A Sessions Judge withdraws an appeal from an Assistant Sessions Judge and decides it personally.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge take over a case from a subordinate court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Sessions Judge may withdraw and reassign or hear the matter.

title: Withdrawal of cases by Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from, or recall any case made over to, a Magistrate subordinate to him and may either try the case himself or refer it to another competent Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers Chief Judicial Magistrates to reallocate cases among Magistrates under their control.
examples: A Chief Judicial Magistrate transfers a case to another Magistrate due to workload considerations.

faqs.question: Can a Chief Judicial Magistrate reassign cases?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cases may be withdrawn and reassigned as permitted by law.

title: Making over or withdrawal of cases by Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: District Magistrates and Sub-Divisional Magistrates may make over proceedings to Executive Magistrates subordinate to them or withdraw such proceedings in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the allocation and withdrawal of proceedings handled by Executive Magistrates.
examples: A District Magistrate assigns preventive proceedings to an Executive Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can Executive Magistrate proceedings be reassigned?
faqs.answer: Yes. Senior Executive Magistrates may allocate or withdraw such proceedings.

title: Reasons to be recorded
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: Whenever a case or appeal is transferred, withdrawn or made over under this Chapter, the authority making the order shall record the reasons for doing so.
simpleExplanation: This section promotes transparency by requiring reasons to be documented whenever transfer powers are exercised.
examples: A transfer order records concerns regarding impartiality and convenience of witnesses.

faqs.question: Must reasons be given when a case is transferred?
faqs.answer: Yes. The authority must record reasons for the transfer or withdrawal.

title: Execution of order passed under section 106
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: An order requiring a person to execute a bond for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour under section 106 shall be carried out in accordance with the provisions governing such bonds and their enforcement.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with implementing court orders that require convicted persons to execute bonds for good behaviour or peace.
punishment: Consequences arise if the bond conditions are violated.
examples: A person convicted of a violent offence is ordered to execute a bond to maintain peace after release.

faqs.question: What happens after a court orders a bond under section 106?
faqs.answer: The order is enforced according to the procedures prescribed by law.

title: Execution of sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a sentence of imprisonment is passed, the Court shall issue a warrant directing that the convicted person be committed to prison and detained according to the sentence imposed.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the mechanism for sending a convicted person to prison after sentencing.
punishment: Imprisonment as imposed by the Court.
examples: After conviction, the Court issues a warrant committing the offender to prison.

faqs.question: How is a prison sentence carried out?
faqs.answer: The Court issues a warrant authorizing imprisonment.

title: Execution of sentence of imprisonment in another case
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where a person already undergoing imprisonment is sentenced in another case, the sentence shall be executed in accordance with the provisions governing concurrent or consecutive sentences.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how additional prison sentences are handled when a person is already in custody.
punishment: Imprisonment according to the Court's order and applicable law.
examples: An inmate receives a new sentence in another case while already serving a prison term.

faqs.question: What happens if a prisoner is convicted in another case?
faqs.answer: The new sentence is executed according to rules governing multiple sentences.

title: Postponement of capital sentence on pregnant woman
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall postpone the execution of the sentence and may commute the sentence to imprisonment for life in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects pregnant women by preventing execution of a death sentence during pregnancy.
punishment: Death sentence may be postponed or commuted.
examples: A pregnant woman sentenced to death receives protection under this provision.

faqs.question: Can a pregnant woman be executed?
faqs.answer: No. The law requires postponement and allows commutation of the sentence.

title: Execution of sentence of death passed by High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, the Court shall issue the necessary warrant directing execution in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the issuance of warrants for carrying out death sentences after judicial confirmation.
punishment: Execution of death sentence as authorized by law.
examples: The High Court issues the warrant after confirming a death sentence.

faqs.question: Who authorizes the execution of a confirmed death sentence?
faqs.answer: The High Court issues the required execution warrant.

title: Execution of sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where a sentence of imprisonment is to be executed, the Court shall issue the appropriate warrant and take steps necessary to ensure that the convicted person is committed to custody in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with carrying out imprisonment sentences through court-issued warrants and prison custody procedures.
punishment: Imprisonment as imposed by the Court.
examples: A convicted offender is taken into custody after the Court issues the imprisonment warrant.

faqs.question: How is a prison sentence implemented?
faqs.answer: The Court issues a warrant and the convicted person is committed to lawful custody.

title: Sentence on escaped convict when to take effect
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When an escaped convict is sentenced for another offence, the commencement of the new sentence shall be determined according to the provisions governing escaped prisoners and multiple sentences.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how sentences are calculated when a prisoner commits offences after escaping custody.
punishment: Imprisonment according to the applicable sentences.
examples: An escaped prisoner receives a new sentence after committing another offence.

faqs.question: How are sentences handled for escaped convicts?
faqs.answer: The law determines when the new sentence begins in relation to the earlier sentence.

title: Collection of fines
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Fines imposed by criminal courts may be recovered in accordance with law through warrants, attachment of property, or other authorized methods of recovery.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how courts collect fines imposed upon convicted persons.
punishment: Recovery of the fine and any consequences prescribed by law for non-payment.
examples: A court issues a warrant to recover an unpaid criminal fine.

faqs.question: How are criminal fines recovered?
faqs.answer: Through legal recovery procedures such as warrants and attachment of property.

title: Warrant for levy of fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When an offender fails to pay a fine, the Court may issue a warrant for attachment and sale of movable property or authorize recovery through other lawful means.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the legal mechanism for recovering unpaid fines.
punishment: Recovery proceedings and other lawful consequences for non-payment.
examples: The Court orders attachment of property after the convicted person refuses to pay the fine.

faqs.question: What happens if a fine is not paid?
faqs.answer: The Court may recover it through attachment and sale of property.

title: Effect of such warrant
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued for recovery of a fine remains effective and may be executed according to law until the amount due is recovered or the warrant is otherwise satisfied.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the legal effect and continuing validity of warrants issued for recovery of fines.
examples: Recovery proceedings continue until the unpaid fine has been fully recovered.

faqs.question: Does a recovery warrant expire immediately if the fine is unpaid?
faqs.answer: No. It remains effective until satisfied according to law.

title: Warrant for levy of fine issued by a Court in any territory to which this Code does not extend
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued for recovery of a fine by a court situated in a territory to which the Code does not extend may be executed in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law when transmitted to a competent authority.
simpleExplanation: This section enables enforcement of fine recovery warrants issued by certain courts outside the ordinary territorial operation of the Code.
punishment: Recovery of the fine as ordered by the Court.
examples: A recovery warrant from another jurisdiction is executed after being properly transmitted to the competent authority.

faqs.question: Can a fine recovery warrant from another territory be enforced?
faqs.answer: Yes. The warrant may be executed according to the procedure established by law.

title: Suspension of execution of sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The execution of a sentence of imprisonment may be suspended in circumstances permitted by law, subject to conditions and safeguards imposed by the competent authority or court.
simpleExplanation: This section allows postponement or temporary suspension of imprisonment in legally recognized situations.
examples: A prison sentence is temporarily suspended pending compliance with a lawful order.

faqs.question: Can a prison sentence be temporarily suspended?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suspension may be granted when authorized by law.

title: Who may suspend sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The authority competent to suspend execution of a sentence shall exercise such powers in accordance with the provisions of law and subject to prescribed conditions.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies and regulates the authorities empowered to suspend criminal sentences.
examples: A competent authority grants suspension of sentence under statutory powers.

faqs.question: Who can suspend a criminal sentence?
faqs.answer: Only authorities empowered by law may suspend execution of a sentence.

title: Sentence on escaped convict when to take effect
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The commencement and operation of sentences imposed upon escaped convicts shall be governed by the provisions applicable to escaped prisoners and multiple sentences.
simpleExplanation: This section addresses how sentences are calculated when an escaped convict is later convicted again.
punishment: Imprisonment according to the applicable sentences.
examples: An escaped convict receives an additional sentence that takes effect as determined by law.

faqs.question: How are sentences managed for escaped convicts?
faqs.answer: The law determines when and how the subsequent sentence takes effect.

title: Sentence on offender already sentenced for another offence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a person already undergoing a sentence of imprisonment is sentenced on a subsequent conviction, the later sentence shall ordinarily commence after expiration of the earlier sentence unless the Court directs that the sentences run concurrently.
simpleExplanation: This section governs whether multiple prison sentences run one after another or at the same time.
punishment: Imprisonment according to consecutive or concurrent sentencing orders.
examples: A Court orders two prison sentences to run concurrently instead of consecutively.

faqs.question: What is the difference between concurrent and consecutive sentences?
faqs.answer: Concurrent sentences run together, while consecutive sentences run one after the other.

title: Execution of sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where a sentence of imprisonment is to be executed, the Court shall issue the appropriate warrant and take steps necessary to ensure that the convicted person is committed to custody in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with carrying out imprisonment sentences through court-issued warrants and prison custody procedures.
punishment: Imprisonment as imposed by the Court.
examples: A convicted offender is taken into custody after the Court issues the imprisonment warrant.

faqs.question: How is a prison sentence implemented?
faqs.answer: The Court issues a warrant and the convicted person is committed to lawful custody.

title: Sentence on escaped convict when to take effect
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When an escaped convict is sentenced for another offence, the commencement of the new sentence shall be determined according to the provisions governing escaped prisoners and multiple sentences.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how sentences are calculated when a prisoner commits offences after escaping custody.
punishment: Imprisonment according to the applicable sentences.
examples: An escaped prisoner receives a new sentence after committing another offence.

faqs.question: How are sentences handled for escaped convicts?
faqs.answer: The law determines when the new sentence begins in relation to the earlier sentence.

title: Collection of fines
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Fines imposed by criminal courts may be recovered in accordance with law through warrants, attachment of property, or other authorized methods of recovery.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how courts collect fines imposed upon convicted persons.
punishment: Recovery of the fine and any consequences prescribed by law for non-payment.
examples: A court issues a warrant to recover an unpaid criminal fine.

faqs.question: How are criminal fines recovered?
faqs.answer: Through legal recovery procedures such as warrants and attachment of property.

title: Warrant for levy of fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When an offender fails to pay a fine, the Court may issue a warrant for attachment and sale of movable property or authorize recovery through other lawful means.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the legal mechanism for recovering unpaid fines.
punishment: Recovery proceedings and other lawful consequences for non-payment.
examples: The Court orders attachment of property after the convicted person refuses to pay the fine.

faqs.question: What happens if a fine is not paid?
faqs.answer: The Court may recover it through attachment and sale of property.

title: Effect of such warrant
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued for recovery of a fine remains effective and may be executed according to law until the amount due is recovered or the warrant is otherwise satisfied.
simpleExplanation: This section describes the legal effect and continuing validity of warrants issued for recovery of fines.
examples: Recovery proceedings continue until the unpaid fine has been fully recovered.

faqs.question: Does a recovery warrant expire immediately if the fine is unpaid?
faqs.answer: No. It remains effective until satisfied according to law.

title: Warrant for levy of fine issued by a Court in any territory to which this Code does not extend
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued for recovery of a fine by a court situated in a territory to which the Code does not extend may be executed in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law when transmitted to a competent authority.
simpleExplanation: This section enables enforcement of fine recovery warrants issued by certain courts outside the ordinary territorial operation of the Code.
punishment: Recovery of the fine as ordered by the Court.
examples: A recovery warrant from another jurisdiction is executed after being properly transmitted to the competent authority.

faqs.question: Can a fine recovery warrant from another territory be enforced?
faqs.answer: Yes. The warrant may be executed according to the procedure established by law.

title: Suspension of execution of sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The execution of a sentence of imprisonment may be suspended in circumstances permitted by law, subject to conditions and safeguards imposed by the competent authority or court.
simpleExplanation: This section allows postponement or temporary suspension of imprisonment in legally recognized situations.
examples: A prison sentence is temporarily suspended pending compliance with a lawful order.

faqs.question: Can a prison sentence be temporarily suspended?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suspension may be granted when authorized by law.

title: Who may suspend sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The authority competent to suspend execution of a sentence shall exercise such powers in accordance with the provisions of law and subject to prescribed conditions.
simpleExplanation: This section identifies and regulates the authorities empowered to suspend criminal sentences.
examples: A competent authority grants suspension of sentence under statutory powers.

faqs.question: Who can suspend a criminal sentence?
faqs.answer: Only authorities empowered by law may suspend execution of a sentence.

title: Sentence on escaped convict when to take effect
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The commencement and operation of sentences imposed upon escaped convicts shall be governed by the provisions applicable to escaped prisoners and multiple sentences.
simpleExplanation: This section addresses how sentences are calculated when an escaped convict is later convicted again.
punishment: Imprisonment according to the applicable sentences.
examples: An escaped convict receives an additional sentence that takes effect as determined by law.

faqs.question: How are sentences managed for escaped convicts?
faqs.answer: The law determines when and how the subsequent sentence takes effect.

title: Sentence on offender already sentenced for another offence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a person already undergoing a sentence of imprisonment is sentenced on a subsequent conviction, the later sentence shall ordinarily commence after expiration of the earlier sentence unless the Court directs that the sentences run concurrently.
simpleExplanation: This section governs whether multiple prison sentences run one after another or at the same time.
punishment: Imprisonment according to consecutive or concurrent sentencing orders.
examples: A Court orders two prison sentences to run concurrently instead of consecutively.

faqs.question: What is the difference between concurrent and consecutive sentences?
faqs.answer: Concurrent sentences run together, while consecutive sentences run one after the other.

title: Period of detention undergone by accused to be set off against sentence of imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The period of detention undergone by an accused person during investigation, inquiry or trial of the same case shall be set off against the term of imprisonment imposed on conviction.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that time already spent in jail before conviction is deducted from the final prison sentence.
examples: An accused who spent six months in judicial custody before conviction receives credit for that period against the sentence imposed.

faqs.question: Is pre-trial detention counted toward a prison sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The detention period is generally deducted from the sentence imposed.

title: Saving
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The provisions relating to execution of sentences shall operate subject to any special provisions of law applicable to particular classes of offenders or punishments.
simpleExplanation: This section preserves the effect of special laws that may override or supplement general sentence execution provisions.
examples: A special statute governing imprisonment is applied alongside the general provisions of the Code.

faqs.question: Do special laws override general sentence execution rules?
faqs.answer: Yes. Special legal provisions continue to apply where relevant.

title: Return of warrant on execution of sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a warrant issued for execution of a sentence has been carried out, the executing authority shall return the warrant to the issuing court with an endorsement showing how it was executed.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures proper documentation after a sentence has been executed.
examples: A prison authority returns the warrant to the court after the convicted person has been admitted to prison.

faqs.question: What happens to a warrant after a sentence is executed?
faqs.answer: The warrant is returned to the court with details of its execution.

title: Money ordered to be paid recoverable as a fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Any money, other than a fine, payable under an order made under this Code may be recovered in the same manner as if it were a fine.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to use fine recovery procedures to collect other monetary amounts ordered under the Code.
punishment: Recovery proceedings as applicable to fines.
examples: Compensation ordered by the court is recovered through the same process used for unpaid fines.

faqs.question: Can compensation ordered by a court be recovered like a fine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Monetary orders may generally be enforced using fine recovery mechanisms.

title: Power to suspend or remit sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The appropriate Government may suspend the execution of a sentence or remit the whole or any part of the punishment imposed upon a convicted person, subject to conditions prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the Government authority to reduce, suspend or remit criminal sentences in appropriate cases.
punishment: Sentence may be suspended or reduced.
examples: A State Government grants remission reducing the remaining period of imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can the Government reduce a prison sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sentences may be suspended or remitted under statutory powers.

title: Power to commute sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The appropriate Government may, without the consent of the convicted person, commute a sentence of death, imprisonment for life, rigorous imprisonment or simple imprisonment into any lesser punishment authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Government to replace a harsher sentence with a lesser one.
punishment: Sentence may be reduced to a lesser lawful punishment.
examples: A death sentence is commuted to life imprisonment by the Government.

faqs.question: What is commutation of sentence?
faqs.answer: It means replacing a punishment with a less severe lawful punishment.

title: Restriction on powers of remission or commutation in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where a sentence of life imprisonment is imposed for an offence punishable with death, or where a death sentence has been commuted to life imprisonment, the offender shall not be released from prison unless at least fourteen years of imprisonment have been served.
simpleExplanation: This section limits the Government's power to release certain life convicts before they complete a minimum period of imprisonment.
punishment: Mandatory minimum imprisonment of fourteen years in specified cases.
examples: A person whose death sentence was commuted to life imprisonment must ordinarily serve at least fourteen years.

faqs.question: Can all life prisoners be released early?
faqs.answer: No. Certain categories of life convicts must serve a minimum period of imprisonment.

title: Concurrent power of Central Government in case of death sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The powers conferred upon the State Government regarding suspension, remission or commutation of a death sentence may also be exercised by the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: This section gives both the Central Government and State Government authority in matters involving death sentences.
examples: The Central Government considers a petition seeking commutation of a death sentence.

faqs.question: Can the Central Government commute a death sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. It shares this authority with the State Government in appropriate cases.

title: State Government to act after consultation with Central Government in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: In specified categories of cases involving Central Government interests, the State Government shall exercise powers of remission, suspension or commutation only after consultation with the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: This section requires cooperation between State and Central Governments in certain sensitive criminal matters.
examples: A State Government consults the Central Government before considering remission in a case investigated by a central agency.

faqs.question: When must a State Government consult the Central Government?
faqs.answer: In cases specified by law where Central Government interests are involved.

title: In what cases bail to be taken
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When a person accused of a bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant and is prepared to give bail, such person shall be released on bail in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section grants a right to bail in bailable offences.
examples: A person arrested for a bailable offence is released after furnishing the required bail bond.

faqs.question: Is bail a right in bailable offences?
faqs.answer: Yes. A person accused of a bailable offence is generally entitled to bail.

title: When bail may be taken in case of non-bailable offence
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: A person accused of a non-bailable offence may be released on bail by a court other than the High Court or Court of Session, subject to restrictions and considerations prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the grant of bail in non-bailable offences.
examples: A Magistrate grants bail in a non-bailable offence after considering the circumstances of the case.

faqs.question: Can bail be granted in non-bailable offences?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bail may be granted depending on the facts and legal conditions of the case.

title: Direction for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Where any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction that, in the event of such arrest, he shall be released on bail.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for anticipatory bail, allowing a person to seek protection from arrest before being taken into custody.
examples: A person fearing arrest in a criminal case applies for anticipatory bail before the Sessions Court.

faqs.question: What is anticipatory bail?
faqs.answer: It is a pre-arrest legal protection directing that a person be released on bail if arrested.

title: Special powers of High Court or Court of Session regarding bail
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: The High Court or Court of Session may direct that any person accused of an offence and in custody be released on bail, and may impose conditions as considered necessary in the interests of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section grants broader bail powers to the High Court and Sessions Court than those available to Magistrates.
examples: The High Court grants bail in a serious criminal case after imposing conditions on the accused.

faqs.question: Who has special powers to grant bail in serious cases?
faqs.answer: The High Court and the Court of Session possess special bail powers.

title: Amount of bond and reduction thereof
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: The amount of every bond executed under this Code shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive. Higher courts may reduce the amount where appropriate.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that bail bond amounts remain reasonable and proportionate.
examples: A court reduces an excessively high bail bond amount imposed by a lower court.

faqs.question: Can a bail amount be challenged as excessive?
faqs.answer: Yes. Higher courts may reduce excessive bond amounts.

title: Bond of accused and sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Before release on bail, the accused and any required sureties shall execute a bond undertaking appearance before the court and compliance with the conditions imposed.
simpleExplanation: This section requires formal bail bonds from accused persons and their sureties.
punishment: Forfeiture and other legal consequences may follow breach of bond conditions.
examples: An accused signs a bail bond and provides two sureties before being released.

faqs.question: What is a bail bond?
faqs.answer: It is a legal undertaking to appear before the court and comply with bail conditions.

title: Discharge from custody
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: As soon as the bail bond is executed and accepted, the person detained shall be released from custody unless required to be detained in connection with some other matter.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the immediate release of a person once bail requirements are fulfilled.
examples: After furnishing bail and sureties, the accused is released from judicial custody.

faqs.question: When is a person released after bail is granted?
faqs.answer: Release follows once the bond and surety requirements are properly completed.

title: Power to order sufficient bail when that first taken is insufficient
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When a court considers that the sureties or bail previously accepted are insufficient, it may require the accused to furnish additional or fresh sureties and may commit the person to custody until such requirements are fulfilled.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to demand stronger or additional sureties if the original bail arrangements are found inadequate.
punishment: Custody may continue until sufficient bail is furnished.
examples: The court requires a new surety after discovering that the original surety lacks financial credibility.

faqs.question: Can a court ask for additional sureties after bail is granted?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may require fresh or additional sureties if the existing ones are inadequate.

title: Discharge of sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: A surety may apply to the court for discharge from the bond. Upon such application, the court may order the arrest of the accused and require fresh sureties before releasing the surety from liability.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a legal process for a surety who wishes to withdraw responsibility for an accused person.
punishment: The accused may be required to furnish new sureties.
examples: A surety requests discharge because he no longer wishes to guarantee the accused's appearance.

faqs.question: Can a surety withdraw from a bail bond?
faqs.answer: Yes. A surety may seek discharge through the court.

title: Deposit instead of recognizance
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Where a person is required to execute a bond, the court may permit the deposit of money or Government securities in place of executing such bond, subject to prescribed conditions.
simpleExplanation: This section allows cash deposits or securities to be provided instead of a traditional bail bond in appropriate cases.
punishment: Forfeiture may occur if conditions are violated.
examples: A court permits a cash deposit instead of requiring a surety bond.

faqs.question: Can money be deposited instead of providing a surety?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may allow a deposit in lieu of a bond.

title: Procedure when bond has been forfeited
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When a bond is forfeited due to breach of its conditions, the court may record the grounds of forfeiture and call upon the person bound by the bond to pay the penalty or show cause why it should not be recovered.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the procedure followed when bail bond conditions are violated.
punishment: Recovery of bond penalty and other lawful consequences.
examples: An accused fails to appear in court, resulting in forfeiture proceedings against the surety.

faqs.question: What happens when a bail bond is breached?
faqs.answer: The court may initiate forfeiture proceedings and recover the bond amount.

title: Procedure in case of insolvency or death of surety or when a bond is forfeited
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: If a surety becomes insolvent, dies, or a bond is forfeited, the court may require the accused or other person concerned to furnish fresh security and may commit the person to custody until compliance.
simpleExplanation: This section ensures that valid security remains in place when existing sureties become unavailable or ineffective.
punishment: Custody may continue until fresh security is provided.
examples: The court orders new sureties after the death of an existing surety.

faqs.question: What happens if a surety dies or becomes insolvent?
faqs.answer: The court may require fresh sureties or other security.

title: Cancellation of bond and bail bond
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Without prejudice to the provisions relating to forfeiture of bonds, where a bond or bail bond is forfeited for breach of a condition, the bond executed by the person and the sureties shall stand cancelled, and the person shall not be released on personal bond in that case except upon furnishing fresh bonds or sureties as directed by the court.
simpleExplanation: This section provides that once a bail bond is forfeited, the existing bond automatically stands cancelled and fresh security is generally required.
punishment: Cancellation of existing bond and requirement of fresh bail arrangements.
examples: An accused fails to appear in court, resulting in forfeiture and cancellation of the existing bail bond.

faqs.question: What happens after a bail bond is forfeited?
faqs.answer: The bond is cancelled and fresh bonds or sureties may be required.

title: Bond required from minor
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Where a person required to execute a bond is a minor, the court may accept a bond executed only by the surety or guardian on behalf of the minor in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides special rules for bail bonds involving minors.
examples: A guardian executes the required bond on behalf of a minor accused.

faqs.question: Can a minor personally execute a bail bond?
faqs.answer: The court may allow a guardian or surety to execute the bond on the minor's behalf.

title: Appeal from orders under section 446
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Any person aggrieved by an order passed under section 446 relating to forfeiture of a bond may file an appeal before the court authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a right to challenge bond forfeiture orders.
examples: A surety appeals against an order directing recovery of the bond amount.

faqs.question: Can a bond forfeiture order be appealed?
faqs.answer: Yes. An appeal may be filed before the competent appellate court.

title: Power to direct levy of amount due on certain recognizances
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: The High Court or Court of Session may direct recovery of amounts due under recognizances or bonds in accordance with the procedure prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers higher courts to recover unpaid amounts arising from bond obligations.
examples: A Sessions Court orders recovery of a forfeited recognizance amount.

faqs.question: Who can order recovery of amounts due under recognizances?
faqs.answer: The High Court or Court of Session may direct such recovery.

title: Order for custody and disposal of property pending trial in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When any property is produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Court may make such order as it thinks fit for the proper custody of the property pending the conclusion of the inquiry or trial, and may, if the property is subject to speedy or natural decay or if it is otherwise expedient, order it to be sold or otherwise disposed of.
simpleExplanation: This section empowers courts to safeguard and manage property that is involved in a criminal case while the proceedings are ongoing.
examples: A court orders the safe storage of seized jewellery until the criminal trial concludes.

faqs.question: Can a court order sale of seized property before trial ends?
faqs.answer: Yes. Property that is perishable or likely to deteriorate may be sold or otherwise disposed of.

title: Order for disposal of property at conclusion of trial
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When an inquiry or trial concludes, the Criminal Court may make orders regarding the disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery of property produced before it or in its custody.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to decide what should happen to property involved in a criminal case after the proceedings end.
examples: A stolen vehicle is returned to its rightful owner after the trial concludes.

faqs.question: What happens to property after a criminal case is decided?
faqs.answer: The court may return, destroy, confiscate or otherwise dispose of the property.

title: Payment to innocent purchaser of money found on accused
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Where a person has innocently purchased stolen property and the stolen property is restored to its rightful owner, the Court may order compensation to the purchaser from money found in possession of the convicted offender.
simpleExplanation: This section protects innocent buyers who unknowingly purchase stolen property.
examples: An innocent buyer of a stolen laptop receives compensation after the laptop is returned to its owner.

faqs.question: Can an innocent buyer of stolen property receive compensation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may award compensation from money recovered from the offender.

title: Appeal against orders under section 452 or section 453
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Any person aggrieved by an order made under section 452 or section 453 may appeal against that order before the court authorized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section provides a remedy against court orders concerning disposal of property or compensation to innocent purchasers.
examples: A claimant challenges a property disposal order before the appellate court.

faqs.question: Can a property disposal order be appealed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Affected persons may file an appeal.

title: Destruction of libellous and other matter
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Upon conviction for offences involving defamatory, obscene, counterfeit or otherwise unlawful materials, the Court may order such materials and related instruments to be destroyed or otherwise disposed of according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to destroy unlawful materials connected with criminal offences.
punishment: Destruction or lawful disposal of offending materials.
examples: Counterfeit printing plates are ordered to be destroyed after conviction.

faqs.question: Can a court order destruction of illegal materials?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may order destruction of materials connected with certain offences.

title: Power to restore possession of immovable property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When a person is convicted of an offence attended by criminal force, show of force or criminal intimidation and it appears that another person has been dispossessed of immovable property by such means, the Court may order restoration of possession to the dispossessed person.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to restore possession of land or buildings to persons unlawfully dispossessed through criminal acts.
examples: A landowner forcibly evicted by criminals is restored to possession after the offenders are convicted.

faqs.question: Can a criminal court restore possession of land?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may order restoration when dispossession occurred through criminal force or intimidation.

title: Procedure by police upon seizure of property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Whenever property is seized by the police and is not produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may make appropriate orders regarding custody, delivery or disposal of such property.
simpleExplanation: This section governs how seized property is handled when it is not formally produced before a court.
examples: Police recover stolen goods and report the seizure to a Magistrate for disposal orders.

faqs.question: Who decides what happens to property seized by police?
faqs.answer: A Magistrate may issue orders regarding custody, delivery or disposal.

title: Procedure where no claimant appears within six months
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: If no person establishes a claim to seized property within the prescribed period after public notice, the Magistrate may order its disposal in accordance with law.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with property that remains unclaimed after seizure.
examples: Unclaimed recovered property is disposed of after notice is issued and no claimant comes forward.

faqs.question: What happens to unclaimed seized property?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may order lawful disposal after the required notice period.

title: Power to sell perishable property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Where seized property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or where immediate sale is otherwise expedient, the Magistrate may order the property to be sold and the proceeds dealt with according to law.
simpleExplanation: This section permits sale of perishable or rapidly depreciating property before it loses value.
examples: Perishable agricultural produce seized during an investigation is sold before it spoils.

faqs.question: Can perishable property be sold before the case ends?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may order an early sale to preserve value.

title: Irregularities which do not vitiate proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: Certain acts performed by a Magistrate in good faith, though done erroneously without proper authority, shall not invalidate the proceedings if they fall within the categories specified by law.
simpleExplanation: This section protects proceedings from being invalidated due to certain minor jurisdictional mistakes made in good faith.
examples: A Magistrate mistakenly exercises a power in good faith, but the proceeding remains valid under this section.

faqs.question: Do all procedural mistakes invalidate criminal proceedings?
faqs.answer: No. Certain good-faith irregularities do not automatically invalidate proceedings.

title: Irregularities which vitiate proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: If a Magistrate not empowered by law performs certain specified acts, such as taking cognizance, issuing process, demanding security, trying an offender, or passing sentence without jurisdiction, the proceedings shall be void.
simpleExplanation: This section lists serious jurisdictional errors that make criminal proceedings invalid.
examples: A Magistrate without legal authority conducts a trial, rendering the proceedings void.

faqs.question: Which procedural errors make proceedings invalid?
faqs.answer: Serious jurisdictional defects specified by law can render proceedings void.

title: Proceedings in wrong place
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order of a Criminal Court shall be set aside merely because the inquiry, trial or proceeding took place in a wrong local area, unless it has occasioned a failure of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents criminal cases from being overturned solely due to territorial jurisdiction errors unless actual prejudice is shown.
examples: A conviction is not overturned merely because the case was tried in the wrong district, where no prejudice resulted.

faqs.question: Does a trial automatically become invalid if held in the wrong place?
faqs.answer: No. The error must have caused a failure of justice.

title: Non-compliance with provisions of section 164 or section 281
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: A confession or statement shall not become inadmissible solely because of non-compliance with procedural requirements under section 164 or section 281, if the Court is satisfied that it was made voluntarily and that no prejudice has been caused.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to accept confessions despite certain procedural defects if fairness and voluntariness are established.
examples: A confession remains admissible despite a technical recording defect when voluntariness is proven.

faqs.question: Can a confession still be valid if there was a procedural error?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the Court finds the confession voluntary and fair.

title: Effect of omission to frame, or absence of, or error in, charge
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order shall be deemed invalid merely because no charge was framed, or because of an error, omission or irregularity in the charge, unless a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents convictions from being overturned for technical defects in charges unless prejudice is shown.
examples: A conviction is upheld despite a minor error in the wording of the charge.

faqs.question: Does an error in a charge automatically invalidate a conviction?
faqs.answer: No. The error must have caused a failure of justice.

title: Finding or sentence when reversible by reason of error, omission or irregularity
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order shall be reversed or altered by a Court of appeal, confirmation or revision on account of any error, omission or irregularity in proceedings unless it has occasioned a failure of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section embodies the principle that technical mistakes alone should not invalidate criminal proceedings unless they cause real injustice.
examples: An appellate court refuses to overturn a conviction based solely on a harmless procedural mistake.

faqs.question: What is the test for reversing a conviction due to procedural errors?
faqs.answer: The Court must find that the error resulted in a failure of justice.

title: Defect or error not to make attachment unlawful
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: No attachment made under this Code shall be deemed unlawful, nor shall any person making the attachment be deemed a trespasser, merely because of a defect or error in the proceedings, provided the attachment was made in good faith and under color of lawful authority.
simpleExplanation: This section protects lawful attachments from being invalidated solely because of minor procedural defects.
examples: Property attached under a court order remains valid despite a clerical error in the attachment proceedings.

faqs.question: Does a minor procedural defect invalidate an attachment?
faqs.answer: No. Attachments made in good faith generally remain valid.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: For the purposes of this Chapter, expressions used shall have the meanings assigned to them by this Code and other applicable laws unless the context otherwise requires.
simpleExplanation: This section provides definitions relevant to the miscellaneous provisions of the Code.
examples: Terms used in limitation provisions are interpreted according to the definitions provided by law.

faqs.question: Why are definitions included in this Chapter?
faqs.answer: To ensure uniform interpretation of terms used in miscellaneous provisions.

title: Bar to taking cognizance after lapse of the period of limitation
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: Except as otherwise provided, no Court shall take cognizance of an offence after expiry of the prescribed period of limitation, which varies according to the maximum punishment provided for the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section sets time limits within which certain criminal prosecutions must begin.
examples: A complaint for a minor offence is rejected because it was filed after the limitation period expired.

faqs.question: What is the limitation period in criminal cases?
faqs.answer: It is the maximum period within which a court may take cognizance of certain offences.

title: Commencement of the period of limitation
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: The period of limitation shall ordinarily commence on the date of the offence, or on the date when the offence became known to the aggrieved person or police officer, or when the identity of the offender became known, as the case may be.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when the limitation period begins to run.
examples: The limitation period starts when a fraud is discovered rather than when it was secretly committed.

faqs.question: When does the limitation period start?
faqs.answer: It generally begins from the date of the offence or the date it becomes known.

title: Exclusion of time in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: In computing the period of limitation, certain periods may be excluded, including time spent prosecuting another proceeding in good faith, time required for obtaining sanction, or other periods recognized by law.
simpleExplanation: This section allows specific periods to be ignored when calculating limitation deadlines.
examples: Time spent obtaining mandatory government sanction is excluded while calculating limitation.

faqs.question: Can some periods be excluded from limitation calculations?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law excludes certain periods to ensure fairness.

title: Exclusion of date on which Court is closed
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: Where the period of limitation expires on a day when the Court is closed, the Court may take cognizance on the day it reopens, as if the limitation period had not expired.
simpleExplanation: This section protects litigants when the last day of a limitation period falls on a court holiday or closure day.
examples: A complaint due on a Sunday is filed on Monday when the court reopens.

faqs.question: What if the limitation period ends on a court holiday?
faqs.answer: The filing may be made on the next day the court is open.

title: Continuing offence
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: In the case of a continuing offence, a fresh period of limitation shall begin to run at every moment during which the offence continues.
simpleExplanation: This section provides that limitation periods continuously renew while a continuing offence remains ongoing.
examples: An unlawful construction that remains in violation may constitute a continuing offence.

faqs.question: What is a continuing offence?
faqs.answer: It is an offence that continues over a period of time rather than ending with a single act.

title: Extension of period of limitation in certain cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: A Court may take cognizance of an offence after expiry of the limitation period if it is satisfied that the delay has been properly explained or that it is necessary to do so in the interests of justice.
simpleExplanation: This section allows courts to condone delay and proceed with cases when justified.
examples: A complaint is accepted despite delay because the complainant was prevented by sufficient cause.

faqs.question: Can a court accept a case filed after the limitation period?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court may extend the limitation period in the interests of justice.

title: Trials before High Courts
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: The provisions of this Code shall apply, so far as may be, to criminal trials conducted by the High Court, subject to constitutional provisions and any special rules governing such proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies that criminal procedure rules generally apply to High Court trials unless special provisions provide otherwise.
examples: A criminal matter tried directly before a High Court follows the Code unless special rules apply.

faqs.question: Do criminal procedure rules apply to High Court trials?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Code generally applies unless modified by law or special rules.

title: Delivery to commanding officers of persons liable to be tried by Court-martial
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Miscellaneous
officialText: When a person subject to military, naval or air force law is accused of an offence for which he may be tried either by a Criminal Court or by a Court-martial, the Criminal Court shall act in accordance with the applicable law and rules governing jurisdiction and transfer.
simpleExplanation: This section governs cases involving armed forces personnel who may be tried by military courts instead of ordinary criminal courts.
punishment: Depends on the offence and the forum conducting the trial.
examples: An Army officer accused of an offence is handed over for consideration under Court-martial procedures.

faqs.question: Can military personnel be tried by Court-martial instead of a criminal court?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jurisdiction is determined according to military and criminal law provisions.
